My stack is deep enough compared to the raise size to justify calling for implied odds, hoping to hit a set on the flop. If I don't improve, I will likely check-fold to aggression on most boards.

Elon opened to 300 and both the button and the SB called — this is a multiway pot. I have A♥7♠ in the big blind. I’m going to call the additional 200 (300 total) rather than raise. Rationale: a small 3-bet here won’t reliably isolate and will bloat a multiway pot where A7o plays poorly; calling is cheap (200 into a 1000 pot, ~16.7% equity required) so I can see a flop with a decent chance to hit top pair. Stack dynamics matter too — Claude is short and may jam later, and Elon has a deep opening range, so I prefer to keep the pot manageable. Postflop plan: c-bet/value-bet if I pair the Ace, play cautiously on wet/coordinated boards (check-fold to heavy action if missed), and re-evaluate if a squeeze or shove occurs.

I am first to act post-flop in a four-way pot with pocket deuces. The flop of 6c 3c Jd does not improve my hand. Given that I am out of position against three opponents, including the pre-flop raiser (Elon), the most prudent play is to check and evaluate the action. I am looking for a cheap showdown or a reason to fold if the betting becomes aggressive, as my hand is very weak in a multi-way pot.

Check to control the pot and realize equity. This is a dry J♣? (J♦) 6♣ 3♣ board and I hold A♥7♠ — just Ace-high with no club draw. It’s a four-way pot after an UTG open, so a flop c-bet won’t fold out enough hands and would bloat a pot where my hand has little showdown value. I’ll check and see a turn; I’m prepared to call small turn bets to try to pick up an Ace or showdown, but I’ll fold to large aggression or a shove from Claude/Elon unless I improve. If checked to on the turn, I may take a free card or consider a delayed, targeted bluff on safe runouts.

Multiway pot pre-flop after my UTG raise with A4s, which has decent playability but completely misses the 6c-3c-Jd flop: no pair, no flush draw (spades not on board), no straight draw beyond runner-runner longshots, ace-high at best. Flash and Minni checked, indicating possible weakness or trapping, but board favors draws (club flush draw, gutshots) and hands like Jx, sets, or overpairs that might check back. C-betting into 4 players here risks bloating the pot OOP with no equity; better to pot control, check to induce bluffs or see turn cheaply. Folding isn't an option as no bet to call. My deep stack (102BB) allows patience; Claude's short stack may shove if I bet. Prioritize stack preservation in tournament early/mid stages.

Good spot to take down the pot. Everyone has checked to me on the button with a weak-looking flop (6c 3c Jd). My A8o is ace-high currently, but I have multiple draws: club flush draw (8c with 6c-3c on board), overcard ace, and potential pair outs. More importantly, this is a favorable situation to take down the pot uncontested. With a short stack of 2325, I need to play aggressively in good spots. A 600 bet (half the pot) looks strong and credible without overcommitting my stack. If called, I still have 1725 chips and genuine outs. If the hand gets to showdown and everyone is folding weak hands, that's a profitable outcome for my chip position.

I have pocket deuces on a Jack-high flop in a four-way pot. I missed my set, which was my primary reason for calling pre-flop. Claude has bet 600 from the button after everyone checked to him. While he could be taking a stab at the pot, I am out of position with two other players (Minni and Elon) still left to act behind me. My hand is extremely vulnerable to any overcards or better pairs, and calling 600 with the worst possible pair in this situation is not a profitable long-term strategy. I will fold and wait for a better spot to commit chips.

Call 600. Claude’s 600 into a 1800 pot is a small c-bet (~25% to call) so I can see another street cheaply. I hold A♥7♠ — only Ace-high with no club draw, but I block some Ax combinations and have about 6 outs to pair by the river (~24% to improve), so this call is borderline but justified given the low cost and my deep stack. Plan: call to realize equity and re-evaluate on the turn; fold to significant raise or shove from Claude/Elon unless I improve to a pair or get a clear read. If the turn is safe and action is checked to me heads-up I may consider a delayed, targeted bluff, otherwise proceed cautiously.

Facing a 600 bet from Claude (short stack on button, 1725 total) after my check, with Minni (deep) calling behind. My A♠4♠ is ace-high with no pair, no club draw, no straight draw—pure bluff catcher at best, but checked UTG range looks weak now. Pot odds ~4:1 (600 into 2400) require ~20% equity, but vs. Claude's polarized range (Jx, sets, clubs flush draw, bluffs) and Minni's calling range (likely Jx/draws), my equity is marginal <20%. Raising risks Minni's deep stack and isolates vs. short shove; calling bloats pot OOP multiway with poor playability on club-heavy board. Tournament stage favors folding marginal ace-high to aggression, preserving 102BB stack for premium spots. History shows Claude aggressive post-flop, Minni sticky caller—exploit by waiting.

Check. I hold A♥7♠ on 6♣–3♣–J♦–8♥ and I’m heads‑up with Claude, who is very short (≈1,725) and opened the action on the flop with a small c‑bet I called. The turn didn’t improve me – I only have six nominal outs (three Aces, three 7s) for the river, ≈6/46 ≈ 13% to improve. If I bet now Claude is likely to shove his short stack rather than fold; a shove of ~1,725 into the current 2,400 pot would force me to call ~1,725 to win ~4,125 (need ≈42% equity) — far more than my equity vs a value‑heavy shove. Betting here therefore either folds out marginal hands I can’t beat or prices me into a jam where I’m a big dog. Checking keeps the pot controlled, lets me realize my equity, and preserves the option to fold to large aggression or to call a small turn bet. Plan: check the turn, fold to a committed shove, call only small turn bets and re‑evaluate on the river for a delayed bluff or a value bet if I pair or pick up better information.

Betting 1200 on the turn with a pair of 8s after Minni checked. This is a strong position - I've improved to a pair, Minni showed weakness with a check, and I need to build chips with my short stack. The bet size is roughly 50% of the pot, making it a credible aggression that forces Minni to make difficult decisions with marginal holdings. If Minni calls, I still have 525 chips left for the river and showdown value. If Minni folds, I win a meaningful pot. This aggressive line is optimal given my tournament situation and current hand strength.

Fold. Claude’s turn bet to 1,200 into a 3,600 pot means I must call 1,200 to win 4,800 (need ≈25% equity). I hold A♥7♠ with no club draw — only six outs (≈13% to pair by the river), well below the pot‑odds threshold. Claude led the flop and barreled the turn, a line consistent with value (Jx, a 6/3, sets, or a club semi‑bluff) and with his short effective stack he’s incentivized to apply pressure. Raising here would be spewy with a weak Ace and just risks getting jammed or committed out of position. Calling to “see” the river isn’t justified by my equity or implied odds. I’ll fold and preserve my deep stack for better spots.

I hold pocket aces (Ad-Ac), the strongest starting hand in poker. In UTG position with a moderate stack (4,125 chips, ~41 BB), a standard aggressive raise is appropriate. A 3.5x raise to 350 total chips builds the pot to get value for my premium hand while still giving opponents the option to call and keeping enough stack depth to play post-flop. This is much stronger than a min-raise and allows me to extract value from weaker holdings while maintaining flexibility. Pocket aces should be played for value here, not slowplayed.

I am on the button with Jack-Nine offsuit. Claude has opened with a raise to 3.5x from under-the-gun. While the button is a good position to play many hands, J9o is too weak to call a raise from early position, especially against a player who has shown they can play aggressively with a relatively short stack. Folding is the most disciplined play to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

Claude opened to 350 from UTG in a short-handed pot — that sizing and seat suggest a fairly strong, not easily dominated range. K6o is a marginal, easily dominated hand with poor postflop playability OOP. Calling 300 to play out of position (and risk a 3-way pot if the BB comes in or a squeeze 3-bet) requires equity I don’t have vs an UTG open. A light 3-bet bluff is possible but thin and unnecessary with my stack; preserving chips for better spots is preferable. Fold.

Js3c is a complete trash hand with no connectivity, no suitedness, and dominated by any reasonable UTG raising range from Claude. Positionally, I'm out of position in the BB facing an early position raise. Pot odds to call 250 into 500 are about 2.4:1, but equity against Claude's likely strong range (pairs, broadway, etc.) is poor (<30%), making it a losing play long-term. Folding preserves stack for better spots in this deep-stack tournament phase (100BB effective). No history this hand suggests bluff-catching value.

King-Five offsuit is a weak hand to open from under-the-gun, even in a four-handed game. While a King has some high-card value, the five kicker is very weak and easily dominated by any other King that might call. With a stack of 5075 (approximately 50 big blinds), there is no need to take unnecessary risks with marginal holdings. I will wait for a better spot to accumulate chips.

A♠T♣ on the button is a clear open-raise candidate — strong broadway potential and the button’s positional advantage. The pot is 150 and it’s 100 to call; I want to take the initiative, isolate the blinds, and pick up fold equity. A raise to 300 (3x BB) is a standard, table-consistent sizing that pressures Claude (BB, ~41bb) and Elon (SB, deep/aggressive) while still allowing me to play postflop in position if called. Plan: if called I’ll c-bet favorable flops and proceed cautiously on ace-high or heavily coordinated boards; if faced with a large 3-bet/shove I’ll re-evaluate and likely fold given ATo’s vulnerability vs very strong ranges. This sizing maximizes expected value from the button while managing risk against aggressive 3-betting players.

68o is a weak offsuit hand with poor playability, low equity against a button raise from Minni who has been aggressive but often shows strength when continuing post-flop. Facing a 3x raise OOP in SB, calling commits chips poorly with multiway potential including BB Claude who may defend wide. Pot odds are marginal (250 to call into 450, ~36% equity needed but we have far less vs range), no good reason to raise for thin value or bluff. Folding preserves stack for better spots in high roller tournament to maximize long-term EV over 150 hands.

4-7 is a very weak hand, even with the clubs suit providing some flush draw potential. I'm out of position against Minni, who is a competent player with a button raise. While I'm getting reasonable pot odds (450:200), the hand strength is simply too marginal to justify calling out of position against a likely stronger range. Minni's button raise range is ahead of 47s significantly. My stack (4175) is healthy enough that I don't need to play marginal hands. This is a straightforward fold to preserve chips for better spots where I have hand strength and/or position advantage.

I’m UTG with 4♥6♥ — a marginal, low suited connector out of position against three players. Pot is 150 and it costs 100 to see the flop (min-raise to 200). Limping/calling 100 OOP would give poor pot odds (you need ~40% equity to break even) and likely produces a multiway pot where this hand performs poorly. Raising to seize initiative is an option, but with deep stacks and active players behind (Elon/Claude/Flash frequently call/3-bet), I don’t want to face a 3-bet or play postflop OOP with 4‑6s. Prefer to conserve chips and wait for a better spot — fold.

On the button with Qd5d, a suited queen-high hand with some potential for straights and flushes, in an unopened pot after UTG fold. Excellent position. SB and BB stacks are around 4k each, mine deep at 10k. Raising steals the dead money (150 pot) frequently, isolates weaker ranges, and maintains initiative. Standard button steal sizing: 3x BB to 300. Fold equity high given history of frequent preflop folds. Calling risks multiway pot OOP; folding too tight.

I'm holding 2s9c (deuce-nine offsuit) from the small blind facing a button raise from Elon. This is a very weak hand with poor equity against a typical button raising range. Given my stack size of 4125 chips (~41 big blinds) and the need to preserve chips in this 150-hand tournament, folding is the correct decision. I lose the 50 small blind already posted, but avoid committing more chips to a hand that will be dominated postflop out of position. The risk-reward doesn't justify defending with this weak holding.

I am in the Big Blind with 8h3c, which is a very weak hand. Elon raised from the Button to 300. While the price to call is only 200 more into a pot of 450, 83o plays extremely poorly post-flop, especially out of position against a button open. Given my stack size and the strength of my hand, there is no justification for defending or 3-betting here. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

UTG position with 10-6 suited, a speculative hand with poor playability multi-way. Preflop pot is small (150), but acting first out of position against three opponents, including deep-stacked Minni in BB. Standard tight play dictates folding low suited connectors UTG to avoid difficult postflop spots without initiative. Preserve stack for premium hands to maximize long-term EV over 150 hands.

I'm on the button with K8o - a marginally playable hand in this position. My stack is relatively short at 4125 (about 40BB), and I need to pick my spots to accumulate chips. Rather than calling the 100 into a multiway pot where K8o will be weak postflop, I should raise to apply pressure and potentially win the blinds preflop. Flash (SB) has a similarly short stack at 4925, and Minni (BB) has a deep stack but may fold to positional pressure. A raise to 280 gives me multiple paths to win: fold equity if both opponents fold, or a heads-up pot where my button position provides a significant advantage. This is better EV than calling or folding given my position and stack dynamics.

I am holding 9c 3s in the Small Blind. This is a very weak hand with poor playability, especially when I will be out of position for the remainder of the hand against the Button. Claude's raise to 2.8x from the Button is standard, but my hand is well below the range required to defend. Folding is the most profitable long-term play here.

Facing a button open to 280 while I’m in the big blind with 8♥5♠ (to call 180 into a 430 pot). Pot odds require ~29.5% equity to justify a call (180/(430+180)), and 8‑5 offsuit has considerably less equity vs a standard button opening range and plays very poorly out of position. Claude has shown frequent aggression and can apply pressure postflop; I don’t want to get into marginal OOP decisions with a dominated, low-equity hand. Preserve stack and wait for a better spot or position — fold here.

I hold Ah-Jd in UTG (early position) with 4,275 chips. AJ off-suit is a premium hand that merits an aggressive opening. From the hand history, I've been folding weak hands and playing quality holdings. Given my stack size and position, I should raise to define my hand and build the pot with a strong hand. A standard 3x BB raise to 300 chips is appropriate here - it's forceful enough to command respect from the table while being proportional to my stack. This raise allows me to play a pot in position post-flop if called, and may win immediately.

I am on the button with King-Trey suited. Claude has opened for 300 from under the gun. While the hand has some potential, a 3x raise from UTG usually indicates a range that dominates K3s. Additionally, with two active players in the blinds (Minni and Elon) who both have larger stacks than me, calling risks getting squeezed. Fold is the disciplined play here to preserve my stack.

I'm in the small blind with 6♦4♥ — a weak, offsuit, non‑connected hand with poor playability. Claude opened to 300 from UTG, so his range is relatively strong; I'd be out of position to him (and possibly the BB) for the rest of the hand. The pot is 450 and calling 250 gives ~36% break‑even odds, but even seeing a cheap flop I will often be dominated or have awkward hands to play postflop. A 3‑bet bluff to 500 is a bad look here — it risks a shove from Claude (shorter stack) or a call/3‑bet from the BB and puts me in a marginal spot out of position. I have a healthy stack and should conserve chips for better, playable holdings — fold.

Facing a UTG raise to 300 from Claude with J4s in the BB. J4s is a marginal suited hand but has poor playability out of position against a strong UTG opening range, which likely includes high pairs, broadways, and strong suited connectors. Pot odds are favorable (200 to call into 450), but the implied odds are limited due to reverse implied odds with a weak hand that rarely makes the nuts. My stack is deep enough (101 BB) to wait for better spots. Folding preserves stack for premium hands.

I am in the UTG position with 8c 2c. While suited, 82 suited is a very weak hand to open from early position, especially with a stack size of approximately 49 big blinds. Given the aggression shown by Minni and Elon in previous hands, opening such a weak hand would likely lead to difficult post-flop situations or being forced to fold to a 3-bet. I will fold and wait for a better spot to commit chips.

On the button with Js6h and only the blinds to act, I want to take the initiative and apply pressure from position. J6o is a marginal hand but playable in position — it has showdown value and can realize equity postflop. Limping (calling 100) surrenders fold equity and invites multi-way pots; a tiny min-raise to 200 is too weak and will invite too many callers. I’ll open to 300 (≈3x BB) to maximize fold equity, keep the pot manageable if called, and avoid dominated multi-way spots. If I face a sizable 3-bet I’ll fold and preserve my deep stack; if I get called I’ll use position to control the pot and look for favorable spots postflop.

Pocket fives is a strong hand preflop, well ahead of a button raising range which is likely wide after the UTG fold. Defending the blinds is important long-term, and calling OOP with set-mining potential is loose here with poor implied odds multiway if BB calls. 3-betting isolates the button, takes advantage of fold equity, and gives me initiative OOP with deep stacks. Sizing to 900 (3x the raise) is standard and balanced in my range. Folding is too tight given position dynamics and history of Minni's frequent button aggression.

I'm facing a 3-bet from Elon (900) after Minni opened to 300 from the button. I hold As4h - a weak hand that plays poorly against a strong 3-bet range. Based on my previous hands, Elon demonstrates selective aggression and likely has a strong range here (likely pairs, AK, AQ, etc.). My A4o has marginal equity at best against that distribution and plays poorly in position post-flop. With my stack at 4325 and needing to call 800, this isn't a profitable spot to get involved. Folding preserves chips to play stronger hands in better positions.

I opened to 300 on the button with Js6h to take the initiative, but Elon (SB) has 3-bet to 900. Facing a 900 3-bet I must call 600 into a 1,300 pot (~31.6% pot odds). J6o is a marginal, often dominated holding vs a typical SB 3-bet range (pairs, Axs, broadways and some bluffs) and its equity vs that range is likely below the required break-even. A light 4-bet bluff is too thin here — I don’t have the blockers or a read that Elon’s 3-bets are wide enough to make it profitable. I’ll preserve my deep stack and fold this marginal hand.

UTG in a 4-handed game with A♥4♣ — in short‑handed play A‑x is a reasonable open: it blocks stronger A‑x combos and has decent heads‑up equity. I want to take the initiative, avoid limp‑multiway pots with a weak kicker, and apply pressure to the shorter blinds. A standard 3xBB open (300 total) fits the table tendencies (frequent 300 opens) and isolates the button more often than a min‑raise would. Plan: c‑bet favorable flops (ace‑high or dry boards), play cautiously if called and fold to a large 3‑bet given the poor kicker; if everyone folds I pick up the blinds.

95o is a complete trash hand with no high card value, no suitedness, and poor connectivity. Facing an UTG raise from Minni, who often raises strong ranges from early position based on history. On button with deep stacks (10500 effective), but no reason to speculate pre-flop without position advantage against this range. Standard fold to preserve stack for better spots in long tournament.

AJ suited is a premium hand in the top 8% of starting hands. Minni's UTG raise of 300 suggests a strong range, but I have excellent holdings. From the small blind, I should play this hand aggressively. My call price is only 250 (1.8:1 odds), but raising takes initiative and builds the pot with a strong hand. A 3-bet to 800 is a standard raise size (~2.7x the original raise) that commits meaningful chips while leaving me room to maneuver. This accomplishes multiple objectives: (1) builds a pot in a favorable situation, (2) applies pressure to Minni and potentially wins the hand immediately, (3) narrows the field if Flash folds. With my 4,275 stack, committing 800 is reasonable given hand strength. The position is weak (SB) but the premium nature of AJ compensates for it.

I am in the big blind with Ten of Clubs and Deuce of Spades, a very weak hand. Minni raised from UTG and Claude 3-bet from the small blind. The action is heavy and my hand is near the bottom of my range. There is no reason to defend or contest this pot with such a weak holding against a raise and a re-raise. I will fold.

I opened UTG to 300 with A♥4♣ intending to take the initiative, but Claude’s quick 3‑bet to 800 changes the spot. To call is 500 more into a 1,200 pot (you need ~29% equity), and we’ll be out of position playing a weak‑kicker ace against a short‑stacked but aggressive opponent who 3‑bets a fairly strong/polarized range (AA/KK/QQ/AK/AQ and some bluffs). A4o is frequently dominated by his value hands and has poor postflop playability — 4‑betting as a bluff is suboptimal here and calling leaves us in a marginal, often losing spot with ~27BB effective. I’ll fold and preserve my deep stack for better spots rather than getting a thin price to continue with a dominated hand.

K6o is a weak offsuit hand with poor playability, dominated by better kings, no straight or flush potential suited. UTG in a 4-handed game, standard range is tight; folding avoids difficult post-flop spots OOP against aggressive opponents like Flash and Minni who frequently raise pre-flop. Deep stacks (105BB) allow waiting for better spots to maximize edge over 150 hands.

I'm on the button with 7-9 unsuited, facing a call of 100 to a pot of 150. While 7-9 is a marginal hand, several factors support a call here: (1) Excellent position on the button means I'll act last post-flop, giving me significant control; (2) Pot odds are favorable at 1.5:1 (150 to 100); (3) My hand has wheel potential and can make straights; (4) With my moderate stack (4,725), seeing flops selectively is reasonable. This is a standard button play - I can fold easily if the flop doesn't connect but have position advantage if it does. The risk is manageable given my chip position and the fold equity available post-flop with position.

I am in the Small Blind with 8d 7h. Claude (Button) has limped into the pot. The pot is currently 250 and it costs me only 50 more to see a flop. 8-7 offsuit is a speculative hand, but in a multi-way or limped pot with good pot odds (5 to 1), calling to see a flop is a reasonable play to try and hit a straight or two pair. I will call the 50 and see what the flop brings.

I’m in the big blind with Js9h and the action is two limp-calls (SB and Button). Pot is 300 and there’s no raise to face, so it’s free to see a flop. J9 offsuit is a marginal hand and loses value multiway and out of position against the button and SB. A small isolation raise (to 200) is too weak to get folds and a larger iso would bloat the pot with a mediocre hand. With deep stacks I can realize equity cheaply and look to play postflop only when I hit or get good odds, so I’ll check and see the flop. Plan: if I hit a pair or a strong draw continue; otherwise give up to significant aggression.

I have flopped middle pair (Eights) with a seven kicker in a limped pot. Since there are two other players in the hand and my hand is marginal, checking is the most prudent play. This allows me to control the size of the pot and see how Minni and Claude react to the board. If the action remains checked around, I can re-evaluate on the turn. If someone bets, I will decide based on the bet size and the player's tendencies.

Flop: 3♠ 8♥ J♣. I hold J♠9♥ — top pair with a medium kicker. Preflop both opponents limped so their ranges are wide (suited connectors, small pairs, broadway singletons, some Jx). This board is relatively dry but my hand is still vulnerable to sets (33/88) and the occasional overpair or two-card combo that limp-calls. Against wide limp-calling ranges I’m ahead of a lot of hands (worse Jx, 9x, many small pairs) and I want to charge any turn cards that could outdraw me while denying free cards. With two opponents I don’t want to bloat the pot but also shouldn’t check a value hand and give good odds to draws. I’ll make a medium value/protection bet — about half the pot (150) — to extract value from worse pairs and charge draws while keeping the pot manageable. If I face a sizable raise I’ll re-evaluate (likely fold to large aggression with this kicker; call small single raises and proceed cautiously on the turn).

With 7d-9s on a 3s-8h-Jc flop, I'm facing a 150 chip raise to a 450 chip pot. While my hand is currently weak, I have a strong open-ended straight draw with approximately 8 outs (four 6s make 6-7-8-9, four 10s make 7-8-9-10-J). Getting 3:1 pot odds (450 to 150) with two cards to come, my equity is around 31-32%, making this a call with reasonable expected value. Key factors supporting the call: (1) Position - I'm on the button and act last; (2) Straight draw equity - 8 outs is a legitimate draw; (3) Odds - 3:1 is adequate for a draw; (4) Stack efficiency - I have room to fold the turn if I miss and face another bet. Minni's flop raise from the big blind suggests a strong hand, but my draw has the potential to improve, and I control my risk by acting last on subsequent streets. If I miss the turn, I can fold without further investment, limiting downside.

I have middle pair (Eights) on a Jack-high board. Minni lead out for a half-pot bet from the big blind, and Claude called from the button. The pot odds are currently 5:1 (calling 150 to win a total pot that will be 750). While my kicker is weak, middle pair in a limped pot is worth a call at these odds to see if the turn improves my hand or if the action remains manageable. Raising would likely only be called by better hands like a Jack, and folding is too cautious given the price.

On the turn, I have improved to two pair (Eights and Sevens). This is a very strong hand in a limped pot. Since Minni was the aggressor on the flop, I will check to her again to see if she continues betting. This allows me to either check-call to keep her bluffs in or check-raise if I feel I can get value from a Jack. Leading out now might telegraph the strength of my hand too clearly, whereas checking maintains the same line I took on the flop.

Turn: 3♠ 8♥ J♣ 7♣ — I hold J♠9♥ (top pair, medium kicker). Both opponents limp-called preflop and then called my small flop bet, so their ranges are wide (worse Jx, 9x, 8x, small pairs, suited connectors, occasional 33/88). The 7 on the turn brings a few hands ahead of me (made 7x, some two-pair combos) and creates straight possibilities (notably 9‑10), and there are now two clubs on board making a river club potentially dangerous. I want to charge draws and extract value from worse pairs while keeping the pot controlled multiway, so I’ll continue with a medium protection/value bet — roughly half the pot (375). If I get a small raise I can call and re-evaluate on the river; if I face large or all‑in aggression I’ll likely fold this medium‑kicker top pair.

Calling 375 with pair of sevens on the turn. At 4:1 pot odds, I only need 20% equity to break even, and my pair of sevens likely has 25-30% equity against Minni's aggressive range. The turn card (7c) gave me a concrete hand to showdown, transforming my open-ended draw into a made hand with legitimate value. Minni's turn raise could indicate a strong hand, but her aggression also creates opportunities. With one card to come and favorable odds, calling preserves my equity and allows me to see the river. My remaining stack of 4,100 gives me flexibility—I can fold to a river bet if necessary. The pot odds mathematically justify this call even against a likely stronger hand.

On the turn, I have improved to two pair (Eights and Sevens) on a board of J-8-3-7. This is a very strong hand in a limped, multi-way pot. Minni led the flop and is continuing on the turn, while Claude has called both streets. This likely indicates that at least one of them has a Jack or a draw. I will raise here to extract value from Jx hands and to protect against the straight and flush draws that are present (T9, 65, and clubs). A call would be passive and would allow draws to see the river cheaply. By raising to 1125, I am putting pressure on weaker holdings and defining where I stand in the hand.

I have top pair (J♠9♥) with a medium kicker. Pot is 2625 and Flash’s check‑raise to 1125 makes my cost to call 750 (about 22% break‑even). Flash check‑called the flop and then check‑raised the turn, so his range is polarized toward strong value (sets: 33/88/77/JJ, two‑pair combos like J8/87, or made straights like 9‑10) plus some semi‑bluffs (two‑club combinations with a big draw). Claude is still in, making a pure bluff by Flash less likely and increasing the value of calling to realize equity against weaker Jx/9x and some draws. Folding here would give up too much equity; raising would bloat the pot with only a medium kicker and commit me unnecessarily. Calling 750 (total 1125 committed this street) is the right, low‑risk play to see the river and re‑evaluate: if I face further large aggression or a shove and the river doesn’t improve me, I will fold; if I improve to two pair/better or the river is a safe card and opponents check, I can extract value.

Folding a marginal pair of sevens on the turn facing a squeeze from two aggressive opponents. Flash's check-raise and Minni's continued aggression strongly indicate I'm beaten - likely facing an overpair, set, or straight. My hand cannot improve (no more 7s), giving me static equity of approximately 15-20% against their strong range. While pot odds offer 4.4:1, they don't justify calling with such weak equity. Preserving my 4,100-chip stack for higher-value situations is the mathematically superior play.

On the river, the board is 3s-8h-Jc-7c-5h. I have two pair, Eights and Sevens. This is a very strong hand in a pot that was limped pre-flop. My turn check-raise was called by Minni, which strongly suggests she has a Jack (top pair) or a draw that may have missed (like clubs or T9). A Jack is highly likely to call a value bet here. While a straight (6-4 or T9) is possible, 8-7 is ahead of the vast majority of her calling range. I will bet 1600, which is roughly half the pot, to extract maximum value from a Jack while remaining committed to the hand given the pot size and my remaining stack.

I have top pair (J♠) with a medium kicker (9♥). Flash’s line (limp‑call preflop, check‑call flop, then a sizable check‑raise on the turn) is polarized toward strong value (sets, two‑pair like J8/87, or the 9‑10 straight) but also can include some semi‑bluffs (club two‑card combos) and weaker Jx. The river bet is small (1,600 into a 4,975 pot) — calling 1,600 puts another 1,600 in and gives me a break‑even equity of only ~20% (pot becomes ~8,175). I block some of his most dangerous combos (I hold a 9 and a jack, which reduce 9‑10 straights and some Jx combos), and I’m getting very good odds to realize equity against a mixed range. Raising would bloat the pot with a vulnerable kicker and is unnecessary; folding would concede too much given the cheap price. I’ll call 1,600 to realize equity and hope to get shown a bluff or thin value; if he has a clear monster I’ll accept the loss.

JT is a strong speculative hand with excellent straight potential and good high-card value. From UTG (early position), I should play a tighter range, but JT qualifies as a solid raising hand. A standard 3x raise to 300 is appropriate here. Simply calling would be too passive with this hand quality and would allow multiple opponents to limp in, creating multiway pots where my hand loses value. I have 41BB of chips (4,100), which is healthy enough to make a normal-sized raise without being committed. This raise applies appropriate pressure while building the pot when I have a solid hand. The aggression is justified by the hand's strength and my position.

I am on the button with Qc 2c. Claude, who is the short stack at the table with 3800 chips, has raised to 300 from UTG. While the hand is suited, Q2s is significantly below the threshold for calling or 3-betting against a UTG opening range, particularly when facing a raise from a player who may be tightening up or looking to commit their stack soon. Furthermore, both Minni and Elon are still to act in the blinds and have been active. Folding is the most prudent play to preserve my stack.

Fold. 9♦3♥ is a clear fold facing an UTG 3x open. I’m out of position in the small blind with a hand that has almost no postflop playability or equity vs an early-position raising range. Calling 250 into a 450 pot requires roughly 36% equity to break even — far more than 9-3o has against Claude’s UTG range. Claude’s stack (3,800) is somewhat short but not short enough to justify isolating or 3-betting with junk, and a 3-bet bluff would put me in difficult spots or risk a shove. Preserve chips for better spots and fold, losing only the small blind already posted.

Qh7c is a weak offsuit hand with minimal playability, dominated by most of Claude's UTG raising range (which includes strong hands like pairs, broadways, suited connectors). Facing a 3x UTG raise, calling 200 into 450 OOP postflop is -EV due to poor equity realization, reverse implied odds, and positional disadvantage. Pot odds require ~31% equity, but we have far less against a tight early position range. Folding preserves our deep stack (104BB) for premium spots in the high roller tournament. Opponents' histories show Claude can have aggression, but doesn't change the fundamentals here.

I am dealt Jack of Diamonds and Four of Clubs (J4o) in the Under The Gun (UTG) position. This is a very weak starting hand with poor playability and low equity. In a four-handed game, UTG is effectively the cutoff/hijack, but even in a short-handed environment, J4o is significantly below the range of hands that should be opened for a raise or called. Given my healthy stack of over 80 big blinds, there is no reason to take unnecessary risks with junk hands. I will fold.

Button with J♠5♠ — a suited hand with good postflop playability and strong positional advantage. Pot is 150, calling costs 100 and the min-raise is 200; I’ll raise to 300 (≈3x BB) to apply pressure and try to steal the blinds. This sizing makes it costly for the short BB (Claude, 4150) to defend and reduces the chance of multiway limp-calls, while keeping the pot manageable against Elon (SB, 10350). If I face a large 3-bet from Elon I’ll fold (hand is often dominated); if called I’ll use position to c-bet favorable flops and extract value from worse hands or fold to heavy resistance. Overall this balances fold equity and postflop playability to maximize expected value over the long run.

Preflop in SB with A♠T♣ facing a button raise to 300 from Minni, who has shown a history of frequent late-position opens that are often wide-ranging (e.g., Hands 17,22,26,30,35,38,41,44,47,50,53,57,59,61,62,66). ATo offsuit blocks AA, AK, AQ and dominates hands like KJo, QJo, A9o in her likely calling range. Equity is strong (~45-55% vs typical button open). Deep stacks (103BB effective vs Minni), so 3-betting for value/isolation/denial of equity from BB is optimal over flat-calling OOP, where we'd be out of position postflop. Sizing to 900 (3x her raise) standard and balanced; fold equity high given villain tendencies to fold to aggression when squeezed (e.g., Hand 51). Minimizes risk if shoved on while building pot with strong hand.

Folding Ts Qs to a 3-bet from Elon (SB) after Minni's button raise. While this is a reasonable hand, the combination of being out of position, facing a disciplined 3-bet indicating a strong range, and having a shallow 4150-chip stack that creates difficult post-flop decisions makes this a fold. Need to preserve chips for premium hands in better position. Tournament is at hand 74/150, so plenty of opportunities ahead.

I opened to 300 on the button with J♠5♠ and Elon responded by 3‑betting to 900. To call I must put in 600 into a 1,300 pot, meaning I need roughly 31.6% equity (600 / (1300+600)). J5s is suited but weak and is frequently dominated by a standard SB 3‑bet range (pairs, broadway A‑x, strong suited hands). Elon has shown heavy preflop aggression and that sizing looks like a value/strong range rather than a light squeeze I can comfortably outplay. 4‑betting as a bluff would bloat the pot out of position and is too risky here. I’ll preserve my deep stack on the button for better spots and fold to this large 3‑bet.

Q6o is marginal but in a 4-handed game UTG opening ranges are much wider than at full ring. I want to take the initiative and apply pressure to the blinds, so I’ll open-raise to a standard 3x (300) to get fold equity and avoid a multiway limp pot. Size: 300 is large enough to discourage light calls/steals but small enough relative to our deep stack to continue postflop if called. Plan: fold to a very large 3-bet (or shove), consider calling a small 3-bet depending on opponent and sizing; if called, c-bet favourable flops, check/protect when out of position and proceed cautiously with just queen-high and weak kicker.

KTs suited is an excellent hand on the button facing a single UTG raise. It plays well postflop in position with flush and straight potential. Minni has shown a wide raising range from early position in prior hands and calls frequently. A 3-bet to 900 (3x the raise) applies pressure, isolates, and builds the pot while maintaining fold equity against tighter ranges. Calling is passive and allows multiway pots; folding is too tight given position and hand strength. Deep stacks support aggressive play to maximize EV over the tournament.

QJs is a premium hand with strong equity. I'm getting 1.6:1 pot odds (need ~38%) and my hand has ~45-50% equity against Elon's 3-bet range. Calling allows me to see how Minni acts while preserving flexibility. With 4100 chips, calling 850 leaves me with 3250 for postflop play, which is better than committing heavily to a 4-bet. This is a standard call in a 3-bet situation with a strong broadway hand.

I am in the Big Blind with Ten of Spades and Trey of Hearts, which is an extremely weak hand. The action ahead of me involves an UTG raise, a Button 3-bet to 900, and a cold-call from the Small Blind. Despite the pot odds, my hand has negligible equity against three opponents who have all shown significant strength, and I would be playing out of position for the rest of the hand. Folding is the only viable strategic choice.

I opened UTG with Q6o as a steal, but facing Elon’s 3-bet to 900 and Claude’s cold-call this has turned into a multiway 3-bet pot. To call I must put in another 600 into a 2,200 pot (poor implicit odds here) and Q6o is frequently dominated by a BTN 3‑bet range and has very limited post‑flop playability out of position. A light 4‑bet bluff is too risky with a cold caller still in and would either get called by better hands or isolate me out of position. With a large, healthy stack and plenty of hands left in the tournament, folding and waiting for a better spot is the +EV choice.

Checking with QsJs on a 4s 2c Ks board. While I have an excellent draw (9 flush outs + 3 straight outs), my hand is not yet made and I'm out of position. My stack of 3,250 is limited relative to the 2,200 pot, so committing chips now with a marginal hand is suboptimal. Checking allows me to see the turn inexpensively, and if I hit my draw, I can play more aggressively. If Elon bets, I can make an informed decision based on pot odds and his action pattern. This is the prudent line with limited chips and a drawing hand.

TPTK on a flop of Ks 4s 2c is very strong heads-up. Board is relatively dry with a spade flush draw possible, but my hand blocks top pair and has showdown value. Claude checked first in, representing possible weakness or a trap, but betting extracts value from Ax, weaker kings/pairs, and draws that he'll call with. Half-pot bet of 1100 builds the pot while controlling sizing against his ~3250 stack. Check risks giving free cards to draws or allowing bluffs. Maximizes EV by charging worse.

Calling with QsJs on a 4s-2c-Ks board. I have excellent equity with a flush draw (9 outs) plus additional straight possibilities. Pot odds of 3:1 require only 25% equity to call, and my hand has approximately 40-45% equity against Elon's 3-betting range. My stack of 3250 is limited, so preserving chips while seeing the turn with a strong draw is more prudent than raising. If I hit my flush or improve, I can leverage my equity on subsequent streets. Calling allows me to keep decision-making flexibility postflop while maintaining chip reserves.

I hold QsJs with a powerful draw on the 4s-2c-Ks-9d board. I have 9 flush outs (any spade) plus 3 additional straight outs (non-spade tens), totaling 12 outs (~48% equity). However, with a depleted stack of 2150 against an 8800 stack, I should avoid overcommitting. Elon just checked the turn, suggesting weakness or a medium-strength hand. By checking, I accomplish several key goals: (1) I see the river card for free if Elon checks behind, (2) I maintain flexibility if Elon bets the river, and (3) I preserve chips for the river decision. If I hit my draw (roughly 48% of the time), I can extract maximum value on the river. If I miss and face a bet, I can make an informed fold/call decision based on odds and pot size. As a short-stacked player out of position with a draw, controlled pot management is preferable to committing chips now with a marginal hand.

Turn 9d is a relatively blank card that doesn't complete strong draws (spade flush draw missed, straight draws like T-J-Q unlikely given preflop action). I hold TPTK (Kd Td), the likely best hand, blocking top pair. Claude's check-check-call flop line suggests Ax, weaker kings (KQo/KJs), mid pairs, or missed draws. His small stack (2150) and passive checks invite a value bet. Betting ~half-pot (2200) extracts maximum value from his calling range while denying equity to any river improvements or bluffs. Checking risks a free card or cheap showdown with worse; pot control unnecessary with nuts advantage heads-up deep. Builds pot for tournament EV, pressures his 2150 stack.

All-in call with QsJs on 4s-2c-Ks-9d. I have 12 outs (9 spades for a flush, 3 non-spade tens for a straight) yielding ~26% equity, which is marginally better than the 24.6% needed given pot odds of 6600:2150. With 75 hands remaining in the tournament and a short stack, I need to take close-to-breakeven +EV opportunities to rebuild my chip count. If I hit my draw, I significantly improve my tournament position.

[9d, Td] is a strong suited connector on the button in a 3-handed game. I will raise to 300 to take control of the pot and potentially take down the blinds or play a pot in position against the larger stacks of Minni and Elon.

I have A♠5♥ (Ace-5 suited) in the small blind facing a button open to 300. Pot is 450 and it costs me 250 more (300 total) to continue. A5s is very playable — good nut-flush potential, decent wheel straight possibilities and reasonable equity versus a wide opening range. With my stack (~163 BB) I don’t need to gamble; a tiny 3-bet to 500 would buy little fold equity and I’d still be out of position on the flop. Calling gives good pot odds (~2.8:1), keeps weaker hands in, and lets me realize equity postflop while keeping the pot controlled. I’ll call 300 total and play the flop accordingly.

8d3s is a complete trash hand with no high card value, connectivity, or suitedness. Facing a button raise from Flash and a call from Minni in SB, the pot is 700 and calling 200 OOP gives poor implied odds and playability. Standard fold to preserve stack for better spots in this tournament.

I called preflop with A♥5♥ to realize equity and keep a wide button opening range in. On Q♦ K♣ 9♠ I have only Ace-high — no pair and only a two-card backdoor heart flush (very low probability). The flop heavily connects with the opener’s raising range (Kx/Qx/9x and many broadways), so leading out OOP with a marginal hand would bloat the pot and put me in uncomfortable decisions on later streets. I’ll check to control the pot and induce bluffs. Plan: if Flash c-bets small (≈1/3 pot or so) I’ll call to see a turn; if he fires a large bet (>~1/2 pot) I’ll give it up unless the turn brings a clear improvement for me (heart/5/A). If he checks back I re-evaluate on the turn and can take a stab there or check-call depending on the card and sizing.

On a board of Qd-Kc-9s, I have bottom pair with a 10 kicker and a gutshot straight draw (any Jack gives me a straight). While I was the pre-flop aggressor, this board texture hits a Small Blind calling range very well (containing many Kings, Queens, and J-T/J-8 type draws). Checking back allows me to realize my equity for free, keep the pot small with a marginal hand, and potentially induce a bluff on the turn or hit my straight. If I bet and get check-raised, I would be forced to fold my 5-out draw and pair.

Heads-up, both checked the dry Q♦ K♣ 9♠ flop and the 2♠ on the turn is a blank. I hold A♥5♥ — currently only Ace-high and only 6 outs (3 aces + 3 fives) to pair on the river (~13%). Given Flash checked the flop, his range contains a lot of weak broadways and missed hands; this is a good spot to take the initiative with a small probe rather than passively bide for a single-out river. I will bet a small fraction (~1/3 pot) to fold out unpaired hands and protect my equity while keeping the pot controllable if called. If he folds I take down the 700 chip pot; if he raises I fold (he likely has a made hand). If he calls I’ll re-evaluate on the river — value-bet small if I pair up, otherwise check and fold to large aggression.

I have bottom pair (Nines) and a gutshot straight draw to a Jack. Minni's lead of 250 into a 700 pot is a relatively small sizing (about 35% of the pot), giving me excellent pot odds to call. With position, I can comfortably call to see if I improve on the river. My hand has decent showdown value if the river goes check-check, and I have several outs to a very strong hand (any Jack, Ten, or Nine). Raising here doesn't seem necessary as it might force out worse hands while getting called by better Kings or Queens, so a call is the most efficient way to realize my equity.

River is a blank for me — with A♥5♥ I only have Ace-high. Both checked the flop and I led small on the turn (250) and got called, so Flash’s calling range is wide (Qx/Kx/9x, some medium pairs and missed broadways). This is a good spot to apply a small probe/bluff: a ~1/3-pot bet targets the unpaired portion of his range and buys the pot without bloating it if called. I’ll bet 400 into the 1200 pot to fold out weaker showdown hands and protect against giving a free card. If he raises I’ll fold (raise indicates a made hand); if he calls I’m usually beaten and will muck at showdown.

I have a pair of nines on a board where the river did not change the texture significantly. Minni's lead on the turn was followed by a 400-chip bet on the river into a 1200-chip pot. This 33% pot-sized bet offers me 5:1 odds on a call, meaning I only need to be right about 17% of the time to break even. In a 3-handed game, bottom pair with a decent kicker has some showdown value against missed draws or lower pocket pairs that Minni might be turning into a small bluff or a very thin value bet. Given the excellent pot odds and the relatively passive line Minni took on the flop, I will call to see the showdown.

Button with J♠4♦ — a marginal hand but good opportunity to leverage position and fold equity in a 3-handed spot. Limping/calling 100 would invite multiway action with a weak holding; folding is too tight given deep stacks and the ability to steal. I’ll make a small, standard open to 200 (min-raise) to pressure the blinds while keeping the pot manageable. Plan: if both fold I pick up the blinds; if one calls I’ll play in position and c‑bet favorable flops for value/bluff; if I face a sizable 3‑bet I’ll fold J4o (dominated and not worth inflating the pot).

Preflop in SB with Ah4h facing a button min-raise to 200 (2x BB). A4s is a strong suited ace hand with good playability, blockers to AA/AK, and flush potential. Button range is wide (Minni opens frequently from history), so I have good equity. Pot odds are excellent: calling 150 more into 350 (~2.3:1, ~30% equity needed, and I have more vs wide range). Calling keeps pot small OOP, allows position play vs BB, and sets up postflop aggression on favorable boards. Folding is too tight; 3-betting risks bloating pot OOP with marginal hand when call realizes equity well. Deep stacks support speculative call.

I am in the big blind with King-Ten offsuit. Minni raised to 200 from the button, and Elon called from the small blind. I am getting immediate pot odds of 5-to-1 to see a flop. While KTo is a decent hand to squeeze with, calling is the most standard and efficient play here to close the action and see a flop against a potentially wide button opening range and a capped small blind calling range.

Flop of 8♦ 2♠ T♦ completely misses my A♥4♥ suited ace. No pair, no draws (heart flush draw blocked by no board hearts, no straight draws or gutshots with A-T gap). Ace-high has some showdown value but is likely crushed by button raiser's wide range (pairs, broadway, diamond draws) and BB defender's calling range (suited connectors, pairs). As SB OOP first to act vs two opponents, pot control is key; checking allows free cards or small bets I can fold to while keeping pot small. Bluffing (check-raise) too risky deep-stacked OOP into multiway pot on drawable board; equity realization poor. Plan to check-fold to significant aggression, check-call small probes only if great odds, but expect to fold. Prioritizes survival and stack preservation in tournament.

I have top pair with a good kicker (Kings) on an 8-2-10 board. While this is a strong hand, checking to the pre-flop aggressor (Minni) is the standard and most profitable play from the big blind in a multi-way pot. This allows Minni to continuation bet with her wide range, including bluffs, whereas leading out (donk betting) would likely fold out hands I dominate and narrow the field to only hands that have me beaten or have significant equity against me. I will check and evaluate based on the ensuing action.

I opened from the button so my range is wide and I have position; both opponents checked the flop (8♦ 2♠ T♦), showing weakness. I have J♠4♦ — no pair and only a very weak backdoor diamond (needs both turn+river), so my hand has little inherent showdown value. With initiative and position I should apply pressure with a small continuation bet to fold out A‑high, weak broadways and small pairs and to keep the pot manageable if called. A ~1/3 pot c‑bet (200 into 600) is the right balance: it maximizes fold equity while preserving stack depth for later streets. Plan: fold to a sizable raise; if called proceed cautiously and barrel only on favorable turns.

Facing a small 1/3-pot bet of 200 into 600 (now pot 800, calling 200 gives 4:1 odds requiring ~20% equity) on a T82dd board that misses my Ah4h completely (ace-high, no draws, backdoor potential only). OOP in SB after two checks, with BB behind. Minni's button range is wide (frequent opens/bets from history), likely polarized cbets: value (TT/88/sets/overpairs), draws (diamond flush draws, str8 draws), and bluffs (missed broadway, weak Ax). Ace-high blocks strong Ax/AA, has showdown value vs bluffs/marginal pairs, and decent equity (~30-40% vs typical loose cb range). Pot odds excellent, calling keeps pot small OOP (~3bb invested total), allows control/cheap float to turn where I can improve (A, heart, runner-runner str8) or showdown. Folding too tight given price/history of Minni's aggression; raising bluff bad (multiway, drawable board). BB may fold/call but doesn't change great price. Deep stacks (~150bb) support realizing equity thin. Plan: call, check turn (fold to big bets unless improves), extract if ahead river.

I have top pair (Tens) with a King kicker on a board of T-8-2 with two diamonds. Minni has made a small 1/3 pot continuation bet, and Elon has called. I am getting 5-to-1 pot odds to continue, which is extremely favorable for a hand of this strength. While I could raise for protection against diamond draws, calling is safer in a multi-way pot as it allows me to control the size of the pot and keeps the ranges of my opponents wider. I will call and evaluate the turn card.

Turn board 8♦2♠T♦6♠ completes no immediate draws for me (still ace-high no pair/draws), but adds spade flush draw threat and pairs 66. Multiway pot (1200) OOP first to act after calling flop cbet thinly for value/showdown equity vs Minni's wide button range (frequent aggression noted in history). Ace-high retains some blocker/showdown value (blocks strong Ax/AA), backdoor equity, but thin vs pairs/draws from Minni/Flash calling ranges. Betting bluffs too thin OOP multiway on connected/drawy board (easy for opponents to continue with equity); risks bloating pot/folding out better. Checking controls pot, induces bluffs/checks from weak hands, allows cheap showdown or fold to aggression (e.g., if Minni value bets pairs/sets/draws). Plan: check-fold to significant bets, check-call small if great odds/improves later. Prioritizes position/exploits loose lines while preserving deep stack (148bb).

On the turn card 6s, the board is 8d-2s-Td-6s. I have top pair with a King kicker (Ks-Tc). In a three-way pot, checking is the most prudent strategy. Elon has already checked, and Minni, the pre-flop and flop aggressor, is behind me. If I check, I can see how Minni responds to the turn. If she checks behind, it suggests she might be slowing down or has a marginal hand, allowing me to potentially go for value on the river. If she bets, I can re-evaluate based on the sizing and Elon's action. Leading out here might force out bluffs that I beat or bloat the pot against hands that have me out-kicked or have improved to two-pair/sets.

Check. The turn (6♠) is a blank for J♠4♦ — my backdoor diamond route is dead and I only have J‑high with one card to improve. Both opponents called my small flop c‑bet, so they likely have real made hands (T, 8, small pocket pair) or are sticky callers; with two players in the pot my fold equity is low and a turn bluff would often be called. Checking controls the pot, keeps my options open in position, and lets me see the river for free; I’ll reevaluate on the river (look to value-bet if I pair, or bluff only if the river is a clean, favorable card and both players show weakness).

River Q♣ on a 8♦2♠T♦6♠Q♣ board completes no flush (diamonds and spades draws missed), adds possible Qx pairs, gutshot fillers (e.g., 97 for str8), or broadway blockers but changes little for my Ah4h ace-high. No pair, no draws realized; pure ace-high with backdoor potential exhausted. Multiway pot of 1200 OOP first to act after turn checked around. Minni (Button) bet flop small/wide (history of frequent aggression/small cbets), checked turn (polarized: weak air/draws missed or traps with monsters like sets/2p/str8s). Flash (BB) passive caller pre/flop, checked turn (likely weak pairs/draws/missed equity). Betting here as bluff too thin OOP vs two (one shorter stack 8975~90bb); scary board but checked turn suggests capped ranges, risks isolation calls from medium strength (e.g., Qx, 98 str8, pairs); folds equity low multiway deep. Value betting impossible. Checking controls pot OOP, retains showdown value (beats Minni/Flash bluffs/air like missed draws, weak Ax, dominated broadway), induces bluffs/bets from worse (e.g., smaller pairs/draws), allows free showdown if checked behind. Plan: check-fold to big bets (e.g., >1000 indicating value/str8), check-call small/medium if pot odds great (~25% equity vs polarized range feasible with blockers). Prioritizes stack preservation in mid-tournament (hand 77/150, deep effective stacks), exploits passive lines/history while avoiding spew.

On the river Queen of Clubs, the board is 8d-2s-Td-6s-Qc. My top pair of Tens has been demoted to second pair, as any Queen now beats me. Elon has checked from the small blind. In a three-way pot where the pre-flop aggressor (Minni) checked back on the turn, the range of hands is likely capped. However, leading out into two opponents with second pair is risky, as I'm unlikely to get called by worse and might be raised by better hands (like a Queen or slow-played sets). Checking is the most standard play here to induce a bluff from Minni or to see a cheap showdown. If Minni bets, I will have to evaluate the sizing, but for now, checking to close the action or see Minni's move is the most prudent strategy.

Final board 8♦ 2♠ T♦ 6♠ Q♣ and I have J♠4♦ — no pair, no meaningful showdown equity. I c‑bet the flop and both opponents called, so they’ve shown willingness to continue with real hands (T,8, small pockets or overcards with a diamond draw). This is a 3‑way pot; bluffing on the river requires both opponents to fold, which is unlikely here. The river Q also helps some parts of their calling ranges. Pot control is the priority — I don’t want to invest more with essentially J‑high against two callers. I’ll check and fold to any sizable raise.

3-handed on the button with deep stacks (~150BB). Unopened pot (pot 150 from blinds, to call 100 to limp). 79o is a standard open-raise from button in short-handed play; it's playable with position, straight potential, and initiative. Limping is suboptimal as it denies equity realization and allows multiway pots without initiative. Folding is too tight. Standard open size is 2.5-3x BB: raise to 300 to build pot and apply pressure on blinds, who have shown aggression but are out of position.

Jc 9c is a strong suited one-gapper that defends well against a button open. While being out of position is a disadvantage, the pot odds offered (250 to call into a 450 pot) make this a profitable call to see a flop. I prefer calling over 3-betting here to keep the pot size controlled and realize my equity.

Fold equity is low and I don’t want to bloat the pot out of position with 4♥8♥. Elon opened from the button and Flash cold‑called from the SB, so this is a multiway pot — a small 3‑bet/raise (to 500) is unlikely to fold out either opponent and just commits more with a marginal hand. Pot is 700 and I only need to put in 200 more to see a flop, which is a good price to realize my suited‑hand equity (flush/backdoor straight possibilities) vs two callers. With deep stacks I can play postflop for stacks when I hit, and fold to heavy aggression on dry boards. I’ll defend the big blind by calling and evaluate on the flop.

On a flop of Qs 7c 5c, I have a flush draw with Jc 9c. Being out of position against the pre-flop aggressor (Elon) and with Minni still to act behind him, the standard and most effective play is to check. This allows me to see how the button reacts. If Elon bets, I have a strong enough draw to call and see a turn card, or potentially check-raise if the sizing and board texture suggest it. For now, checking is the best way to realize my equity.

Board Q♠ 7♣ 5♣ — I have 8♥4♥: no pair, no immediate flush, only a gutshot to a 6 (4 outs) and backdoor heart possibilities. This is a three‑way pot (button raised, SB called) and I’m out of position to the button. Betting here would have very low fold equity and just bloats the pot with a weak, draw‑heavy hand; Elon (button raiser) can apply pressure from last action. My raw chance to improve to a meaningful hand by the river is small (roughly ~16% to hit the straight by river, ~5% runner‑runner heart flush, plus small pair chances), so I should control the pot and realize equity. Plan: check, fold to significant aggression, call only small probe bets that give correct pot odds (e.g. ≲1/3 pot), and re‑evaluate on the turn if checked through.

Middle pair (sevens) with a nine kicker on a Qs-7c-5c board in a 3-way pot. The board has flush draw potential (two clubs) and straight draws (e.g., 86, 108), but I have decent showdown value and block one seven. Both opponents checked, giving me initiative as the preflop raiser with position on the button. C-betting for value against weaker pairs/draws, protection against equity realization, and to maintain aggression. Pot is 900; betting ~2/3 pot (600) balances value and fold equity without overcommitting deep stacks. Checking back risks giving free cards to draws and loses value from floats.

I have a flush draw (Jack-high) on a flop of Qs 7c 5c. Elon, the pre-flop aggressor, has made a continuation bet of 600 into a 900 pot. My pot odds are approximately 28.5% (600 to call for a total pot of 2100). A flush draw has roughly a 19% chance of hitting on the turn and about 35% by the river. While the immediate pot odds for the turn aren't perfect, the implied odds of hitting the flush and extracting more value from Elon's likely range (which include top pair or overpairs) make this a clear call. Raising here as a semi-bluff is an option, but keeping the pot manageable and realizing my equity is the safer, more standard play, especially with Minni still to act behind me.

Call 600. I’m on 4♥8♥ on Q♠7♣5♣ in a three‑way pot — I have no pair but a backdoor heart plan and a 4‑out (6) straight draw plus pair‑up outs. Elon’s raise to 600 is small relative to the 2,100 pot (I only need 600 to see the turn), giving roughly 3.5:1 pot odds (~22% break‑even). My combined chance to improve by the river (straight + pairs + backdoor flush possibilities) is in the neighborhood of — or above — that threshold, so calling to realize equity is +EV. Raising would bloat the pot out of position with a marginal hand and folding would surrender good pot odds; plan is to call, see the turn, and fold to significant turn aggression unless I hit or get correct odds to continue.

I have a flush draw (Jack-high) on a board of Qs 7c 5c 3s. The turn 3s is essentially a blank for my specific draw. Given that I am out of position against two opponents who both showed interest on the flop (Elon betting and Minni calling), leading out with a bet here is risky as it's unlikely to fold out better hands like a Queen or a set. Checking is the most standard and effective play to see how the button (Elon) reacts. If he bets, I will re-evaluate the pot odds and implied odds to determine if calling to see the river is profitable. For now, checking allows me to realize my equity as cheaply as possible.

Check. With 4♥8♥ on Q♠7♣5♣3♠ I have no made hand and no realistic flush — the only meaningful draw is a gutshot to a 6 (4 outs) plus pairing either hole card (≈10 total outs, ~22% to improve on the river). Pot is 2700 and I’m out of position in a three‑way pot against an aggressive button. Betting or raising here would bloat the pot with very marginal equity and has low fold equity multiway, so I preserve my stack and equity by checking. Plan: fold to large turn aggression, call only small turn bets that give correct odds (roughly ≤1/3 pot, ~900), and re‑evaluate on the river or on improvement.

Middle pair with 9 kicker (7s9h) on a Qs7c5c3s turn board in a 3-way deepstacked pot (2700). Board is draw-heavy with club flush draw and now spade flush draw (Qs3s), plus gutshots and possible straight draws. Opponents both checked flop after my c-bet and checked turn again, representing weakness or traps but likely floats/draws/weaker pairs given check-check. I have showdown value, block the 7s, and position. Betting thin value risks raising from better pairs/sets/draws or free equity to villains; pot control is key 3-way deep (~140BB). Checking behind realizes equity, controls pot, denies free cards selectively, and sets up river play where I can value bet strong or bluff-catch. Optimal vs. calling ranges seen in history.

I have completed a Jack-high flush on the river (7c 5c 3c with Jc 9c). The river card also paired the board (3s 3c). However, since the turn checked through after Elon's flop continuation bet, it is highly unlikely that either opponent holds a full house or a very strong hand like the Ace-high flush, as those would typically have bet the turn for value or protection on a draw-heavy board. I am leading out with a value bet of approximately 52% of the pot to extract value from a Queen or an stubborn mid-pair. If I check, I risk the hand checking through and losing value.

River summary: board Q♠-7♣-5♣-3♠-3♣, pot 4,100, bet to me 1,400 (I must call 1,400 to win 5,500 total). Required break‑even equity = 1,400/(4,100+1,400) ≈ 25.5%. I hold 8♥4♥ — no pair, no club, nothing that improved on the river. Flash called the flop, checked the turn, then bets after the 3♣ — a card that both completes club flushes (anyone with two clubs) and gives trips/full‑house lines to holdings with a 3 or Q+3 combos. That line and bet size are exactly what I’d expect from a newly‑made flush or strong trips/full house value bet, and Elon (button) still to act behind. My actual showdown equity versus a reasonable value‑heavy range is far below the ~25% needed, and raising or calling would bloat the pot out of position with essentially air. Fold — preserve a healthy stack for better spots.

River facing Flash's 1400 bet into 4100 pot (~1/3 pot) heads-up deep (~140BB effective, his stack 7875). Board Qs7c5c3s3c pairs treys, completing spade flush draw (Qs3s) and club flush draw somewhat (but 3c low card). I hold 79s (7s blocker), middle pair +9 kicker + spade blocker. Flash checked flop/turn (weakness post my flop c-bet), now donk bets river after board pairs low card. His range: polarized - value (33/3x trips+, Q7/Q3/75 two-pair, sets pre) vs bluffs (missed club/spade draws like AcXc, KJs, A4 suited, gutshots 46/68, floats turning into bluffs). History shows Flash rivers aggressively (e.g., Hand 53 river bet called, Hand 72 river value). My 7s beats all bluffs, Ax floats, weaker pairs (Q9-Q6, 65, etc.), loses to ~20-30% value hands. Pot odds 4100:1400 (~26% needed), I have 40-50%+ equity vs polarized range (blocks 7s/3s somewhat). Calling realizes equity, extracts from worse, villain reps strength poorly with check-check-check-bet pattern. Folding too tight (loses to bluffs); raising thin (risks cooler, commits deep). Standard call with position/showdown value in 3bet pots history favors calls here.

I am on the button with 7-2 offsuit, which is widely considered the worst starting hand in Texas Hold'em. With two active and competent opponents behind me in the blinds, there is no strategic justification for entering the pot with this hand. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

I’m in the small blind with 8♣3♠ — a very weak, unplayable offsuit hand. Button folded so it’s heads-up vs Elon (BB). Pot is 150 and I’d need to call 50 (100 total) or raise to 200; calling leaves me out of position on the flop with very poor playability, and a small raise will just get called by a wide defending BB range and put me in marginal post‑flop spots. With deep stacks and many hands left, this is not a spot to bloat the pot with junk — fold and wait for a better hand/position.

I’m on the button with 2♣7♣ — a very weak hand, but it’s suited and I have position on both blinds. The pot is 150 and it’s only 100 to call (a tiny fraction of my 14,025 stack), so the price to see a flop is excellent. Raising with 7‑2s would be spewy; folding is too nitty given the cheap price and positional advantage. Plan: call to see a flop, but fold to any meaningful preflop 3‑bet or large raise. Postflop I’ll only continue with a made pair, a strong draw, or a clear opportunity to exploit checked streets — otherwise I’ll fold to pressure.

5d6c is a speculative offsuit connector with straight potential. In the small blind facing a button limp, the price to complete (50 more into 250 pot) is excellent, offering over 5:1 odds. With deep stacks (all over 12k) and opponents showing loose tendencies from hand history (frequent calls, multiway pots), implied odds are strong for hitting flops hard. Post-flop OOP but multiway pots favor speculative hands. Folding is too tight pre-flop here; raising is unnecessary with this hand. Will play cautiously post-flop if miss.

I have Ace-Trey offsuit in the Big Blind. Both Minni and Elon have limped into the pot. While Ace-high is often the best hand here, A3o doesn't play particularly well in a raised pot out of position against the button. Checking allows me to see a flop for free and realize my equity without bloating the pot with a marginal kicker.

Flop board 7s-8c-6d gives me bottom pair (6s) with an open-ended straight draw (4x or 9x completes). Equity is strong (~30-40% multiway against typical limping/checking ranges). OOP but deep stacks favor betting for value, protection against overcards/draws, and thin value from worse pairs/draws. Opponents' loose preflop actions (limp/call/check) suggest wide ranges including Ax suited, smaller pairs, connectors. Half-to-3/4 pot bet builds pot while denying equity to floats/draws. Checking risks free cards or multiway pot OOP; leading out is optimal semi-bluff/value line.

On a highly connected flop of 7s-8c-6d, my Ace-high has very little equity. This board texture heavily favors the ranges of players who limp-in (like Elon and Minni), as they often hold middling connectors or gappers. Elon's lead for 225 into a 300 pot indicates strength or a strong draw. With Minni still to act behind me and no significant draw of my own, there is no reason to continue with Ace-Trey offsuit.

I have 7♣2♣ on a 7♠–8♣–6♦ flop: I flopped a pair of sevens (weak kicker) and a very small backdoor club possibility. Elon limped/called preflop and now bets 225 into a 525 pot — a sizing consistent with a wide c-bet (draws, worse pairs, or thin value like 8x/66/77). Given my position and the price (225 to win ~750, ~30% pot odds), calling is correct: my hand has good showdown value versus a wide range and I can control the pot from position on the turn. Raising would bloat the pot with a marginal hand and likely fold out many of the draws I beat. Plan: call now; on the turn re-evaluate — if he checks I’ll barre­l for value on many runouts, if he bets a reasonable size again I’ll call to see showdown unless the turn completes obvious value lines (an 8/6 or a 9/5 giving straights) or he fires a polarized/very large bet, in which case I’ll fold without improvement. If I improve to two pair or better I’ll get aggressive.

Turn board pairs the 8, now 6d-7s-8c-8d. With 5d-6c, I have flopped bottom pair + open-ended straight draw (4 or 9), and the turn 8d doesn't change much but completes many draws. Crucially, I now hold the 5-6-7-8 straight, which is very strong (second nuts behind only 6-7-8-9). Heads-up vs Minni OOP after my flop c-bet got called. Minni's button limp-call range is wide: pairs (66-88), suited connectors (45-97), Ax suited, draws. Many of these (worse pairs, two-pair like 67/68/78, gutshots, flush draws) will call a value bet. Paired board slows down some overpairs/draws, but deep stacks and my flop bet story suggest strength without nuts; continue for max value. Pot 750, bet ~3/4 pot (550) balances value from worse + semi-protection vs unlikely full houses/quads. Check risks free river or checked-down worse hands; betting extracts from calling station tendencies seen in history (frequent calls multiway, loose limps). Avoid pot/overbet to keep worse hands in.

I hold 7♣2♣ and the turn (7♠ 8♣ 6♦ 8♦) gives me two pair (8s and 7s). Heads‑up vs Elon’s line (cb 225 on the flop, bet 550 on the turn) this is a very strong showdown hand against a wide c‑bet range, but it is vulnerable to any 8 (trips/quads), pocket 7s/full house, and rare straights (5‑9). The pot is 1,300 and calling 550 is ~30% pot odds, so a call only needs ~30% equity — well within what two pair has vs a continuing c‑bet range. Raising to 1,100 would bloat the pot and commit me awkwardly when I’m behind to trips/full houses and would often fold out hands I can extract more value from on the river. I’ll call to control the pot, keep position, and re‑evaluate the river: bet for value if he checks, call small bets, and fold to a large polarized jam unless I improve to a full house/quads.

River 3h is a blank that doesn't complete obvious draws or straights in villain's range. Board: 6d-7s-8c-8d-3h. With 5d-6c, I have two pair (8s and 6s), pairing the board's 6. This is a very strong hand since the paired board gives everyone at least pair 8s, and mine upgrades to top two pair. Minni's button limp + two calls (flop 225 into ~250 pot, turn 550 into ~700 pot) suggests a wide range: pairs 4-9, suited connectors (45s-97s), Ax suited, that hit the 678 flop somewhat (pairs, gutshots/OESD). Many of these are now single pair 8s (weak kickers Ax/JT), lower two pairs (e.g., 83s, 84s-85s), or marginal like unpaired turned pairs. History shows Minni as a calling station (frequent multiway calls, loose limps/calls post-flop even OOP). Betting extracts value from ~50-60% of her range (worse two pairs, pair 8s) while fold equity chips away air/draws. Check risks free showdown with worse or her value betting/raising monsters only. Size ~2/3 pot (1200 into 1850) balances value/protection, deep stacks allow it without committing. Positive EV to bet.

I have two pair (8s and 7s) with 7♣2♣ on 7♠–8♣–6♦–8♦–3♥. Elon has c‑bet and barreled the turn and river (225 flop, 550 turn, 1200 river). The river bet is sizable but not polarizing; hands that truly beat me are a relatively small subset (any 8 -> trips/quads/full house; pocket 77/66/33 -> full houses; 8+7 full houses; and rare straights like 4–5 or 5–9). Rough combinatorics put those combinations well under a majority of his possible continuing range (on the order of ~20% of raw combos), while my two‑pair is ahead versus most of his barrel/bluff and single‑pair holdings. Pot is 3050 and calling 1200 requires ~39% equity — well within the equity my hand should have versus his likely range. Raising would bloat the pot when I’m only vulnerable to a narrow, stronger set of hands and would fold out the weaker hands I want to keep. I will call to get to showdown and avoid committing more unless he jams far above this sizing.

3-handed on the button with Ac2c, a suited ace which plays well in position. Unopened pot (assuming standard blinds posted, pot 150, to call 100 implies limping in for 100). Button is the best position to steal blinds frequently. Standard play is to raise a wide range including A2s. Raise to 300 (3x BB) for value, fold equity, and pot control. Deep stacks ~100BB, but sizing keeps it standard. Limp weak, fold too tight given position and hand equity.

I am in the Small Blind with King-Nine offsuit. The Button (Elon) has opened for 300 (a 3x raise). While K9o is a somewhat respectable hand, it plays very poorly out of position (OOP) against a wide but aggressive button range. Calling invites the Big Blind (Minni) to come along or squeeze, and if I do see a flop, I will be first to act on every street. Given the stack sizes and the aggressive nature of the opponents, folding is the most disciplined play to avoid losing chips in a marginal situation where I am likely to be dominated or forced to fold on later streets.

Heads-up to Elon (button). I have K♥7♦ in the big blind — a marginal but defendable hand heads-up. Elon opened to 300; calling puts 200 more in and gives me a 300 total commitment (pot becomes ~650), which is good pot odds (~3.25:1) to see a flop. I’m out of position so I avoid a light 3-bet with this hand; calling keeps the pot manageable and lets me realize equity or fold to strong aggression. Plan: call to see the flop, value-bet top pairs, bluff-catch or fold to heavy action on bad boards, and fold to a large preflop 3-bet.

I defended K♥7♦ preflop vs Elon's button open. Flop 8c 3h 8d is a paired board and I have no pair (only K‑high and negligible backdoor potential). Out of position on a dry, paired flop I don't want to bluff into a wide calling range or bloat the pot with a marginal hand, so I check to control the pot and see Elon's action. Plan: call a small c-bet (roughly ≤ ~1/2 pot ≈ 325) to realize equity and try to hit a K/7 on later streets; fold to large bets or heavy turn aggression. If he checks back I'll re-evaluate the turn (consider a delayed play only if the card helps).

Heads-up vs Minni after Flash folded pre-flop. I raised wide from button with Ac2c suited, she defended from BB, which is wide. Flop 8c-3h-8d is a paired board favoring my range slightly as pre-flop raiser. I have Ace-high plus nut flush draw (8c on board), excellent equity (~35-45% vs typical calling ranges). Her check suggests weakness, possibly floating or weak pairs/draws. This is a strong c-bet spot for thin value (vs weaker pairs/draws), protection (vs gutshots/OESD), and fold equity (missed high cards). Pot 650, bet 2/3 pot (approx 425, sizing to 400 for clean number) builds pot while denying equity. Deep stacks allow aggression. Folding or checking away equity bad; raising pre-flop range reps strength here.

I have K♥7♦ on a paired flop 8c 3h 8d and currently have no pair — just K-high and a very flimsy backdoor heart. Pot is 1,050 and Elon bet 400; calling 400 requires roughly 28% equity to break even. Versus a button raiser who continuation-bets this size, his range will include 8x, pocket pairs/overpairs, strong A/K hands and some polar bluffs. My hand has very little showdown equity and no reliable draw, and I’m out of position — this is not a spot to inflate the pot. My pre-stated plan was to call small c-bets (~≤325) to realize equity, but this bet is larger than that threshold. Raising as a bluff would bloat the pot with almost no fold equity and is exploitable. Therefore I fold to preserve my stack for a better situation.

I am on the button with Six of Spades and Trey of Hearts (6s 3h). This is one of the weakest possible hands in Texas Hold'em.
