While being on the button in a three-handed game allows for a wider opening range, both Minni and Elon have shown significant aggression in recent hands. Trying to steal the blinds with such low equity and poor playability post-flop is unnecessarily risky given my current stack size. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

6h2s is a garbage hand and is heavily dominated, but the price to see a flop is tiny — I only need to call 50 into a 150 pot (about 3:1 pot odds) and heads-up I'll have postflop position. Elon has shown he defends and can be aggressive, so open-raising with pure junk will often be called or 3-bet; I don't want to inflate the pot out of position. Plan: call 100 total (50 more), see the flop, use positional advantage to c-bet selectively on favorable textures or fold to significant aggression.

Preflop in BB vs SB limp after BTN fold. Heads-up pot of 200, no money to call. T3s is a speculative suited connector with straight and flush potential. Deep stacks (107BB effective). Checking realizes equity postflop cheaply with position after SB acts first. Raising risks commitment OOP if called, and hand plays better multiway or seeing flops. SB limp range is wide (any two often), so equity realization postflop is preferable to thin value/steal pre. History shows Minni limps weak hands frequently. Standard play: check to flop.

Check. I’m out of position preflop but now I’m in position and the flop (Kd 7s Ts) is very unfavorable for 6♥2♠: no pair, only a weak backdoor spade and a very low showdown equity. The board has top-pair and two spades, so villain’s checked range includes lots of hands that beat me (Kx, Tx, overcards with turn/river play, spade draws) and I have almost no fold equity here. I’ll keep the pot small and see a free turn to realize my thin equity (about ~24% to pair a 6 or 2 by the river, ~3.8% to complete a double-spade backdoor flush). Plan: check and fold to meaningful aggression; call only a very tiny probe bet (if <~25% pot) to chase, otherwise check-behind and reassess on the turn for a possible bluff if a favorable card appears.

Heads-up flop after completing BB preflop vs SB limp (BTN folded). Board K♦7♠T♠ is somewhat drawy with spade flush draw, but dry enough for middle pair value. Hold T♣3♣ for middle pair (Tens), weak kicker, backdoor club draw, and gutshot to broadway straight if live cards come (e.g., Q or J). SB limp range is wide and capped (frequent weak limps from history: low pairs, suited junk, offsuit broadways occasionally). SB checks flop, representing weakness or traps, but unlikely monsters given limp. Deep stacks (~100BB effective vs Minni's 158BB). Betting thin value ~60% pot extracts from worse pairs (87, Jx suited, weak Tx), bluffs, draws while protecting vs spade draws/spade overcards. Check-back risks giving free card to draws/overcards that beat me, and pot control unnecessary with decent showdown equity. Small bet denies equity and builds pot OOP if called. Standard play: bet small for value/protection.

I have 6♥2♠ on K♦7♠T♠. I checked and Elon bets 125 into a 325 pot (I must call 125). I have no pair and only very thin equity: ~24% to hit a 6 or 2 by the river and ~4% to make the backdoor flush — well below the ~27.8% pot odds a call requires (125/(325+125)). Elon’s betting range is full of hands that already beat me (Kx, Tx, strong spades, overcards that can continue) and I have essentially no fold equity to make a bluff profitable. With a healthy stack and lots of hands left in the tournament, the correct, lowest-variance play is to fold and wait for a better spot.

I’m on the button in a 3-handed spot with Qd3d — a marginal but playable suited queen. In short-handed play I should open a wide range to apply pressure and take advantage of position. Opponents: Elon (SB) has shown frequent preflop aggression/3-bets in past hands; Flash (BB) defends sometimes but has folded to steals as well. Stacks are deep (~150+ BB), so risk is limited and I can fold to a big 3-bet. I’ll raise to 300 (3x the BB) — a standard open size at this table that maximizes fold equity versus the blinds while keeping the pot manageable. Postflop plan: play position — c-bet favorable dry flops, check/float or pot-control on wet/connected boards, and fold to heavy action when I’ve missed. If faced with a large 3-bet, usually fold Q3s given domination risk.

Jh4d is a complete trash hand with no high card value, suitedness, or connectivity. Facing a button raise from Minni, who is aggressive but still has a decent range, calling from the small blind puts me out of position with poor equity and playability post-flop. Pot odds are insufficient (450:250), and folding preserves my stack for better spots in this tournament phase. No value in raising such a weak hand.

I am in the Big Blind with Ten-Seven of spades, a suited connector-type hand with decent playability. Minni has raised to 300 from the Button, and Elon has folded. I need to call 200 more into a pot of 450, giving me 3.25:1 odds. This hand is well within a standard defending range against a button open, especially with the potential for flushes and straights. While I will be out of position, the price is too good to fold, and 3-betting is unnecessary with this hand strength at this depth.

I have defended my big blind with Ten-Seven of spades and the flop comes Jack-Four-Four rainbow. I have no pair and no direct draw on this board. In this situation, it is standard to check to the pre-flop aggressor to see how they react to the paired board. Betting here would be a pure bluff and unlikely to succeed against a wide button-opening range that contains plenty of Jacks and pocket pairs. I will check and evaluate based on Minni's action.

I opened from the button and missed this flop with Q3dd. Board Jc 4h 4s is a dry, paired texture that misses most of the BB’s calling range (broadways, Axs, small pairs). Flash checked, so a standard continuation-bet is the right play to seize the initiative and fold out many overcards and weak hands. I’ll size to ~1/2 pot (325) — big enough to have meaningful fold equity but small enough to keep the pot controllable if I’m called. Plan: if I get raised heavily I’ll fold (Q3 has little equity vs aggression); if called I’ll proceed cautiously on the turn and give up to strong action unless I improve or the turn is very favorable.

On a paired board of J-4-4, my Ten-Seven of spades has very little equity. I have no pair and only a marginal backdoor flush draw. Minni's continuation bet on the button is standard, but without a Jack, a Four, or a strong draw, continuing would essentially be a float with the intention of bluffing later. Given the tournament context and the fact that I am out of position, it is more prudent to fold this weak hand and wait for a better spot to commit chips.

3-handed on the button with 4h5d offsuit, a speculative low connector with poor high-card strength and no suits. Facing an unopened pot (pot 150, to call 100), but this hand plays poorly post-flop without position advantage fully compensating, especially against aggressive opponents like Flash and Minni who have shown wide ranges. Equity realization is low OOP for opponents, and better spots available with deep stacks. Folding preserves stack for premium hands to maximize long-term EV.

I am in the Small Blind with KJo, a strong starting hand in a three-handed game. Elon on the Button has folded, making this a blind vs. blind battle. I only need to call 50 more to complete the small blind, but KJo is definitely strong enough to raise for value and to seize the initiative. A standard raise to 300 (3x the big blind) is appropriate here to put pressure on Minni.

I'm in the big blind with Th7s facing a 3x open to 300 from Flash (SB). Pot is 400 and I need 200 more to call (total committed 300). Th7o is weak, but heads-up vs a likely wide SB opening range it has decent equity (~30–35%), and I'm getting ~3:1 pot odds (need ~25% equity) so a call is +EV. Raising to 500 would bloat the pot out of position with a marginal hand and invite 4-bets; folding is too tight given the price and opponent's frequent 300 opens. Plan: call, see the flop, and proceed cautiously — fold to heavy action on dry over-card flops or continue when I connect or can apply pressure appropriately.

I have King-Jack offsuit on a board of Queen-Ten-Deuce. This gives me an open-ended straight draw (needing an Ace or a Nine to complete a straight). As the pre-flop aggressor in a blind vs. blind battle, I should maintain initiative with a continuation bet. This board favors my range, and even if called, I have significant equity to improve on the turn. A half-pot bet is sufficient to apply pressure and build the pot.

I have top pair (T♥) with a weak 7 kicker on a relatively dry T♣ 2♥ Q♦ board. Flash opened wide from the SB and c‑betting 300 into the ~900 pot looks like a standard continuation with a lot of worse hands and bluffs in his range, so calling gets ~4:1 (need ~20% equity) and is +EV. Raising out of position with a marginal kicker would bloat the pot and often get me in trouble vs Qx, better tens or sets, so I prefer pot control. Action: call to realize equity and evaluate the turn — if I improve (two pair/trips) or get a clear read I can press for value; if the turn is a scary card and Flash barrels big, I’ll fold to heavy aggression.

On the turn, the King of Clubs improves my hand to top pair (Kings) with a Jack kicker, while maintaining my open-ended straight draw (needing an Ace or a Nine). This is a very strong card for my range as the pre-flop aggressor. Minni has called two streets, suggesting she likely has a piece of the board, perhaps a Queen or a Ten, or a draw herself. I will bet for value and to protect my hand against potential draws. A bet of 750 (62.5% of the pot) is large enough to extract value from weaker hands like a Queen or a lower King while pricing out potential straight draws.

I have top pair (T♥) with a weak 7 kicker on Tc‑2h‑Qd‑Kc. Pot is 1950 and I must call 750, so I’m getting ~27.8% equity (750/(1950+750)). Flash opened wide preflop and barreled the flop and turn, so his range still includes many hands I beat (weaker tens, bluffs, many missed overcards) even though Kx/Qx are possible. Raising out of position would bloat the pot and often get me committed against hands that dominate me; folding here would be too tight given the pot odds. I’ll call to realize equity and control the size of the pot — re-evaluate on the river and fold to clear heavy value (big shove) or call small/medium bets on harmless runouts.

On the river, the board is T-2-Q-K-7. My hand is top pair with a Jack kicker. While this is a strong hand, the turn King was a 'scare card' that completed several likely straights for Minni's calling range, specifically AJ and J9. Minni has called every street so far, which suggests she either has a strong made hand or hit her draw. If I bet, I am unlikely to get called by worse hands (like a Queen), but I will certainly be called or raised by better hands (straights or two-pair like KQ/KT). Checking is the most prudent play here to reach showdown as cheaply as possible and potentially induce a bluff from missed lower draws like 98, although most draws arrived. I have significant showdown value but the board is too coordinated to go for three streets of value.

The river 7d pairs my 7 — I now have two pair (Tens and Sevens). Flash opened wide preflop and barreled flop and turn, then checked the river. That line contains a lot of one-pair hands and missed overcards (Kx, Qx, weaker Tens, bluffs) that I beat, and a smaller portion of hands that still crush me (sets: TT/22/QQ, or stronger two-pair combos like KQ/KT). Checking behind guarantees showdown but surrenders value from many calling hands. A half-pot value bet extracts calls from worse pairs and weaker Tens while keeping the pot controllable against a possible check-raise. Size: pot is 2700, so I bet ~half-pot (1350) to maximize thin value while allowing comfortable fold equity if he check-raises big.

I have top pair (Kings) with a Jack kicker. The board is coordinated with potential straights (AJ, J9) and two-pair combinations (KQ, KT, QT). However, by checking the river, I have potentially induced a bluff from missed draws like 98 or J8, or a value bet from a weaker hand like a Queen or a lesser King. Given the pot odds of 3:1 (calling 1350 to win a total pot of 5400), I only need to be right 25% of the time. My hand is too strong to fold in this spot against an active opponent like Minni.

On the button in a 3-handed game, Ten-Five suited is a marginal opening hand. While it's occasionally within a wide button opening range, both Minni and Elon have shown willingness to defend their blinds and play aggressively post-flop. Given my stack size and the current tournament stage, I prefer to wait for a stronger hand to avoid being outplayed in a bloated pot with a weak kicker.

Heads-up from the small blind with Qs6h — a marginal but openable hand in heads-up play. With the button folded I want to take the initiative instead of completing and playing out of position. The min-raise to 200 is too small; I’ll raise to 3×BB (total 300) to generate fold equity, discourage limp-calls, and keep the pot controllable with deep stacks (~188bb). Plan: if Elon 3-bets large I’ll fold (Q6o is often dominated); if he calls I’ll c-bet appropriate flops and play straightforward postflop.

Preflop in the big blind facing a 3x raise to 300 (200 to call) from Minni in the small blind after Flash folded. My hand is 4c Ts, which is a weak offsuit hand with poor connectivity, low playability, and dominated by most raising ranges. Minni has been aggressive in recent hands but still likely has a wide but stronger range than this trash. Folding preserves my stack for better spots; calling risks 200 into 600 pot (3:1) with minimal equity or postflop potential, especially OOP. No need to defend this widely.

On the button with 5♣4♠ and deep stacks (~190bb) in a three-handed pot, I want to open a wide, aggressive range to leverage position and steal the blinds. 5-4o is marginal but has decent playability in position (straight and two-pair potential) and earns fold equity. A standard 3x BB open to 300 applies pressure, reduces multiway callers compared with just calling 100, and keeps the pot manageable. Plan: fold to a large 3-bet, call if I get one caller and play postflop in position with a c-bet on favorable textures, and proceed cautiously if it becomes multiway.

Qs5h is a marginal hand at best, queen-high with poor kicker and no suited or connected value for speculative plays. Out of position in the small blind facing a button raise to 300 (pot 450, to call 250 ~55% pot), which gives implied odds but poor postflop playability against Minni's wide button range. Minni has been active in raising, but folding trash preserves stack for premium spots in this deep-stacked tournament (10775 effective). Standard fold to avoid multi-street mistakes OOP.

I am in the Big Blind with Ten of Clubs and Nine of Hearts. Minni has raised to 300 from the Button. While T9o is not a premium hand, it is strong enough to defend against a standard button opening range, especially given the pot odds. I need to call 200 more into a total pot that will become 650. This is roughly a 3:1 price. Defending here is standard to see a flop and realize my hand's equity in position relative to the small blind (who already folded) and close the action pre-flop.

On a paired board of 6c-6s-7d, I am first to act against the pre-flop aggressor, Minni. My hand (Tc 9h) has two overcards to the 7 and a gutshot straight draw (needing an 8 for a 6-7-8-9-T straight). Standard play for the Big Blind in this situation is to check to the aggressor. This allows me to see how she reacts to the board and potentially realize my equity cheaply or see if she checks back with a range that I currently beat or can outdraw.

I opened from the button and villain (BB) checked a paired flop 6c-6s-7d. My 5c4s has no pair but it has a double-gutter (3 or 8) — ~8 outs and ~31% to hit by the river — so it’s a good semi-bluff candidate. Flash’s check suggests weakness; a small continuation bet leverages position, folds out unpaired overcards and weaker holdings from his wide BB calling range, and keeps the pot manageable when I miss. Bet sizing: ~1/3 pot to apply pressure without bloating the pot. Plan: bet to take it down now; if called I’ll re-evaluate on the turn and continue only if I improve or get favorable action; if he raises I’ll assume strength and fold.

On a paired board of 6c-6s-7d, I have checked to the pre-flop aggressor. Minni has made a small continuation bet of 200 into a pot of 650. My hand, Tc 9h, provides me with two overcards to the 7 and a gutshot straight draw (needing an 8). The pot odds offered are roughly 4.25:1, meaning I only need about 19% equity to justify a call. Given the small sizing and the texture of the board, it is highly likely Minni is betting a wide range of air or weak draws. Calling allows me to realize my equity and see a turn card cheaply without bloating the pot with a marginal hand.

On the turn, the Jack of Spades (Js) is an overcard to the previous board. I have Ten-high with a gutshot straight draw (needing an 8) and two overcards (9 and 10). Being out of position against the pre-flop and flop aggressor (Minni), the standard play is to check. This allows me to see how she reacts to the turn card. If she checks back, I get a free river to potentially hit my straight or a pair. If she bets, I will evaluate based on the sizing, but leading here (donking) doesn't accomplish much against her likely range.

I opened a wide range in position and led the flop as a semi‑bluff with 5♣4♠. On the turn the Jack checked to me — that's weakness and a good spot to continue applying pressure. I currently have no pair but an 8‑out double‑gutter (any 3 or 8 completes a 3‑4‑5‑6‑7 or 4‑5‑6‑7‑8 straight), so about 17% to hit the river if called. Pot is 1,050; I want meaningful fold equity against overcards, weak pairs and some showdown‑type hands, but I don't want to bloatedly commit if I get resistance. I'll fire a medium turn bet of 450 chips (~0.43 pot) as a semi‑bluff. If I'm check‑raised or face heavy aggression I'll fold (hand has too little showdown value); if called I'll re‑evaluate on the river and proceed only with a made straight or a good read/value line.

On the turn, the Jack of Spades is an overcard to my previous overcards (9 and 10). I currently have Ten-high with a gutshot straight draw (needing an 8). Minni has bet both the flop and the turn. To call, I need 450 chips to win a total pot of 1950, which requires approximately 23% equity. With only 4 outs to a straight (roughly 9% chance to hit on the river) and my overcards likely being no good if they pair, I do not have the required equity to continue. Folding is the best play to preserve my stack.

3-handed on the button, Q8s is a premium opening hand with good high-card strength, suited connectivity for playability, and position advantage. Blinds are posted (pot 150 with SB 50/BB 100), so raising to 3x BB (300) is standard to steal frequently against loose defenders like Flash and Minni (who raise/call wide from history). Equity vs typical calling range ~45-50%, postflop initiative and position strong. Limp too passive, fold too tight given deep stacks (~100bb effective). Maximizes EV long-term.

I am in the Small Blind with Jack-Ten offsuit. Elon, the button, has opened for a standard 3x raise. JTo is a solid hand to defend in this spot, as it has good playability on many flops. While I will be out of position for the rest of the hand, folding is too tight against a button raise, and 3-betting might turn a good showdown value hand into a bluff. I will call to see a flop.

I have A♣6♦ in the big blind facing a button open to 300 and the small blind called — a three-way pot. A6o is marginal and will be dominated by a lot of the raiser’s Ax and strong broadway hands, so 3-betting is too thin here. Given the pot (~700) and the cheap price to see a flop (200), defending is correct: good pot odds and I retain post‑flop flexibility with a blocker to Aces. Plan: call to see a flop, play cautiously out of position — fold to heavy coordinated aggression on dry boards, continue aggressively if I pair my Ace/6 or pick up strong initiative/draws.

I have flopped middle pair (Jacks) with a Ten kicker on a board containing a King and a flush draw. In a three-way pot, it is standard to check to the pre-flop aggressor (Elon) to see how the action unfolds. My hand has some showdown value but isn't strong enough to lead out into two opponents, one of whom has the range advantage on a King-high board.

Check. I have A♣6♦ in the big blind in a three-way pot (pot ≈ 900) on J♥ K♦ 4♦. I’m unpaired with only a backdoor diamond and an Ace blocker — very little showdown equity and the K on board heavily favors the opener’s range (Kx, strong broadways, Ax). In a multiway pot a c-bet would be too thin and I want to control the size of the pot out of position. Plan: check and see the turn; I’ll fold to any significant bet (I have almost no fold equity and don’t want to invest more without improving). I’m willing to call a very small continuation bet (roughly ≤1/3 pot, ~300–350) to try to see a turn cheaply, but will release to larger-sized bets. If I pair my Ace, make a real draw or pick up additional equity on the turn I’ll look to take the initiative then.

Preflop raiser on a K-J-4 rainbow-ish board (two diamonds) with both opponents checking, indicating likely weakness in their wide calling ranges (Ax suited, suited connectors, small pairs). My Q8s has queen-high showdown value against pure air and blocks some straight draws like Q10. Board has some straight draw potential (Q10, T5 etc.) and diamond flush draw, so c-betting as a continuation to deny equity to draws/improvers while maintaining initiative with position. Both opponents defend wide historically but check weak flops often. Half-pot bet sizes well for fold equity and pot control deep-stacked. Checking risks giving free cards to overcards/draws and forfeits dead money.

I have flopped middle pair (Jacks) on a King-high board. Elon, the pre-flop aggressor, has followed up with a half-pot continuation bet. In a three-way pot, this bet could represent a King, a draw, or simply a standard C-bet. With middle pair and a decent kicker, I have enough equity to continue, especially getting 3:1 odds on a call. However, I must be cautious since Minni is still to act behind me. I will call to see how the turn develops, but I am prepared to fold if the action becomes too aggressive or if the turn card significantly worsens my position.

Fold. I defended preflop and checked the flop per plan, but Elon’s bet (450 into the ~900 pot — Flash called) is bigger than the thin c‑bet size I was willing to float. The pot is 1800 and I must call 450 (final pot ≈2250), so I need ~450/2250 ≈ 20% equity to justify a call. With A♣6♦ on J♥ K♦ 4♦ in a three‑way pot my actual equity is poor: only a backdoor two‑card diamond flush (very low chance) plus a few pair‑outs (3 sixes and ~3 aces) that are often dominated by opponents’ Kx/Jx/Ax ranges. I’m out of position, face two opponents (one who called the c‑bet), have almost no fold equity and will face tough turn decisions if I call. Raising is unprofitable with this holding. As chip leader I preserve chips for better spots — fold and wait for a cleaner opportunity.

On the turn, I still have second pair (Jacks) and have picked up a gutshot straight draw (any Queen). Following the standard line of checking to the pre-flop aggressor (Elon) is the most prudent move. My hand is a medium-strength holding that is currently ahead of bluffs and draws but behind any King or better Jack. By checking, I control the pot size and can decide how to proceed based on Elon's action and bet sizing.

Facing Flash's check on the turn after calling my flop c-bet on a K-J-4 flop, my Qd8s is queen-high with minimal showdown value against his range, which likely includes Kx, Jx, Ax, draws, or pairs given the wide preflop defend and flop call. The 9h turn completes straight draws (e.g., T8 now hits 8-9-T-J-K, though unlikely), heart flush draws, and doesn't improve me (backdoor diamond blocker but irrelevant). Pot is 1800 with deep stacks (~9k effective). Continuing aggression risks value-owning with bluffs or thin value-bets villain calls profitably with better; checking takes pot control, denies him equity realization on draws, realizes my equity for free showdown vs worse high cards/bluffs, and retains position to bluff or value-bet river if checks through. Consistent with balancing range after frequent c-bets. EV-maximizing play avoids spew in tough spot.

On the river, the board is Jh-Kd-4d-9h-Td. My hand (Ts-Jc) has improved to two pair, Jacks and Tens. While this is a decent hand, the river 10d is a significantly coordinated card. It completes any straight draw containing a Queen (9-10-J-Q-K) and also completes the diamond flush draw (as there are three diamonds on board). Elon checked the turn after being the pre-flop and flop aggressor, which could indicate a medium-strength hand or a draw that has now potentially hit. By checking, I can reach showdown cheaply or evaluate based on Elon's betting size. Value betting here is risky because most hands that would call me (like a King or better) have me beat, and I don't want to get raised off my showdown value.

On the river Td completing the board J♥ K♦ 4♦ 9♥ T♦, my Q♦8s makes the 8-9-T-J-Q straight, a very strong hand (Q-high straight). This is likely the best hand against Flash's range, which includes top pair like Kx, Jx, two-pair combinations (KJ, J9, K4 unlikely), missed draws (diamond flush draws like AdXd, QTs for straight draw), and bluffs/air from wide preflop defend and flop call but multiple checks. Broadway (TJQKA) is possible but requires specific QAx combos, which are narrow; higher straight (9TJQK) even rarer. No flush completes (only 3 diamonds). Flash's triple check (flop call then check-check-check) polarizes to strong made hands or weakness, but value-betting thin with top pair likely in range (e.g., KQo, KJs). Pot 1800, effective stacks ~9k. 2/3 pot bet (1200) sizes for value from worse (Kx ~30% equity vs us, AJs), fold equity vs air, and pot control if raised (we have 10k stack). Checking risks free showdown and missing value from calling stations like Flash (history of hero-calls). Maximizes EV in position.

On the river, the board is Jh-Kd-4d-9h-Td. My hand (Jc-Ts) has improved to two pair, Jacks and Tens. While the river card (10d) is a 'scare card' that completes a potential straight (any Queen) and a potential diamond flush, Elon's check on the turn suggests he likely does not have a monster hand like a set or a turned straight. By betting 1200 into 1800 on the river, Elon is likely betting for value with a King or possibly bluffing with a missed draw. Since my two pair now beats all one-pair hands (like AK, KQ, KJ), and I am getting 2.5:1 pot odds (calling 1200 to win a 4200 total pot), this is a mandatory call. Raising is too risky as it would only be called by hands that beat me (straights, flushes, or sets).

I am on the button with King-Trey offsuit. While K-high hands have some value in three-handed play, K3o is at the bottom of the opening range. Given that Minni and Elon have been active and willing to defend their blinds or 3-bet, I will choose to fold this hand to preserve my stack and wait for a better spot, rather than attempting a steal with a weak kicker that plays poorly post-flop.

Heads-up from the small blind. 6♦3♦ is a weak, but suited, hand — marginal postflop but fine to open in heads-up to seize initiative. With the button folded I want to take the lead and apply pressure with a small standard open to 2xBB (200 total). This gives fold equity to win the 150-chip pot immediately, and if called I still have backdoor flush/straight potential and can c-bet favorable flops. If Elon 3-bets heavily I'll fold — this raise is mainly a steal/isolation play. Calling would be too passive out of position; folding is overly tight given deep stacks and the heads-up situation.

Preflop in the big blind facing a small blind raise to 200 after button folds. My hand 5hTc is a weak offsuit connector with poor playability, low equity against any reasonable calling or raising range, and difficult to play profitably out of position postflop. Minni has been aggressive with frequent preflop raises throughout the history, suggesting a wide stealing range from SB, but folding trash preserves my stack for better spots in this tournament setting where survival and stack maximization over 150 hands is key. Pot odds are not compelling enough (calling 100 into 300 for ~25% equity needed, but realized equity lower OOP with this hand). Standard fold.

I'm on the button in a 3-handed game with Jh4c — a weak holding in isolation but playable as part of a wide button opening range. Limping is suboptimal (invites multiway pots and domination), and folding is too tight given the positional advantage. I want to apply pressure and steal the blinds or play postflop in position, so I open to a standard 3x the big blind (300). If I face a large 3-bet I'll fold this hand; if called I'll use position to C-bet favorable flops and proceed cautiously on ace-high or paired boards where my jack is likely dominated.

9s2h is a complete trash hand: offsuit, disconnected low cards with poor playability and dominated potential against any calling range. Facing a button raise from Minni, who has been aggressive but with a strong initiative, calling 250 into 450 pot offers terrible equity and postflop position/disadvantage from SB. Standard fold to preserve stack in deepstack tournament play, avoiding speculative calls with no hand potential.

I am in the Big Blind with 8s 6s, a decent suited gapper that plays well post-flop, especially in a 3-handed game. Minni, the chip leader, has raised to 300 from the Button. I need to call 200 more into a pot that will be 650, giving me roughly 2.25:1 odds. Given the hand's potential and the favorable price, defending the blind is a standard play here. I will call to see a flop.

I have 8-high on a very heavy Broadway board (Ace, King, Jack) which heavily favors the pre-flop aggressor's range. I have no significant draw and checking is the standard play to see how the opponent reacts.

I opened preflop and on the Kh Jd Ad flop I have middle pair (Jh) with a very weak 4-kicker. The big blind (Flash) called pre and checked the flop — his calling range contains a lot of Ax/Kx and broadways that dominate me, plus some pocket pairs and suited combos that I can still beat. This is an Ace-high, two-overcard board where my hand has decent showdown value but poor playability if I inflate the pot. I want pot control and to let the caller either bluff or show intent first so I can react in position. Plan: check and re-evaluate on the turn — call small, noncommittal bets to realize equity; fold to large, polarized bets that represent strong A/K or sets. Checking preserves chips and keeps weaker hands and bluffs in the pot.

The board (Kh-Jd-Ad-4d) is extremely heavy with Broadway cards and now includes three diamonds. My hand, 8s 6s, has no equity and no relevant draws. Minni checked back on the flop, but this board hits her pre-flop button opening range very hard. There is no reason to lead out with a bluff here, as it's unlikely she will fold a pair or a diamond draw. I will check and hope to see a free river, but I am prepared to fold to any bet.

Turn pairs my 4 and I now have two pair (Jacks & Fours). This is a clear improvement from the flop and is strong heads-up, but the board is dangerous (A/K on board and three diamonds), so I want value and protection without bloating the pot. Flash checked the flop and turn, so his range contains a lot of single-pair A/K hands, some Jx, pocket pairs and some diamond draws. I’ll bet a modest, value-oriented half-pot (325 into 650) to extract calls from worse pairs and Jx, charge any single-diamond flush draws, and keep the pot manageable against the small subset of hands that already beat me (two-pair combos, trips or flushes). If he raises I’ll re-evaluate — call reasonable raises for pot odds with two pair, fold to highly polarized overbets that strongly represent the nut holdings.

The board is Kh-Jd-Ad-4d, which is extremely heavy with Broadway cards and a potential diamond flush. I have 8s 6s, which provides me with zero equity—no pair, no straight draw, and no flush draw. While Minni's turn bet of 325 into 650 is relatively small, I have no showdown value and no realistic chance of improving to the best hand on the river. Given that this board texture heavily favors a pre-flop button opener's range, there is no justification for calling or bluffing in this spot. I will fold.

Preflop on the button, action folded to me after SB Flash folded, facing only BB Minni who has checked. Pot 150, to call 100 for a limp. Hand is 3s5h offsuit, gapped and very weak (bottom ~15% of hands). Even in a prime stealing spot on button vs single BB deep, this hand has poor playability postflop, low equity vs calling range, and tough to realize equity OOP if called or 3-bet. Minni has a massive stack and from history appears aggressive in BB, likely to defend wide or squeeze. Folding trash preserves stack for better spots; stealing here adds unnecessary variance with no edge.

I am in the Small Blind with 7d 6h. The action has folded to me. While completing the bet for an additional 50 chips is tempting, Minni (the Big Blind) has shown considerable aggression and stickiness in previous hands. Playing a weak, non-suited hand like 76o out of position against the chip leader is likely to lead to difficult post-flop situations where I am forced to fold or bluff. I prefer to preserve my 74BB stack for better starting hands or more advantageous positions.

In a 3-handed game, Ace-Nine offsuit (A9o) is a very strong starting hand, especially from the Button. My goal is to open for value and take control of the hand. My stack size is approximately 74 big blinds, which gives me plenty of room to play post-flop, but I want to narrow the field or win the blinds immediately. I will stick to my consistent opening size of 300 chips, which I have used in previous hands from this position.

Facing a 3x open to 300 on the button with 2♦2♥ in the small blind. Pot is 450 and I need to commit a total of 300 (250 more) to call. With deep stacks (my 19,650, BTN 7,125, BB 12,775) pocket deuces are a classic set-mining hand — ~11.8% to flop a set and excellent implied odds if I hit. A tiny reraise to 500 is too weak and a larger 3-bet would bloat the pot and put me in a tough out-of-position spot versus two players; calling keeps the pot controlled and preserves fold equity postflop. Plan: call to see the flop, fold to heavy action on dry boards if I miss, and extract value/raise if I flop a set or strong two-pair draw. Total to commit: 300.

Preflop in the big blind facing a button raise from Flash to 300 and a small blind call from Minni. My hand is 9d4d, a weak suited gapped connector with poor playability out of position. Pot odds are about 3.5:1 (200 to call into 700), but multiway OOP with this hand, my equity is low against typical button opening ranges (which are wide but still crush this) and Minni's calling range. Folding preserves my stack for better spots in this tournament phase, avoiding difficult postflop decisions with marginal equity.

I flatted preflop as a set-mining call and hit my set on the flop (2h2d on 5h-2s-7h). This is a very strong hand heads-up vs the button raiser’s wide range (7x/5x, overcards, pocket pairs, suited connectors). The board is relatively dry (no straights, only two hearts), so I want to extract value and deny free cards. Betting ~50% pot will get called by worse pairs, overcards and many pocket pairs while not folding out too many marginal callers. If I face a serious raise I’ll largely be committed (set vs likely two-pair/top pair/overpair), but for now I’ll build the pot and keep the initiative.

Minni has led into me on a low, somewhat connected board (5h 2s 7h) after calling from the small blind pre-flop. This 'donk bet' often represents a marginal pair or a draw. With Ace-high (A9o), I still have two overcards and decent showdown value against a wide range of bluffs or semi-bluffs. Folding is too tight here, and raising would likely only get called by better hands. I will call the 350 to see the turn and evaluate.

I flopped a set of deuces on 5♥-2♠-7♥ and led the flop to build the pot; Flash called, showing a wide calling range (overcards, 5x/7x, small pocket pairs). The K♠ on the turn is an overcard that actually improves many hands I want value from (Kx/top pair). I should be charging those hands rather than giving a free river. A ~50% pot bet (700 into 1400) will get called by worse pairs and Kx while still denying cheap cards and preserving fold equity. If he raises big I'm largely committed with trips and will call; if he calls I'll continue extracting on the river.

Minni has led into me (donk-betting) on both the flop and the turn. Although the King of Spades on the turn is generally a good card for my range as the pre-flop aggressor, Minni's continued aggression suggests she either has a piece of this board (like 7x or a King) or a strong draw. With only Ace-high and no hearts in my hand, I have very little equity if I'm behind and no real potential to improve aside from hitting an Ace. I'll fold here to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

Button with Qc3c — a suited but weak hand. Versus only the blinds this is a clear spot to open-steal: deep stacks and positional advantage mean I can profitably widen my range. I’ll open to a standard 3x BB (300) to take the initiative, gain fold equity, and play postflop in position. Plan: if faced with a large 3-bet I’ll fold (Q3s is frequently dominated); I may consider calling a very small squeeze depending on sizing, but otherwise proceed to c-bet favorable flops, look for club/queen/backdoor opportunities, and avoid bloating the pot without improvement.

Preflop in the small blind facing a button raise to 300 (250 to call into 450 pot). My hand is J5o, offsuit and disconnected with poor playability and dominated by any reasonable raising range from Minni. Minni has been aggressive with frequent raises throughout the history (e.g., Hands 17,22,25,26,30+), often isolating or stealing blinds. Flash in BB with ~6.7k stack may defend or check-fold weak hands. Equity against Minni's button opening range (~25-40% hands) is low (~30-35% vs typical range including broadways, suited connectors, Ax), with bad postflop prospects on most boards. Effective stack play doesn't justify calling; fold preserves stack for better spots in this 3-handed dynamic where position and fold equity matter.

I am in the Big Blind with Jc5h. Minni, who has been quite active and has a significant chip lead, has raised to 300 from the Button. Elon folded the Small Blind. I have to call 200 more into a pot of 450. While J5 offsuit is a weak hand, I'm getting decent pot odds (2.25:1) to defend. However, out of position against a dominant chip leader who has been aggressive post-flop, this hand plays very poorly. Looking back at recent hands, Minni frequently raises and often follows through on boards. Given that I have 66.75 big blinds, there is no need to defend such a weak holding and risk getting tied into a marginal pot out of position. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

Preflop on the button with SB likely folded (pot at blinds total of 150, to call BB 100). Excellent position heads-up against BB Minni who checked. 65o is a marginal but playable hand with connector potential for straights and occasional strong hands, plus decent implied odds deepstacked. Standard play is to open-raise for fold equity and to play postflop in position. Raising 3x BB to 300 is a good size to build pot or take it down immediately. Minni defends wide but position allows control.

I am in the Small Blind with K4s. Elon, the Button, has raised to 300. K4s is a marginal hand to defend from the SB against a Button raise, as it's often dominated and playing out of position is difficult. However, given the stack sizes and the 2.5:1 pot odds (calling 250 to win a total pot of 450), I can justify a call to see a flop. The hand has decent playability due to being suited. 3-betting is an option but against a Button raise, calling allows me to keep the pot smaller and see if I hit a King or a diamond draw.

I’m in the BB with Js6d facing a BTN open to 300 and the SB calling. Js6 is a marginal, dominated hand but in 3-handed play the opener’s range is wide and I’m getting good pot odds to defend. Calling costs me 200 more (300 total committed) into a 700 pot (900 after my call) — roughly 4.5:1 — so I’ll call to see a flop and realize equity rather than folding or making a thin 3-bet. I won’t inflate the pot preflop with a weak offsuit J; plan on folding to heavy preflop pressure (a sizable reraise) or to large continuation bets on unfavorable runouts. If I hit a Jack or get decent draws, I will continue; otherwise I’ll play cautiously out of position.

On a flop of 7c 9c Jh, I have King-high with a backdoor diamond draw. This board is quite coordinated and likely hits the ranges of both the pre-flop raiser (Elon) and the Big Blind (Minni), who both called pre-flop. Being out of position against two opponents with a very weak hand, the most prudent action is to check and see how the other players react to the board texture.

I have top pair (J♠) with a very weak kicker (6♦) on 7♣ 9♣ J♥. There are two clubs on board so plenty of club draws, and the BTN’s preflop raising range in 3-handed play is wide (lots of Jx, broadways and draws) — meaning I’m often up against better Jx or hands that will continue. I’m out of position with a decent but vulnerable hand, and the SB has checked to me. Pot control is the priority: checking keeps the pot manageable, lets the button show aggression (and potentially fold out bluffs), and avoids bloating the pot with a marginal kicker. Plan if action continues: call small-to-medium c-bets to realize equity and deny draws (roughly ≤ ~1/2–2/3 pot), fold to large overbets/raises unless getting correct odds or clear info to continue, and if checked to showdown I’ll show down my pair.

Preflop raise with 65o in position got two callers, building a nice pot. Flop 7c9cJh is coordinated with flush draw and straight draw potential (e.g., 8T, T8, 10x), but I have a gutshot straight draw to the 5-6-7-8-9 (4 outs to nuts). No pair or flush draw, but ~9% equity against a pair. Both opponents checked, showing weakness on this scary board. As the preflop raiser on the button with position, this is a standard spot for a continuation bet semibluff: apply pressure to fold out worse (e.g., ace-high, weak pairs, missed draws), protect my draw, and take down the pot often. Pot is 900, betting 2/3 pot (600) balances value (if they have overpairs or sets rarely) with fold equity. Deep stacks allow fold equity to work. If called, I have position to realize equity or fold to aggression. Checking risks giving free cards to draws that beat me.

I have King-high with no direct draws on a very coordinated flop (7c-9c-Jh). Elon, the pre-flop aggressor, has bet 600 into two players, indicating strength. With Minni still to act behind me and no real equity, calling 600 is not profitable. I should fold and wait for a better spot.

I have top pair J♠ but a very weak 6 kicker, out of position on a 7♣-9♣-J♥ board. Elon opened wide preflop in 3-handed play and his small bet (600 into 1500 ≈ 0.4 pot) represents a mixed range of value Jx, some pairs/sets and a fair number of draws/bluffs. Calling 600 gives ~3.5:1 pot odds and keeps the pot under control while allowing me to realize equity and see a turn card before committing more. Raising out of position with a marginal kicker is thin — I’ll often get called by better Jx or bloated by sets/draws — and folding would be too passive given the odds. Plan: call to evaluate the turn; fold to large, polarized aggression on the turn (especially if a club or big overcard hits), call small-to-medium turn bets, and proceed cautiously to showdown if action calms down.

Elon checked the turn after a small flop c‑bet; I have top pair (J♠) with a very weak 6 kicker and I’m out of position. Betting here is thin — it bloats the pot and will often get called by better Jx or sets and can be check‑raised by polarized hands. There are dangerous river runouts (any club gives a flush, an 8 completes a 7‑8‑9‑10‑J straight on board), so pot control is priority. I’ll check the turn to keep the pot manageable, let Elon show aggression (and possibly fold bluffs), and re‑evaluate on the river: call small/medium river bets for value, fold to large polarized overbets or clear showdowns when a scare card lands.

Heads-up vs Minni on the turn after she checked. Board 7c9cJhTd is very draw-heavy with flush and multiple straight draws (e.g., 8x for 6-7-8-9-10 or 8Q for 8-9-10-JQ). My 65o gives a live gutshot to the 6-7-8-9-10 straight (4 outs, ~8% equity). Minni called flop c-bet but checks turn, indicating weakness: likely floating pre, weak pair (e.g., Jx, 9x), or missed draw. As preflop raiser in position, continue aggression with semi-bluff bet for fold equity against her capped range, deny equity to her inferior draws/worse, and build pot if she folds or for value if she calls with worse. Pot 2100 deepstacked, 2/3 pot bet (1400) is well-sized: not too thin for value balance, high fold equity. Checking risks giving free card to rivers that beat me (club flush, Q/8 str8). Position realizes equity profitably.

Call 1400. I have top pair (J♠) with a very weak 6 kicker and I'm out of position versus a wide BTN raising/continuation range in 3‑handed play. Elon c‑bet a modest 600 on the flop and turned a smallish 1400 into a 3500 pot — sizing that mixes value (better Jx, two‑pair hands like T9, sets) with a fair number of semi‑bluffers (club draws, Q8/KQ type hands). Calling 1400 gives me roughly 28.6% equity break‑even (1400 into a 4900 pot), which is reasonable: I still have showdown equity vs his whole range and can extract value from worse Jx or induce bluffs on the river. Raising here is thin — I’ll often get called by better jacks or crushed by sets/two‑pair and simply bloat the pot with a marginal kicker — and folding surrenders too much equity for the price. Plan: call to see the river, re‑evaluate on the runout (fold to large polarized river overbets, call small/medium bets for value, and fold if a club/8 hits and he jams), keeping pot control while giving myself a chance to realize equity and scoop value from his bluffs/weaker Jx combos.

I have top pair (J♠) with a very weak 6 kicker on 7♣-9♣-J♥-T♦-A♥. Elon opened wide preflop and has shown aggression (small c-bet on the flop, a bigger bet on the turn), so his turn action polarizes between value (better Jx, T9/two-pair, sets, A‑x) and bluffs (missed clubs/straight draws). The river Ace is a dangerous card that improves a lot of hands ahead of me and also gives Ax hands a comfortable showdown option — his check on the river looks like pot control with value or a missed draw he doesn’t want to bluff away. I only get thin value from a very small number of worse J combos and would often get called by hands that beat me if I bet; bluffing is risky OOP vs an aggressive player who can call or raise. For pot control and to avoid bloating the pot with a marginal kicker, I’ll check and take this to showdown (and fold to a large river shove, call small-to-medium river bets rarely).

River Ah completes some broadway possibilities like QK for straight but mainly an ace-high card that strengthens top pairs (AJ+, AQ) and blocks some bluffs. My 65s5d missed its gutshot entirely, giving me pure air with no pair, straight, or flush. Minni has called flop c-bet (600) and turn bet (1400) on a highly coordinated board (7c9cJhTd), representing significant strength: likely a pair (J9, J7, T9, sets), completed straight draw (e.g., 8x for 7-8-9-10-J), or strong draw that improved. Her river check could be trapping with a monster or weakness, but given her history of calling multi-street aggression deepstacked, her range is capped but strong enough to call a river bet often. Pot is 4900, effective stacks deep (~10k/18k), but bluffing here has low fold equity against her calling range as the defender who floated/called two barrels. No showdown value, so checking avoids spew and realizes 0 equity loss. Value betting thin isn't in range; triple-barrel bluff too risky with river check inviting check-back. Position doesn't matter on river showdown. Goal: preserve stack for better spots over 150 hands.

In a 3-handed game, 96s on the button is a standard opening candidate. I have the positional advantage for the rest of the hand if either of the blinds calls. I will raise to 300 to stay consistent with my previous opening sizes and attempt to take down the blinds or play a pot in position.

I have pocket Jacks in the small blind — a strong, value-heavy hand but vulnerable to overcards postflop. Facing a standard button open to 300, I prefer to take the initiative and isolate the opener rather than play OOP multiway. A 3-bet to 1000 (about 3.3x the open) is large enough to fold out many speculative hands and get value from worse pairs/Ax, while leaving room for postflop maneuvering and not overcommitting my deep stack. If the big blind wakes up or the opener 4-bets, I’ll re-evaluate based on sizing and ranges, but the default is to apply pressure and play the hand for value now.

3s5d is a complete trash hand with no high card value, no suitedness, and poor playability. Facing a button raise from Flash followed by a strong 3-bet to 1000 from Minni in the small blind, this indicates significant strength from at least one opponent. The pot odds (1400:900) require too much equity against likely premium ranges, and my hand has negligible showdown or bluff value. Folding preserves my stack for better spots, aligning with GTO principles of tight big blind defense against 3-bets.

I opened the button with 96s and encountered a 3-bet from Minni in the small blind. While 96s is a decent speculative hand in position, Minni has been quite active and has a massive stack. Calling a 3-bet out of 6075 chips when I have already committed 300 puts me in a tough spot post-flop, especially if I don't hit the board hard. Folding preserves my stack for better opportunities where I'm not facing significant aggression from the chip leader.

I hold 7s2h — essentially the worst starting hand. While button opens widen in 3-handed play, 7-2 offsuit has almost no equity or playability and is a long-term loser. The pot is 150 and it costs 100 to call (or 200 to min-raise). Calling to see a flop with this junk risks getting into marginal post‑flop situations where I have no backdoors; min‑raising to steal is likely to be called or 3‑bet by opponents who have shown they defend and apply pressure. With a healthy stack (23,600) and many hands left, preserving chips and waiting for a better spot is the correct, low‑variance play. I will fold.

Preflop in SB vs BB after BTN fold. 67o is a profitable open-raise in this spot due to high fold equity against a BB defending range and my out-of-position postflop playability with straight potential and suited blockers. Blinds are cheap relative to stacks (100BB deep, opp ~60BB), so stealing is +EV to maximize stack growth. Standard sizing around 3x BB given opponent tendencies to raise/call frequently but fold to pressure in heads-up pots. No reads suggest ultra-tight BB defense. Aggression over passive play OOP.

I am in the Big Blind with Jh 8h, a suited connector that plays reasonably well in post-flop scenarios. Elon, the Small Blind, has raised to 300, which is a standard opening size. I only need to call 200 more into a pot that will be 600. The pot odds are 2:1, which is sufficient for a hand with this kind of playability. Folding is too tight, and raising is unnecessary given my position and the hand strength. I will call to see a flop and use my position to navigate the rest of the hand.

Preflop, I raised with 67o from SB as a profitable steal attempt given fold equity and playability. Flash called from BB, which widens his range to include suited connectors, broadway, pairs, etc. Flop Kc4dJd is coordinated: possible broadway draws (QT), flush draws (diamonds), sets/pairs. My 67o has zero equity - no pair, no straight draw (missed 5/8/9/T), no flush draw. Pure air OOP with no blockers to villain's value (no K,J,4 blockers really). No reason to bloat pot or bluff into his likely capped calling range. BB calling preflop often continues with top pair+, draws. Check-fold is optimal to preserve stack for better spots. Deep effective stacks (his 57BB), no need to risk chips here. Goal is long-term stack growth over 150 hands, not forcing bluffs without equity.

On this flop of Kc-4d-Jd, I have flopped middle pair (Jacks) with an 8 kicker. This is a decent hand on this board, although I am vulnerable to any King or a better Jack. Elon, who raised pre-flop from the Small Blind, has checked to me. This check often indicates either a medium-strength hand, a draw, or a complete whiff. By checking back, I can control the size of the pot and see a turn card for free, keeping his range wide and avoiding a potential check-raise if I were to bet. I will check to see how the turn develops.

Turn 5♣ completes my open-ended straight draw with 6♦7♥ (4-5-6-7 needs 3 or 8, ~32% equity, 8 outs). Board K♣4♦J♦5♣ is coordinated (diamond flush draw, gutshots like QT, 23, 68). Preflop I raised as SB steal, checked flop air (gutshot only then), villain checked back (caps his range: medium pairs, draws, weak broadway; unlikely nuts). Now OOP with strong semi-bluff: my range hits top pairs (KJ/AJ/KQ), two-pairs, sets. Bet denies equity to his draws, extracts from weaker made hands (J7/J9/K9/44/55), builds pot for river value if hit. Check risks free card to villain's draws/improves, or check-raise OOP tricky. Sizing 400 (~2/3 pot, 600 pot) standard: thin value + protection, commits villain's 58BB stack if he shoves (fold equity). Long-term +EV aggression vs history of Flash calling stations but folding to turn pressure. Maximizes stack growth.

I have middle pair (Jacks) on a board of Kc-4d-Jd-5c. Elon checked the flop and is now leading for 400 on the turn into a 600 pot. This bet size is standard for a delayed continuation bet or a semi-bluff. Given my position and the fact that I have a solid pair with showdown value, folding would be too tight. I am getting 2.5:1 pot odds to call. Raising is unnecessary as it would likely only be called by better hands (Kings or better Jacks) and would fold out the bluffs I beat. I will call to see the river and potentially reach showdown.

River 10c bricks my OESD from turn (still needing 3 or 8), leaving 7-high air with no pair, draw, or relevant blockers (6d blocks some missed diamond draws, but irrelevant). Board Kc4dJd5cTc scary: completes nut club flush (Ac/Qc/9c/8c/6c/3c/2c + clubs), Q9 straight (9-10-J-Q-K nuts). Villain's check-call turn (flop check-check pre) = medium pairs (KJ/JT/KT/44/55), weak TPs/TK, two-pair combos, unlikely monsters (would raise turn?). My SB raise-check-flop-bet-turn polarizes to strong value (KJ/AJ/KQ sets twopair) or missed draws. But OOP river bluff vs calling station (Flash calls turn/river light per history: H53 calls river 1100, H50 calls 1500, H78 etc.) low fold equity, poor blockers to value (no K/J/T). Pot 1400, villain eff 5375 (~4x pot shove possible), risk/reward bad for 0 equity bluff. Deep stacks (mine 9625), 55 hands left: fold equity bluffs better with blockers/equity earlier streets. Check: villain may value thin (TPTK) or check back worse air/draws; fold to bet. Maximizes EV long-term stack growth by avoiding -EV spew.

I have middle pair (Jacks) with an 8 kicker on a board of Kc-4d-Jd-5c-Tc. Elon checked the flop, led the turn for a standard bet, and has now checked the river. This sequence suggests Elon likely has a hand with some showdown value that he doesn't want to overcommit with, or he was semi-bluffing the turn and has given up. While my pair of Jacks is relatively strong, betting for value is risky as it is unlikely a worse hand calls, and a check-raise would be difficult to handle. By checking back, I can safely see the showdown and win against missed draws or smaller pocket pairs.

AKo is one of the best hands in poker, top 10% range, excellent playability postflop with top pair top kicker potential. On the button with position on both opponents, and no preflop action indicated (likely blinds only, pot 150, to call BB 100). Raising steals blinds often or builds pot when called. Standard raise size 3x BB to 300, given min raise 200 but larger for fold equity. Opponents have shown tendencies to fold to raises (e.g., many preflop folds in history). Deep stacks allow flexibility, but prioritize value and initiative.

I am in the Small Blind with KJs, which is a strong starting hand in a three-handed game. Elon, the button, has raised to 300. KJs is ahead of a typical button-opening range. While I could 3-bet, calling allows me to play a relatively strong hand from out of position without bloating the pot too much against a larger stack. Given Minni is in the Big Blind with a massive stack and frequently defends or raises, calling keeps the pot manageable and invites her in or allows me to see a flop. I will call the 250 to see the flop.

Pot is 700 and I need +200 to call (total 300). I have QdJh (QJ offsuit) in the BB facing a button open to 300 with the SB already calling — a three-way pot. QJ is worth seeing a flop but is too marginal for a light 3-bet/squeeze multiway, especially out of position versus the button. Calling gives ~3.5:1 immediate pot odds (~22% equity needed) and lets me realize equity with deep stacks; a small raise to 500 would bloat the pot and put me in tough spots without a premium. Plan: call 200 (commit total 300), evaluate the flop — proceed aggressively with a made hand or strong draw, control the pot or fold to heavy action on dry boards.

On this paired board of 7h-7c-3h, I have missed completely with my King-high (Ks Jd). Being out of position in a three-way pot, the standard and most prudent play is to check to the pre-flop aggressor (Elon) to see how the action develops. Betting here into two opponents on a board that likely hasn't hit my range is unnecessary. I will check and evaluate based on my opponents' actions.

I called preflop with QJo to see a flop. Flop 7h-7c-3h gives a dry, paired board and I have no pair — just Q and J (one heart). Backdoor flush would need both turn+river to be hearts (~4.3%), so it’s essentially dead. With two opponents and out of position, betting as a bluff here is a poor line (too many callers, high chance someone has a 7 or a made hand). I’ll check to control the pot and realize equity; fold to significant aggression unless I improve to a pair or pick up a clear, strong draw on the turn. I may call a small probe bet for pot odds, but will fold to large bets/raises on this dry board.

Preflop, AKo is premium, raised from button for initiative and value, got two callers building a nice pot of 900. Flop 7h7c3h is a paired, dry board with two hearts, favorable for my range as preflop raiser. I have AK high, overcards, backdoor flush draw with Kh (three hearts total: Kh,7h,3h), and excellent blockers to Ax and Kx. No strong made hands likely called pre (77 rare), so ahead of floats, draws, weak pairs. Both opponents checked, giving me free initiative. Standard c-bet to take it down, deny equity to draws (flush, str8 draws like 45,56), protect, and build pot when ahead. Position allows control postflop. Bet sizing 2/3 pot (600) balances value from worse and fold equity vs trash. Opponents fold frequently to aggression per history (e.g., many folds to c-bets). Deep effective stack (Flash 6475), but pot odds poor for calls without equity.

I am holding King-high (Ks Jd) on a paired board of 7h-7c-3h. Elon, the pre-flop aggressor, has bet 600 into a 900 pot, which is a significant 2/3 pot bet into two opponents. I have no draws, no hearts, and very little equity to continue. Furthermore, Minni is still to act behind me. Calling 600 chips here with no clear path to winning the hand is not a profitable move. I will fold to conserve my stack for better opportunities.

I checked the flop to control the pot with Q♠? (Jh Qd) — I have two overcards and a single heart, but no pair on a dry, paired 7-7-3 board. Elon bets 600 into a 1500 pot; calling 600 would need roughly 28.6% equity (600 / (1500+600)). My realistic chance to improve to at least a pair by the river (pairing Q or J, plus a tiny runner-runner flush chance) is roughly mid-20s percent — borderline or below the break-even equity, and some of those hits still lose to trips or other made hands. Flash folded to the bet, which reduces the likelihood this is a pure bluff. I’m out of position with deep stacks and don’t want to bloat the pot or get C-bet/turned by a strong range. Raising as a bluff is too risky. Sticking to my pre-flop/flop plan (fold to significant aggression on this dry board), I fold and preserve my stack for better spots.

I am on the button with a pocket pair (77) in a 3-handed game. This is a very strong starting hand in this context. I will put in a standard raise to 300, which has been my consistent opening size throughout the tournament, to take advantage of my position and build the pot.

Facing a button open to 300 with QcJs in the small blind: pot is 450 and it costs me 250 to call. Flash (button) has been opening to 300 a lot, so I expect a fairly wide opening range. QJs is strong enough in this spot to 3‑bet — it has good equity and blockers and I prefer to seize initiative rather than play multiway out of position. The min‑raise is 500, but I want a sizing that gives meaningful fold equity and pressures weaker holdings without committing an excessive portion of my deep stack. I will 3‑bet to 1000 total (≈10BB) to isolate the opener and force tougher decisions from the big blind. Postflop I’ll continuation-bet favorable textures and proceed cautiously if I face a large 4‑bet/very strong action (re-evaluating vs shove-sized 4‑bets).

Pocket deuces is a marginal set-mining hand. Facing a button open to 300 and SB squeeze re-raise to 1000 total (900 more to call into a 1400 pot), pot odds are 1400:900 or ~39% equity needed heads-up after calling. 22 has roughly 20-25% equity vs a strong 3-bet range (JJ+, AK, etc.). Button (Flash) likely folds wide to the SB squeeze given history of folding to 3-bets, making it heads-up OOP vs Minni's deep stack. Implied odds exist but not enough to overcome poor direct equity and tough post-flop spot OOP. Minni's large sizing suggests strength. Fold to preserve ~100BB stack for better opportunities in this tournament phase.

I have a solid pocket pair (77) on the button. While Minni's 3-bet from the small blind shows strength, 77 is too strong to fold in a 3-handed game. By calling, I maintain position for the rest of the hand and can see how the flop develops before committing more of my stack.

I have QcJs on Jd Qd 6h — top two pair. I acted OOP and 3‑bet preflop, so Flash’s flatting range will include a lot of single-pair hands (weaker Qx, Jx), KQ/KJ, some pocket pairs and some diamond combos. This is a very favorable flop for my range and for this specific holding, so I should lead for value and protection. A sizing around half-to-two-thirds pot extracts value from worse Queens and Jacks and charges the limited straight/flush draws while not overcommitting if he shows heavy strength. Pot is 2100, so I’ll bet 1200 (~55–60% pot) to deny free cards and get called by worse hands. If he raises big or shoves I’ll re-evaluate (large aggression often represents sets/trips and I’ll fold or call depending on sizing and reads).

The flop of 6h Jd Qd is unfavorable for my pocket sevens, as there are two overcards and potential diamond draws. Minni's 3-bet pre-flop and a continuation bet of 1,200 into a 2,100 pot show significant strength. Given the board texture and Minni's aggression, it is highly likely I am currently behind or facing a very strong draw. With 4,475 chips remaining, calling would commit a large portion of my stack to a marginal hand. I will fold to preserve my remaining chips for a more favorable situation.

On the button with Kh3s and deep stacks (244 BB), I want to take the initiative and use position to apply pressure. K3o is marginal but well within a standard button opening range — a 3x-ish raise is small relative to stacks, gives fold equity versus the blinds, and lets me play postflop in position if called. I’ll raise to 300 (total) to isolate or pick up the blinds. Plan: vs a single caller continue with standard c-bets on dry/favorable flops and exercise pot control or check back on heavy overcard/connected boards; fold to a sizable 3-bet preflop since K3o performs poorly vs strong ranges.

Tc6d is a very weak offsuit hand with poor playability, no straight or flush potential, and dominated against typical button raising ranges. Defending SB OOP against an aggressive button raiser like Minni with this hand is -EV long-term. Pot odds (250 into 450 for ~700 pot) aren't sufficient given the positional disadvantage and multiway potential with BB behind. Prioritize stack preservation and wait for better spots in a deep-stack tournament.

I am in the Big Blind with Jd 2h. Minni, who has been active and aggressive, raises to 300 from the button. My hand is very weak (Jack-high with a low kicker) and does not play well out of position. Given Minni's large stack and the fact that I'd be playing post-flop against a dominant stack, there is no reason to defend this specific holding. Folding is the most disciplined and standard play here.

84d 4h is an extremely weak offsuit hand with large gaps, low high card, and poor post-flop playability. Even on the button in a 3-handed pot after SB folded and BB checked (implied by action to me with to-call BB), this is not in a profitable stealing range here. Historical actions show folding similar or stronger hands like K4o, T7o from button. BB Minni has a massive stack and defends aggressively in big pots per history. Deep stacks, many hands left (51/150), better to wait for premium spots to maximize EV long-term. Fold preserves stack.

I am in the Small Blind with 5d6d, a playable speculative hand in a 3-handed game. The Button (Elon) has already folded, leaving only Minni in the Big Blind. Minni has been playing aggressively, but 5d6d has decent equity and playability if I complete or raise. However, since the goal is to maximize stack size and I have a healthy stack, completing the small blind (calling 50 more) is a standard move to see a flop with a suited connector while keeping the pot manageable against a chip leader.

Three-handed with the button folded and the small blind simply limping, K♦J♥ is a strong hand to open raise: it plays well heads-up and dominates a lot of the SB’s limp/call range. I want to isolate Flash, take the initiative and play a pot with position on the flop. I’ll raise to a standard 3x-ish size (to 300 total) — big enough to fold out very weak holdings and build value vs worse hands, but small enough to keep the pot manageable vs Flash’s ~5.3k stack. If I face a large 3-bet or shove I’ll re-evaluate (likely fold to an overbet/commitment), and if called I’ll c-bet favorable flops and control pot size when behind.

I started by completing the small blind with 5d6d, a hand with good post-flop potential. Minni, who is the massive chip leader and has shown significant aggression throughout the tournament, raised to 300 from the big blind. I need to call 200 more to see a flop. Given the pot odds (calling 200 to win a total pot of 600) and the playability of my suited connectors, I have a clear call to see a flop, even out of position. I'll need to be cautious post-flop if I don't hit hard, as Minni often follows through with bets.

I have 6-high on a paired board of 4-9-4. I was the one who called the pre-flop raise from the Small Blind, so I am out of position against Minni, the chip leader. Given that I have no real pair or significant draw on this flop, checking is the most standard and prudent play. I want to see how she reacts to the board before committing any more chips.

I raised preflop to take the initiative and play in position. The flop 4c‑9h‑4s largely misses my K♦J♥ but is very dry and disfavors a lot of the SB caller’s range (many broadways, suited connectors, weak Ax, small pairs that often fold). With position and fold equity I should continuation-bet to take the pot now and extract from worse hands/draws. I’ll size to ~1/2 pot (300 into 600) — big enough to fold unpaired holdings and get value from weaker pairs/draws but small enough to keep the pot manageable if called. If he flats I’ll proceed cautiously on the turn (check and re-evaluate); if he check‑raises big I’ll fold given my lack of pair.

I have 6-high on a paired board (4-9-4) with no draws. Minni, the chip leader, raised pre-flop and has now made a continuation bet on the flop. Given her high frequency of aggression and my lack of equity or even a backdoor draw, there is no reason to continue in this hand. I will fold and preserve my stack for a better opportunity.

I am on the button with J8o. Minni and Elon are both aggressive players, but Minni has a massive stack and can easily bully. J8o is a marginal hand, but it's often worth a standard raise from the button to steal the blinds or play in position. However, given my stack size is roughly 50BB and I'm between two very active stacks (one massive, one double mine), I will open for a standard raise to 300 to take control of the hand.

Facing a raise to 300 from the button while I'm in the small blind with 4♥2♣. I must call 250 to see the flop and will be out of position against both the raiser and the big blind. 4-2 offsuit has almost no equity versus a button opening range, is frequently dominated, and has very poor postflop playability — a min-raise is too thin and calling puts me in marginal spots for 250 chips. Preserve stack and fold this weak hand.

AQo is a premium hand with excellent equity against a button opening range, which is typically wide in this multiway tournament setting. Flash has been aggressive with frequent raises from late position throughout the history, often continuation betting but folding to resistance post-flop in spots. As BB, we have position post-flop if called, but 3-betting builds the pot for value, denies equity to weaker aces/dominators, and can take down the pot pre-flop often enough. Calling OOP risks playing out of position with a hand that plays better aggressively. 3-bet sizing to 900 (3x the open) is standard: large enough to fold out junk but not overbetting given stacks (my 99BB, his 47BB). Folding is never correct here.

I opened J8o from the button, which is a standard steal attempt. Elon responded with a 3-bet to 900 from the big blind. While I have position on Elon, J8o is significantly behind a typical 3-betting range and does not play well in bloated pots with a medium stack-to-pot ratio. Given Elon's demonstrated aggression throughout the tournament, I expect to be put under significant pressure post-flop. I have 47 BBs remaining, which is plenty of room to wait for a more favorable hand or a better spot to contest a pot. Folding is the most disciplined play here to preserve my stack.

Button with K♦6♦, facing only the blinds (pot 150, to call 100). In 3-handed play I should open a fairly wide range — K6s is playable with position and suitedness. I'll take the initiative and raise to apply pressure, pick up the blinds, and give myself position postflop. I choose a standard 3x BB open to 300: big enough to generate fold equity vs the blinds but keeps the pot manageable with deep stacks. Plan: if both fold I take the pot; if either defends I’ll play postflop in position, c-betting favorable textures and folding to strong resistance. If SB (Elon) 3-bets big (large sizing or shove) I will usually fold K6d out of position; if Flash (shorter stack) jams, I’ll evaluate pot odds and likely call smaller shoves to isolate.

Facing a button raise to 300 from Minni, who has a wide opening range in 3-handed play, but my 6c9s is a weak offsuit gapped hand with poor playability and low equity against a calling range. In the small blind, out of position, calling 250 into a 450 pot offers poor implied odds even against a wide range, as postflop I'll be OOP with a marginal hand. Flash in BB may squeeze or call. Best to preserve stack and wait for better spots; folding is standard to avoid difficult postflop situations and realize fold equity doesn't apply here since I'm not raising.

I am in the Big Blind with Jack-Five offsuit. Minni, who has a massive chip lead and has been very active, raises to 300 from the Button. Although it is only 200 more for me to call into a pot of 450, my hand is very weak and easily dominated. Given the stack dynamics (I am the short stack with about 46 big blinds) and Minni's aggressive history, I prefer to fold and wait for a better spot rather than play a weak hand out of position against the chip leader.

82o is a complete trash hand with no high card value, poor connectivity, and offsuit. As first to act preflop on the button in a 3-handed pot with blinds posted (pot 150, to call 100 for a limp), there's no fold equity if I raise, and limping invites multiway pots where this hand performs terribly postflop. Opponents have shown aggressive tendencies in history, but this spot calls for a fold to preserve stack and wait for playable hands. Tight play maximizes EV long-term over 150 hands.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Four offsuit (Ah4d). Elon has folded from the button. It's now down to me and Minni in the Big Blind. The pot is currently 150 (100 from Minni's BB and 50 from my SB). I need to put in 50 more to call. Given it's a blind-vs-blind situation, Ace-high is a strong hand. Raising is usually better than calling here to take initiative and potentially win the pot immediately, especially since Minni has a massive stack and has been aggressive. However, a standard open raise from the SB is typically 3x. I will raise to 300 to put pressure on Minni and assert control of the hand.

Call 300 total (I’ve already posted 100, so I’m adding 200). Heads-up vs the SB open to 300, K5s is marginal but worth defending from the big blind: the raise is small so pot odds are attractive and the suited king has backdoor/fold equity and reasonable showdown value. I’m out of position, so I don’t want to 3-bet a weak kicker; calling keeps my range wide and lets me see a flop cheaply and fold to heavy aggression if I miss. I’ll re-evaluate on the flop and play straightforwardly unless I improve.

I was the pre-flop aggressor and the flop (6d 3s 2h) is relatively dry, which should favor my perceived range. While I only have Ace-high, I also have a gutshot straight draw. A continuation bet of 300 (50% of the pot) is a standard play to maintain initiative and potentially fold out better hands or hands that have air.

Flash's flop bet is small (300 into ~900) so a call only needs ~20% equity. With K♠5♠ on 6♦-3♠-2♥ I have a king overcard plus a backdoor spade possibility — not great, but enough equity vs a wide SB c-bet range that includes many unpaired hands and bluffs. I'm out of position and have a weak kicker, so I don't want to lead or bluff-raise; calling keeps the pot manageable, lets me see a turn cheaply, and fold to heavy aggression if I miss. I will call and re-evaluate on the turn.

The turn is a Queen of Clubs, which is an overcard to the flop. I have Ace-high and a gutshot straight draw (needing a 5). Minni has called my pre-flop raise and my flop continuation bet, suggesting she has at least some part of this board or a pocket pair. While the Queen is a good card for my perceived range, Minni's stickiness makes a second barrel risky. I will check to see how she reacts to the overcard. If she checks back, I might still have the best hand or can evaluate on the river. If she bets, I will have to consider the price and my outs.

Flash checked the turn after c-betting the flop. I hold K♠5♠ — only king-high with a thin backdoor spade draw. The Q♣ on the turn is an overcard that likely helped a large portion of the opener’s range (Qx, Ax, or even slow-played pairs), so I have very little value and limited fold equity. Pot is ~1200 and the effective stack is roughly 4075, so betting here risks a raise or call from hands that currently beat me. I’ll check to control the pot, realize my equity to the river, and keep options open: if the river is a clean, low card I can consider a small delayed bluff; if it pairs or brings more danger I’ll check/fold to aggression.

On the river, the board has completed with 6-3-2-Q-K. I was the pre-flop aggressor and continuation-bet the flop, but checked the turn when the Queen appeared. Minni also checked the turn, suggesting she might have a medium-strength hand like a small pair (6x, 3x, 2x) or a pocket pair that she is playing cautiously. While the King on the river is a good card for my perceived range, I have some showdown value with Ace-high against potential busted straight draws like 5-4. Given Minni's large stack and her tendency to be sticky in previous hands, a bluff here is risky. I will check and hope to win at showdown or fold if she makes a significant bet, preserving my remaining 4,075 chips.

The river Kc changes the decision: I’ve turned top pair (Ks) with a very weak 5-kicker. Flash’s line — open preflop, small c-bet on the A-irrelevant flop (6-3-2), then check on the turn and river — looks like a wide, fairly weak c-bet range that contains many missed overcards, small pairs and bluffs. Very few hands in his checking range reliably beat my K5s (mostly Kx with a better kicker or rare sets). 

Betting a small-to-medium size extracts value from Qx, small pairs (6x/3x/2x), and missed hands that will call a modest price, while keeping me from committing a large portion of my deep stack. I don’t want to overbet and force folds from worse or create a spot where a check-raise (which would strongly indicate a K with a better kicker or a set) puts me to a difficult decision. 

I’ll bet half the pot (600) to get called by the worse parts of his range and fold to significant aggression if he turns this into a big raise — his check-check line into a raise would indicate enough strength that my weak kicker is likely second-best.

I have Ace-high on a board that run out with two overcards (Queen and King). Minni called my pre-flop raise and flop continuation bet on a low board, which typically suggests she has at least a small pair or a draw. After we both checked the turn, she bet half the pot on the river. Given the board texture and her stickiness in previous hands, it is highly likely she has a pair that beats my Ace-high or has connected with the river. I will fold to preserve my stack.
