I am on the button with A7o, which is a strong starting hand in a three-handed game. I'm facing a small blind and a big blind with significantly larger stacks, especially Minni. Standard strategy on the button is to raise to put pressure on the blinds and take control of the hand. An Ace-high hand has good value here, and a standard 3x raise is appropriate.

I'm in the small blind with 9♣T♣ facing a button open to 300 (pot 450; I need 250 more). 9T suited is a strong, playable hand three-handed — good straight/flush potential — but it's not strong enough to 3-bet for value out of position. Flash (button) is short (~3.8k) so his open is likely wide; Elon (BB) has shown a tendency to defend and is likely to come along if I raise, which would leave me out of position in a multiway pot. A minimum 3-bet buys little fold equity here and a larger isolation raise risks bigger confrontations I don't need yet given my big stack advantage. I’ll call 300 total (250 more) to see a flop, keep the pot controlled, and use postflop skill to extract value or fold to heavy action depending on the texture and opponents' reactions.

6d4s is a weak suited connector with gaps, low playability out of position in a multiway pot. Facing a button raise from Flash (loose position) and a call from Minni in SB (defending wide). Pot odds are about 3.5:1 for calling 200, but equity against their ranges is insufficient (~25-30% at best) without strong implied odds postflop OOP vs two opponents. Flash's stack is 37BB, limiting big implied odds. Folding preserves stack for better spots in tournament with 48 hands left. No value to raise with this hand.

Board K♥ Q♥ T♦ — I have T♣9♣: a pair of tens with a 9 kicker. This is a decent but vulnerable holding: many hands in the button’s preflop opening range (Kx, Qx, AJ, J9, some two‑heart combos with flush potential) beat me or have strong equity. I’m out of position to the preflop raiser (Flash), so I don’t want to inflate the pot OOP with a hand that is often second best. Betting OOP here risks getting check‑raised or induced shoves from a short stack and bloating the pot when I’m behind; checking keeps the pot under control and lets me call a reasonable c‑bet to deny draws equity. Plan: check and call small-to-medium bets to realize equity and re‑evaluate on the turn; fold to an over‑aggressive raise/shove that represents K/Q/A or a made straight. Given pot control and positional disadvantage, checking is the best line.

The flop of Kh-Qh-Td is extremely wet and hits a large portion of Minni's calling range from the small blind (broadway cards, pairs, and straight draws). While I have an Ace as an overcard, any Jack on the board currently makes a straight. Betting into this board is risky as I'm likely to face a call or a check-raise from hands that have me significantly beat. Checking back allows me to see a free turn card, control the pot size, and potentially realize my equity or reassess if the board improves my hand.

Turn brings J♣ to K♥ Q♥ T♦ — my 9♣T♣ now makes a 9–10–J–Q–K straight. Heads‑up this is a very strong made hand; the main hands that beat me are A‑10 (Broadway) and rare combos that make the same straight (T9). Flash checked the flop, lowering the chance he holds A10 or a slowplayed two‑pair/set; more likely he has Kx/Qx/Tx or other marginal hands. I should extract value OOP rather than check and give him a free card, but avoid overcommitting since A10 is possible. A ~1/2‑pot value bet (350) charges worse pairs and tens and induces calls from hands I beat while keeping the pot manageable if he raises or shoves.

The board is Kh-Qh-Td-Jc. Any nine or any ace now makes a straight. I hold the Ace of Diamonds, giving me the 'nut' straight (A-K-Q-J-T). Minni leads out for 350 into a pot of 700. Since I have the best possible hand at this moment, I want to extract value. However, the board is extremely coordinated; any 9 also has a straight (though a lower one). If Minni has a 9, she might call a raise. If she has a smaller straight or a set, she is likely to continue. Given the stack depths and the fact that I have the nuts, I will call here to keep her range wide and potentially induce a bigger bet on the river, or raise if I feel I can get immediate value from a King or a Queen. Actually, with the nuts, a raise is standard to build the pot. I will raise to 1050.

The turn J♣ gives me 9♣10♣ a 9‑10‑J‑Q‑K straight — a very strong made hand but the second‑nut: any Ace in Flash’s hand now makes the nut straight (10‑J‑Q‑K‑A) and beats me. Flash’s line (button open, checked flop, big check‑raise to 1050 on the turn) is polarized — he may have the nut (A‑x), the same straight (T9), or strong two‑pair/sets trying to protect; there are fewer pure bluffs given his short stack. Folding a made straight heads‑up is too passive; re‑raising is risky because it bloats the pot and mainly gets action from hands that already beat me (A‑x). Calling 700 more (1050 total) controls the pot, keeps worse hands (KQ, pairs, some J/T combinations) in to extract value, and gives me the chance to re‑evaluate if he jams. I will call to 1050 total and reassess if he commits the rest of his stack.

I have the second‑nut straight (9♣10♣ on K♥ Q♥ T♦ J♣ 3♠); only an Ace in villain’s hand makes the absolute nut. Flash’s line (button open, checked flop, then a big check‑raise to 1050 on the turn) is polarized — Ax, T9 (the same straight), or strong two‑pair/sets are the main candidates, but his preflop opening range contains lots of Kx/Qx/Jx and single‑pair hands that I beat. I want to extract value from those worse hands while keeping the pot controlled against the Ace portion of his range. A small‑to‑medium river bet (~1/3 pot) charges Kx/Qx/Jx/pairs and is less likely to induce an all‑in from an Ax hand than a large overbet. I’ll bet 1000 into the 2800 pot to get value and fold equity against weaker holdings; if he jams I’ll re‑evaluate based on his exact shove sizing and my read (pot odds require ~29% equity vs a shove of his remaining stack), calling only if his shove range looks mixed enough to make a call +EV.

Flash holds the Ace of Diamonds, which completes the nut straight (A-K-Q-J-T) on a board of Kh-Qh-Td-Jc-3s. There are no possible flushes or full houses. Minni has led out for 1000 on the river after calling a raise on the turn, which indicates she likely has a very strong hand, such as a lower straight (any 9) or a set. Since Flash has the best possible hand, the correct play is to raise all-in to maximize value and capitalize on Minni's likely willingness to call with a inferior straight or set given the pot size and her massive stack.

I have 9♣10♣ on K♥ Q♥ T♦ J♣ 3♠ — the 9–10–J–Q–K straight (second‑nut). Only an Ace in villain's hand makes the absolute nut; any 9 (including T9) chops. Flash checked flop, then check‑raised my turn raise to 1050 and now shoved to 2725 — a polarized line that includes Ax, the same straight (T9), two‑pair/sets and some bluffs. The pot is 6525 and I must call 1725 more; calling 1725 risks winning a pot of 8250 (pot odds ≈ 21%). Even giving villain a wide, strong line‑weighted range, the frequency of Aces required to make a call unprofitable is unrealistically high. Many of Flash's shove combos (Kx/Qx/sets/Two‑pair) are behind my straight; T9 ties; only Ax beats me. With ~21% required equity and a large portion of his shove range that I beat or chop, calling is +EV. I will call the all‑in and accept the chop/beat possibilities rather than folding a very strong made hand.

I’m on the button with 8♣2♠ — a very weak hand, but in a 3-handed game the button should be opening a wide stealing range. A min-raise to 200 (2x BB) applies pressure to the blinds for a small cost and preserves fold equity. I won’t limp (too passive) and will fold to a sizable 3-bet — if called I’ll play postflop in position and c-bet or exercise pot control depending on the texture. Min-raising is the lowest-variance, exploitative way to try to pick up the blinds while keeping options open.

3-handed, blinds 50/100. In SB with 4d7c, a complete trash hand: offsuit, gapped, low cards, no potential. Facing button raise to 200 (pot 350, to call 150). Button opens wide, but this hand has zero playability OOP post-flop. No fold equity for 3-bet, calling commits ~1.5% stack OOP with dominated range vs raiser. Stack 10225 effective. Folding preserves stack for better spots. Clear fold.

I am in the Big Blind with Qs Td. Minni, the chip leader, has made a minimum raise to 200 from the Button. Elon has folded the Small Blind. I need to commit 100 more chips to call into a pot that will be 450. QTo is well within the defending range against a Button raise, especially at these odds. I will call to see the flop.

I have middle pair (Queens) with a Ten kicker on a board containing an overcard (King). Since Minni was the pre-flop aggressor and I am out of position in the Big Blind, the standard play is to check to the raiser and see how they react to this board. I want to control the size of the pot given that a King is present.

I opened preflop and am in position; the flop (4♠ K♥ Q♦) completely misses my 8♣2♠ but is fairly dry with no real flush draws. As the preflop aggressor I have initiative and can often take this down against a wide Big Blind calling range (many broadways, Kx/Qx, small pairs) by applying pressure. A small continuation bet (~1/3 pot = 150 into 450) gives good fold equity while keeping the pot controllable if called. If Flash folds I win the pot; if called I’ll proceed cautiously on the turn (check and re-evaluate, fold to strong raises). This is the lowest-variance, exploitative line here.

I have middle pair (Queens) with a Ten kicker. The board shows an overcard (King), but Minni's bet is quite small (150 into 450). Middle pair is too strong to fold to a single small continuation bet on this texture. I will call the 150 to see a turn card and evaluate further, as my hand has decent showdown value but is not strong enough to raise for value against her range.

I am out of position against Minni, who was the pre-flop aggressor. I currently have middle pair (Queens) with a Ten kicker on a board with an overcard (King). The 3d on the turn is a blank that doesn't significantly change the board texture. My goal is to reach showdown as cheaply as possible or induce a bluff from her air. Betting here doesn't achieve much as I'm likely only called by Kings or better, so I will check to see how she responds.

I hold 8♣2♠ and completely missed. I opened preflop and made a small c-bet (150) on a fairly dry flop and was called — Flash’s calling range is likely Kx/Qx, some broadways and small pairs. The turn 3♦ is a blank for my hand and actually increases backdoor possibilities for opponents (another diamond on board). I have no realistic equity and betting here would bloat the pot with air and expose me to a check-raise or value calls from better hands. In position I should control the pot and re-evaluate on the river: check behind, fold to a large bet on the turn, and look for a river spot to bluff or realize equity for free if checked to.

On the river, the board has completed with an Ace of diamonds. I currently hold second pair (Queens) on a board that now contains two overcards (King and Ace). The Ace is a significant scare card. Minni checked back the turn, which could indicate a medium-strength hand or a missed draw. However, my hand is not strong enough to bet for value, as I am unlikely to get called by worse hands and will almost certainly be called by better ones (Aces, Kings, or flushes). Checking is the most prudent play to see if I can reach a cheap showdown or evaluate based on any bet Minni might make.

I have 8♣2♠ and missed every street. I opened a wide button range and made a small C‑bet (150 into ~450) on a dry K♥Q♦4♠ flop; Flash called, then both of us checked the turn (3♦) — his turn check signals pot‑control or weakness. The river A♦ is a real scare card for my missed range: it gives showdown value to Ax and creates a 3‑diamond board (so two‑card diamond hands can make a flush). Flash checked the river as well, which is a sign he likely doesn’t have a strong value hand to bet with, but that same river also makes a bluff thin — many Kx/Qx hands that called the flop will still call a small river bluff, and there’s some chance of a made flush or Ace that will extract value if I bluff. The pot is small (750) and stacks are deep, so I don’t want to bloat the pot with air or risk a large river raise/bluff‑catcher situation. Pot control and preserving my stack is the higher‑EV, lower‑variance line here: check and concede unless Flash fires a very small, highly exploitable bet (in which case I’d consider a targeted call). For now: check.

Ts3h is a complete trash hand: offsuit, disconnected, low cards with poor playability. Acting first pre-flop on the button with 3 players left, facing deep stacks and aggressive opponents based on history. Pot odds to call 100 into 150 are poor for such a weak hand, and limping invites raises. Folding preserves stack for better opportunities to maximize long-term EV in this deep-stack tournament.

I am in the Small Blind with 8-5 offsuit. The button has folded, leaving only me and the Big Blind (Minni). Minni is the chip leader and has been playing aggressively. While it is only 50 more to call, 8-5 offsuit is a very weak hand to play out of position against a large stack. To conserve my stack and wait for better opportunities, I will fold.

I am on the button in a 3-handed game with T9o. This is a standard opening hand for this position to put pressure on the blinds and take advantage of being in position post-flop. I will raise to 300, which has been my standard opening size throughout the tournament.

I have A♦7♠ in the small blind facing a button open to 300. I must call 250 into a 450 pot (final pot 700) so I need ~36% equity to break even. I'm out of position post‑flop versus the button, and A7o is frequently dominated by the button's opening range (many Axs and broadways). With deep stacks both opponents can apply pressure and I have mediocre post‑flop playability. A minimum raise (to 500) or a light 3‑bet are higher variance lines that are only profitable with a specific read that Flash is opening extremely wide. Without that read, the +EV play is to conserve chips and fold this marginal offsuit ace OOP. I'll fold and wait for a better spot.

Facing a button raise to 300 after SB folds, pot 450, to call 200 (already in 100 BB). K3s is a marginal but playable hand OOP vs wide button range: suited connector-ish, King blocker, good implied odds if I hit. Pot odds excellent (~2.25:1 direct, plus future streets). Folding too tight; 3-bet risks OOP with poor postflop playability; shoving overkill with stacks ~10k effective. Call to realize equity, control pot OOP if miss, play draws aggressively if hit. Flash aggressive historically but folds to resistance often postflop.

On the flop of Ts Ah 6h, holding Kh3h in BB (OOP), I have the nut flush draw (four to the nuts with Kh blocker) plus backdoor straight potential. Board is draw-heavy (flush draw, gutshots like KJ for broadway, 78, 45), coordinating well with my hand. Pot 650, acting first vs Flash's button raise (wide range: Ax suited, broadway, suited connectors, pairs). Equity ~35-40% vs his range, excellent implied odds with effective stacks ~8k. Leading risks him check-raising Ax or sets; better to check-raise his likely continuation bet (he c-bets frequently per history, e.g., Hands 1,6,9,11). Induces bluffs/draws, builds pot OOP when strong, controls if checked behind. Flash folds to postflop resistance often (e.g., Hand 1,7,12). Check to realize equity optimally, set up raise to ~1k-1.5k or shove if bet.

I have middle pair (Tens) with a 9 kicker on an Ace-high board with two hearts. Elon checked to me after calling my pre-flop raise. While an Ace is definitely in Elon's range, his check gives me the opportunity to take a 'c-bet' (continuation bet) to see where I stand and potentially end the hand right here if he missed. However, checking back to control the pot size and realize equity with my pair is also a valid strategy. Given the board texture and the fact that I have a piece of it, a small bet of around 40-50% of the pot (300) serves as a good semi-bluff/value bet to get information and put pressure on missed draws or smaller pairs.

Facing a small 300 c-bet into 650 pot (pot odds 3:1+), with nut flush draw (Kh3h on Ts Ah 6h, 9 outs to nuts, plus backdoor straight/gutshot potential with any heart or runner-runner). Equity ~40%+ vs Flash's wide button range (Ax suited, broadway like KQ/KJ, suited connectors 87s/54s, mid pairs; board coordinates heavily with both ranges). OOP check-raise optimal: induces folds from bluffs/missed draws (Flash cbets flops frequently but folds to postflop aggression often, e.g., Hands 1,6 flop fold, 12 fold, 20 river fold, 29 river fold, 36 turn fold). Builds pot when ahead/draw heavy, gets value from worse Ax (A10/A9), weaker draws (non-nut flush draws like QhJh). Raise to 1200 (~4x his bet, ~1.25x pot) balances value/protection without overcommitting stacks (~10k eff); if reraised/shoved, excellent implied/ pot odds to continue. Folding/calling too passive OOP with this strength; flat risks free cards to his draws. History confirms aggression exploits his tendencies.

I have middle pair (Tens) on an Ace-high board with a heart flush draw possible. After I made a continuation bet of 300, Elon check-raised to 1200. This action strongly indicates he either has a better hand (likely an Ace, which is well within his big blind calling range) or a high-equity draw. My Ten with a Nine kicker is very vulnerable to many turn cards (any Ace, King, Queen, Jack, or Heart), and continuing would likely require committing even more of my stack on later streets while being out of position relative to his shown strength. Folding preserves my stack of 8000 chips for more favorable situations where I am not facing significant aggression with a marginal hand.

Three-handed on the button I should be opening a wide range — position and fold equity are my biggest assets. 6♦8♥ is marginal but playable in position, and a standard 3x BB open maximizes fold equity against the blinds while avoiding giving good odds to callers. I’ll raise to 300 to take the initiative. Plan: fold to a large 3‑bet; if called I’ll use position to C‑bet favorable flops and proceed cautiously on coordinated boards or when facing big resistance.

Preflop in SB facing a button raise to 300 (2.5BB). My hand 7h6s is offsuit, mediocre connectivity, poor playability postflop. Minni on button likely raising wide, but 76o doesn't defend well here. Pot odds are 250 to call into 450 (~1.8:1), but implied odds are low with this hand due to domination risks and reverse implied odds. Folding preserves stack for better spots in this deepstack tournament phase. No need to speculate on Flash's action.

I am in the Big Blind with 3d 4h, one of the weakest starting hands in poker. Minni opened to 300 from the Button. While I only have to call 200 more to see a flop, I would be playing a very low-equity hand out of position against a player with a significant chip lead. There is no strategic value in defending this hand given the circumstances.

Pocket Jacks is a premium hand, especially on the button with position on both opponents. The pot has 150 in dead money from blinds. No action before me, so this is a standard spot to raise for value, build the pot, and potentially isolate weaker hands. Opponents have shown willingness to call raises wide in past hands (e.g., Minni calls frequently, Flash mixes aggression), but JJ dominates most calling ranges and performs well postflop in position. Raising to 3x the BB (300) is standard sizing here to get value from worse pairs, suited connectors, and broadways that they might call or 3-bet light. Avoid limping to prevent multiway pots. Stacks are deep enough for flexibility postflop.

I am in the Small Blind with Queen-Trey offsuit, which is a very weak hand. Elon, the Button, has raised to 300. Calling here would put me out of position for the rest of the hand with a sub-par holding, and Minni in the Big Blind still has the option to squeeze or call. Given my stack size and the stage of the tournament, there is no reason to defend such a weak hand against a raise from a competent opponent. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I'm in the big blind with A♥8♣ facing a standard button open to 300 (I have 100 already posted, 200 to call). A8o is worth defending here: the Ace blocker and decent showdown equity vs a wide button opening range make a call +EV. Pot odds to call 200 into a 450 pot require ~31% equity, which A8o usually meets against a button's opening range. I prefer to call rather than 3-bet: I'm out of position postflop and don't want to bloat the pot with a marginal hand. Plan postflop is straightforward — continue aggressively if I hit an Ace or strong draw, otherwise proceed cautiously and fold to heavy, consistent pressure on dry boards.

Check. I have A♥8♣ on Q♦7♦4♣ — no pair, just Ace-high with a useful Ace blocker. I’m out of position versus the preflop raiser (Elon) and the flop is fairly dry, so I don’t want to bloat the pot with a marginal hand. Checking controls the pot size, preserves my ability to call a standard c-bet, and lets me fold to heavy multi-street pressure. Plan: call small-to-medium continuation bets (roughly up to ~1/2 pot, ~325) to realize equity and fold to large or polarized raises; if Elon checks back I retain showdown equity and can re-evaluate on the turn.

Pocket JJ is an overpair on a Qd7d4c flop, which is dryish but has a diamond flush draw and minor straight draws. Minni checked from BB after calling pre-flop raise, representing a wide range including Ax, KQ, suited connectors, pairs below J, and draws. JJ has strong equity against her calling range, blocks QQ, and position allows control. Betting for value from worse (TT-88, Ax) and protection against draws (flush/straight draws ~20-30% equity). Checking risks giving free cards to draws or induces bluffs, but betting builds pot OOP while I have fold equity. Size to 450 (~70% pot) balances value/protection without overbetting vs her sticky tendencies seen in history (frequent calls post-flop). Avoids check-raise traps from sets (unlikely). Continues pre-flop aggression.

I checked the flop with A♥8♣ to control the pot and see a c-bet. Elon fires 450 (Pot: 1100, To Call: 450) — calling 450 into the 1100 pot requires ≈29% equity (450/(1100+450)). This is a relatively large, polarizing c‑bet on Q♦7♦4♣ that is very consistent with many Qx hands, two-pair/sets, and strong value combinations; my hand is just Ace‑high with limited showdown equity out of position. I planned to call small-to-medium continuation bets but fold to heavy, polarized pressure. Raising as a bluff here is unnecessarily risky without a pair or clear blockers to Q combos. Preserve stack, avoid playing OOP in a bloated pot with a marginal hand — fold.

I am on the button with A2o. This is a standard raising hand in a three-handed game to put pressure on the blinds. However, looking at the recent history, Minni and Elon have been playing aggressively. A2o is a weak Ace, but usually good enough for an open raise to 300 to take down the blinds or play in position. Given my current stack size of 7850, I want to maintain aggression but be careful if re-raised.

Facing a standard button open to 300 from the SB with Q9o. Pot is 450 and calling 250 requires ~35.7% equity to break even (250/(450+250)). Q9o is a marginal, often dominated hand versus a button’s open (lots of Qx, broadways and pairs) and I’ll be out of position postflop acting first against up to two opponents. A tiny reraise to 500 is possible, but with this hand and deep stacks I don’t want to bloat the pot OOP or risk a big 3-bet from Elon. Fold and wait for a better, positionally favorable spot.

2c3d is an extremely weak hand with no playability, low equity against a button raising range (even a wide one), and terrible postflop potential out of position. Pot odds are ~2.25:1 (calling 200 into 450), requiring ~31% equity to call profitably, but this hand realizes far less equity OOP vs. a typical button open (closer to 25-30%). Folding preserves stack for better opportunities in a tournament setting where survival and stack maximization over 150 hands is key. No reads from history suggest Flash is opening super wide here warranting a defend.

6♠4♦ offsuit on the button facing both blinds: this is a weak, poorly-connected hand that plays badly multiway and is often dominated. Calling 100 into a 150 pot requires ~40% equity to be break-even (100/(150+100)=40%), which 6‑4o will not have vs two reasonable SB/BB ranges. A min‑raise to 200 to try to steal is possible, but both opponents have shown willingness to defend and to 3‑bet/raise — raising here with junk just creates marginal spots OOP or vs aggression. With deep stacks and many hands left, preserving chips and waiting for a better spot is the +EV play. Fold.

Pocket Jacks is a premium hand, top of my range pre-flop. Minni has folded, so heads-up against Flash in the BB with a shorter stack of 7900. Raising takes initiative, builds the pot when ahead against a wide defending range, and denies equity to weaker hands. Standard raise size of 3x the BB (300 total) is appropriate given the blinds and dynamics observed in prior hands where players raise around 250-400 frequently. Out of position but value warrants it over limping or calling.

I am in the Big Blind with Ten-Four offsuit. Elon, in the Small Blind, has raised to 300. While I am getting 2:1 pot odds to call, T4o is a very weak hand to play out of position against an aggressive opponent like Elon. To preserve my stack for better opportunities, I will fold.

Pocket nines is a premium hand, especially on the button with position on both blinds. With no pre-flop action indicated and pot at 150 (blinds posted), standard play is to raise for value and to isolate against wide blind defending ranges. Opponents have shown aggression in history, but 99 plays well post-flop in position. Raising to 3x the big blind (300 total) is a balanced size to build the pot while maintaining fold equity. My stack of 11k allows for deep play, and this maximizes EV over many hands.

In the Small Blind with King of Spades and Ten of Hearts (KTo), I am facing a button raise from Elon to 300. KTo is a decent hand to play in a 3-handed game, but being out of position makes it tricky. Elon has been active and has a larger stack. However, with the current stack of 78.5 big blinds, calling 250 more to see a flop is reasonable to realize equity, though folding or 3-betting are also considerations. Given the tournament context and Elon's frequency, I will call to see the flop and play post-flop.

2♦T♠ is a very weak offsuit hand — poorly connected and a bad kicker. Elon opened to 300 from the button and Flash (SB) called, so this is a three‑way pot and I’m out of position to both. The pot is 700; calling 200 would put me in for 300 total and requires ~22.2% equity to break even (200/(700+200)). T2o’s multiway equity versus a button open and an SB cold‑call is well below that and is frequently dominated by Ax or broadway hands. A min‑3bet to 500 is technically available but would be a thin, non‑credible bluff OOP facing two players (SB has shown a propensity to call opens), and I don’t want to bloat the pot with a marginal hand out of position. With deep stacks I can wait for a much better spot to invest chips. Fold and preserve stack for higher EV situations.

I have King-high on a dry board (6c 9d 4h) after calling a pre-flop raise from the button. As I am out of position against the pre-flop aggressor, the standard play is to check to him and see how he reacts. I do not have a made hand or a significant draw, so I will play cautiously.

Top set on a dry flop 6c-9d-4h is an extremely strong hand, likely the nuts or close to it. Flash called a preflop raise from the SB (loose range) and checked the flop, which is standard but his range is wide including overcards, weaker pairs, and draws like 5-7 or 8-10 for gutshots. No strong flush or straight draws completed. With position and pot control, betting for value and protection is optimal to build the pot against his floats, weak pairs, and any draw that might continue. Half-pot size (around 350) extracts value from worse hands like 7x, 8x, Ax while denying equity to draws. Effective stacks are deep (Flash 7600), so no need to overbet. History shows Flash calls bets but folds to aggression often when beat.

I am holding King of Spades and Ten of Hearts on a board of 6c 9d 4h. This is a very dry board and I have completely missed. My opponent, Elon, raised pre-flop from the button and followed up with a continuation bet of 350 into a pot of 700. I have only King-high with no real draw besides two overcards. While K-high has some showdown value against a total air bluff, calling out of position with just two overcards on this texture is unprofitable in the long run against a continuation bet, as I will have to fold to further aggression on most turns. Folding is the most disciplined play here to preserve my stack.

I am on the button with QJ-offsuit, a solid hand in a three-handed game. My stack is currently 7,600, which is around 76 big blinds. Elon and Minni have both shown aggression pre-flop in earlier hands. Standard strategy here is to raise to 3x the big blind (300) to take control of the pot and pressure the blinds. QJ is well ahead of an average calling range and plays well post-flop. I will raise to 300.

Facing a standard button open to 300 while I’m in the small blind with Qh4c (offsuit). I’ve already posted 50 and would need to call 250 into a 450 pot and then play out of position (likely against both the button and the big blind). Q4o is frequently dominated by the opener’s range and has very poor post‑flop playability; with Elon (BB) capable of 3‑betting, peeling here invites difficult multiway OOP decisions. The min‑raise to 500 makes a light 3‑bet less attractive deep stacked and I don’t have enough fold equity or hand strength to justify it. Preserving a healthy 20,775 stack for better spots is the priority — fold this marginal hand.

A8s is a strong speculative hand from the BB facing a button raise. Flash's opening range is wide given position and history of frequent raises. Pot odds are favorable (calling 200 into 450, ~3:1), with excellent implied odds due to suited ace potential for top pair top kicker, straights, flushes. Playable post-flop despite OOP. Folding too tight; 3-bet risks vs polarized range. Call to realize equity.

Flop: Ac6h5h gives me top pair (aces) with weak 8 kicker and a weak backdoor flush draw (three hearts total). Board is draw-heavy with heart flush draw and open-ended straight draws (e.g., 4x7x). Acting first OOP vs button aggressor Flash, who raised pre-flop and has a wide continuing range including Ax (often better kickers), sets, two-pair, straights/flush draws. Check to pot control, induce bluffs/C-bets from missed hands/draws, realize equity cheaply if he bets small, or get free river if he checks behind. Leading risks raising into monsters or getting check-raised off equity; check-call or check-fold post-bet depending on sizing/range. Deep stacks (eff. 7300) favor controlled pot with marginal top pair.

I raised pre-flop and the flop came Ace-high, which is very favorable for my perceived range. Elon checked, indicating weakness or a willingness to play a pot-control line. Although I haven't hit anything with QJ, this is a prime opportunity for a continuation bet. A bet here puts pressure on Elon's middle pairs and missed draws. I will bet 325, which is exactly half the pot, to represent the Ace and take down the hand now.

TPTK (As pairing Ac) with backdoor nut flush draw on a draw-heavy board (hearts flush draw, OESD possibilities like 2-3-4-7). Flash's button c-bet is small (33% pot), consistent with wide range: weaker Ax (dominated), draws (KhQh J7h suited connectors), floats, bluffs. OOP check-call realizes equity, controls pot size, induces further bluffs/checks behind on turn, while preserving value from worse hands/draws. Raising risks check-raise from sets/two-pair/combos and folds out equity from draws; folding too tight given strength and pot odds (>3:1 direct, massive implied). Deep effective stacks (Flash 6975) favor floating turn/river plays. Plan: call flop, reassess turn (check-call/lead if improves, check-fold to big bets on scary cards). History shows Flash bets light post-flop.

TPTK (Aces with Ace kicker) on a paired board (sixes) with open-ended straight draw threats (e.g., 34, 78) and lingering heart flush draw. Turn 6s pairs the 6h, improving some straight draws (e.g., 47 now makes straight) and pairing the board, which reduces Flash's bluffing range slightly but his small flop c-bet (325 into ~1000? pot) suggests wide continuation: weaker Ax (dominated like A7-A9), draws (KhQh, suited connectors like 74h, 87s), floats, or made hands like sets/two-pair. OOP first to act deep (~7bb effective vs Flash's 6975). Leading risks bloating pot with marginal value hand vs better Ax/sets and folding out worse hands/draws; check controls pot, induces bets from his bluffs/draws/marginal hands for thin value or cheap showdown, potentially free river if checks behind. History: Flash frequently bets light post-flop, calls stations; plan to check-call small bets or check-fold large overbets. Raising unnecessary with villain's capping range thin. Check optimal to realize equity while minimizing loss vs monsters.

The board has paired on the turn with the 6s. Having missed the flop entirely with Qd Jc and being called on my flop continuation bet, I need to be cautious. Elon's call on the flop suggests he has a piece of the board (likely an Ace, a 5, or a heart draw). By checking back the turn, I control the pot size and take a free card to potentially improve to a pair on the river. It also prevents me from being check-raised, which would force an immediate fold. If Elon checks the river as well, I can evaluate whether a bluff or a check-down is the best path forward based on the final card.

River Kh completes the heart flush draw (flop had 6h5h, now three hearts) and is a high card that improves AK, pairs kings for sets, or gives broadway to some hands. I hold As8h: top pair (aces) with a weak 8 kicker and a heart blocker (8h blocks some flush combos like QhJh, but not nuts like Ahxh). Board paired sixes on turn, limiting some straight draws. Flash's line: button RFI (wide: Ax suited, broadway, suited connectors, suited aces like A8s itself), small flop c-bet (33% pot, consistent with wide continuation range including Ax weak kickers, heart draws like KhQh/JhTh/9h7h, floats, bluffs), check-turn behind OOP check (polarized: strong hands trapping/checking value, medium strength pot control, missed draws giving up). OOP first to act on scary river into deep-stacked button aggressor (6975 eff). Value betting thin: gets called/folded to by flushes/sets/two-pair, but risks check-raise from monsters; worse Ax (A7-A9, suited) or bluffs less likely to call raise. Raising isolates vs possible hero calls unnecessary. Checking optimal: induces bluffs/value bets from missed draws (e.g., stubborn flush draw chasers, overpairs fearing flush), allows cheap showdown vs his bluffs/medium Ax, pot controls vs better hands. History shows Flash bets light post-flop, checks behind medium strength; pot odds favor calling small-medium bets (~2-3x pot max we'd defend). Plan: check, call small bets (realize equity vs bluffs/worse), fold to large overbets (protect vs nuts). Maximizes EV long-term in tournament.

On the river, the board is Ac 6h 5h 6s Kh. I have Qd Jc for Queen-high. After my flop c-bet was called and the turn was checked through, the river King is an interesting card. However, Elon has shown a tendency to be sticky in previous hands. My check on the turn likely capped my range in his eyes, making a bluff on the river less believable. Since I have no showdown value but Elon's range contains many small pairs (5x, 77-TT) or weak Aces that he likely won't fold to a single river bet, I will check and concede the pot rather than risk more chips on a low-frequency bluff.

Button with J4o in a 3-handed pot — small investment to try to steal the blinds and play the flop in position. I’ll open to 3xBB (300) to get fold equity and present a credible range. J4o is marginal but playable in position; if I get a large 3‑bet I’ll fold and preserve my deep stack. If called I’ll use position to C‑bet favorable jack/high flops and control the pot on dry/low boards. 300 is a standard, sensible size (big enough to discourage light callers, small enough to keep risk manageable).

43s is a very weak hand, offsuit connector with low cards, no high card strength or suited playability against a button raise. Facing Minni's frequent button raises from history, this range is wide, and I'm OOP postflop. Pot odds are poor (calling 250 into 450 gives ~29% equity needed, but hand realizes low equity OOP). Folding preserves stack for better spots with 12200 effective. BB can defend if he wants.

In Hand #113, I am in the Big Blind with T5o. Minni has raised to 300 from the Button and Elon has folded. The pot is currently 450 and it costs me 200 more to call. Given my position and the hand strength of T5o, it's generally a weak hand to defend with, especially against a button raise from a large stack like Minni. While I am getting 2.25:1 on a call, T5o plays poorly post-flop and doesn't offer much equity. I'll stick to a more conservative strategy and fold.

3-handed on the button first to act pre-flop with T7o. Excellent position. Hand has straight potential with 10-7 and decent high-card value. In short-handed play, button opening range is very wide to steal blinds frequently. Opponents have shown aggression but also calling tendencies. Raising to 300 (3x BB) applies pressure, builds pot when ahead, and allows post-flop playability in position. Limping is suboptimal as it invites multi-way pots without initiative. Folding too tight given position and stacks.

I am in the Small Blind with 4d 3d, which is a speculative hand. Elon opened for 300 from the Button. While the hand has some post-flop potential as a suited connector, calling from the Small Blind puts me out of position for the rest of the hand against both the original raiser and potentially the Big Blind. Given my stack size is the shortest at the table (though still healthy at 68 BBs), I prefer to avoid marginal spots out of position with weak starting hands. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

2♦9♣ is a trash hand — offsuit, unconnected, poorly positioned to realize equity. Elon opened to 300 from the button (3x), Flash folded, so this is effectively heads-up. Calling would commit 200 more (300 total) to see a flop with a hand that is largely dominated and has little implied odds; a raise would be pure spew. With a healthy stack and many hands left, preserve chips for better spots. Fold.

I am on the button with J8o. While this is often an opening hand in a three-handed game, both Minni and Elon have been playing aggressively and frequently 3-betting or defending their blinds. My stack is the shortest at the table (approx. 68 BBs), and J8o is a marginal hand that doesn't play well against a 3-bet. I will choose to fold and wait for a better spot to preserve my stack as we approach the final 35 hands of the tournament.

Th4d is a marginal, unsuited, disconnected holding — not worth an isolation raise from the small blind and easily dominated. Pot odds are attractive here: calling 50 more to contest a 150 pot gives ~25% break-even equity, so completing to see a flop cheaply is the lowest-variance, highest-EV play. If Elon shows strength (raises/3-bets) I can fold out of position and avoid difficult postflop decisions; if we see a neutral or favorable flop I can continue with top pair, good draws, or use position dynamics. Action: complete to 100 total and re-evaluate on the flop.

Hand #115 pre-flop in BB with Jh8d offsuit. Flash folded on button, Minni limped from SB. Pot 200, to call 0. J8o has mediocre playability with straight potential but poor high card strength. Against a single limper like Minni (deep stacked at 20k+), checking to see a flop multiway or heads-up is standard and low-risk, preserving stack while gaining position post-flop if we play. Raising light (min 200) risks bloating pot OOP with a marginal hand vs an opponent who has shown aggression in history. With 12k stack, 115/150 hands, better to play pots in position or with stronger holdings. Check to realize equity cheaply.

Heads-up on a Qd Kh As rainbow flop I have T♥4♦ — no pair and no real draw. My only realistic outs are four Jacks to make the Broadway straight (≈8–9% to hit by the turn/river) and a remote two-card heart flush possibility, so equity is very low. Elon checked preflop and the pot is only 200; betting as a bluff here would be high-variance and will often put me in tough spots if called or raised. With deep stacks I should control the pot and avoid bloat — check and see how Elon responds. Plan: check now and fold to any meaningful bet; if Elon checks back we can reassess on the turn (only continue if we pick up a Jack/heart or a free card that improves our equity).

Flop is AKQ rainbow, a highly coordinated and draw-heavy board favoring strong made hands like top pair/top kicker (AK, AQ), two-pair (AK, KQ, AQ), sets, or straights (JT). My J8o is a complete airball: no pair, no flush draw (off-suit to board), weak gutshot backdoor (needs T9 essentially, unlikely). Equity is minimal (~10-20% vs limper range). Minni limped SB (wide range possible: suited connectors, Ax, Kx, broadway) and checked flop, which could be weakness (missed high cards entirely) or trap/induction. Betting here as OOP aggressor bloats pot with no showdown value or draw, risks stack (12k effective vs her 20k) against villain who calls stations light post-flop per history. Checking preserves stack, takes free card (board improving unlikely to help), allows pot control, and induces bluffs later streets if she weak. Multiway potential low since Flash folded. Fold equity unnecessary; check to realize thin equity cheaply and maneuver post-flop.

Board Qd Kh As Kc; I hold Th 4d. I have no pair and only four clean outs (the four Jacks) to make the Broadway straight on the river — roughly 8–9% (~4/46). There is no realistic flush draw and pairing the Ten on the river will often be hopeless versus Ax, Kx or Qx. Elon checked preflop and on the flop so his range is wide, but that range contains many hands (Kx trips, Ax for A+K two‑pair, Qx for K+Q two‑pair, and slowplays) that either crush me or will not fold to a turn bluff. Betting here would bloat the pot with very poor equity and risk a raise from a value hand. I will check to control the pot and preserve fold equity on the river — fold to any meaningful turn bet, and if Elon checks back I’ll re-evaluate the river only if I hit a Jack or otherwise improve.

Turn board pairs the kings (Qd Kh As Kc), creating possible full houses (AA, KK, QQ, AK), two-pair combos (AK, KQ), straights (JT, AT), and strong pairs. My Jh8d remains pure air: jack high, no pair, no flush draw, no straight draw (board too high). Equity <10% against Minni's SB limp-check-check range (wide: Ax suited, Kx, suited connectors, small pairs that missed). She's checked thrice, indicating possible weakness (missed flop entirely, trapping unlikely multiway pre with limp). Betting as OOP bluff (even small 125-150) risks check-raise from monsters or call from medium strength (Kx, Ax), bloating tiny 200 pot with 12k stack vulnerable deep vs her 20k. No fold equity needed; checking takes free river card (improvement unlikely but possible backdoor), controls pot, induces her bluffs/value bets on river for fold equity later, realizes thin showdown value (J high beats bluffs/missed draws). Consistent with prior pot control strategy; preserves stack for better spots with 35 hands left.

I hold T♥4♦ on Q♦ K♥ A♠ K♣ 5♦ — I missed completely. I have no pair and no meaningful river outs; any value hands (A×, K×, Q×) or even slowplayed strong hands in Elon’s checking range will rarely fold to a thin river bluff. The pot is tiny (200) relative to both stacks, so a bet would be high-variance and unlikely to be +EV: either it gets through infrequently or it gets called by better hands. I already planned to check and fold to any meaningful bet on earlier streets — stick to that. Action: check and conserve chips; fold if Elon leads out for a sizable amount.

River board Qd Kh As Kc 5d is extremely dry for new draws (5d adds gutshot possibilities but pairs kings prominently, favoring made hands like KK, AA, AK, KQ, AQ, straights JT/AT). Jh8d is pure jack-high bluff-catcher with no pair, straight, or flush. Minni's SB limp-check-check-check line suggests weakness (missed high cards, speculative hands like suited connectors/low pairs that bricked, or trap but unlikely after limp OOP). Pot only 200 vs my 12k stack and her 20k; betting any amount (e.g., 125-200 pot-sized bluff) risks check-raise from monsters or call from medium strength (Kx, Ax, Qx), with low fold equity on tiny pot after passive line. No value; checking realizes thin showdown equity (J-high beats her air/missed draws from wide limp range, per history of calling light speculatively). Pot control consistent all streets, preserves stack with 35 hands left in tournament.

Button with Q♦4♦ is a weak but suited hand — not worth limp/call passively every time, but in a 3‑handed spot I should be opening a wide range to pressure the blinds. Both opponents have shown frequent preflop aggression, but Flash (BB) is shorter and folds or calls reasonably often; Elon (SB) will 3‑bet sometimes and I can fold without much cost. A standard 3x open (300 total) gives good fold equity, keeps the pot manageable if called, and preserves position and post‑flop maneuverability with backdoor diamond play. Plan: fold to a sizable 3‑bet, call small defenses and c‑bet favorable, and play straightforwardly postflop — seize pots with position or fold to heavy resistance.

89s suited connectors from SB facing button raise to 300 (3x). Excellent pot odds: already posted 50, calling 250 more into 450 pot for ~2.8:1 direct odds, plus strong implied odds for flush/straight potential. Minni on button likely wide range. Flash in BB may call/raise, but multiway fine for speculative hand. Postflop playability high despite OOP. Folding too tight; 3bet sizing risky with non-premium hand. Standard call to realize equity and implied odds. Stacks deep enough (124BB effective).

I am holding Ace-Jack offsuit in the Big Blind. The button (Minni) raised to 3x, and the Small Blind (Elon) called. AJo is a very strong hand in this three-way configuration. Calling provides excellent pot odds (4.5:1), but playing a three-way pot out of position is difficult. By 3-betting, I can capitalize on the dead money in the pot, potentially win it immediately, or isolate one opponent. Given my stack size of approximately 67 big blinds, I will raise to 1200 to put pressure on the original raiser and the caller.

I opened wide from the button with Q♦4♦ to pressure the blinds, but Flash’s large 3‑bet to 1200 and Elon’s cold call turn this into a multiway spot. Q4♦ is frequently dominated by 3‑betting/flatting ranges (many Qx and broadways) and has poor equity multiway despite the diamond suit. Calling 900 more (1200 total) commits chips into a bloated pot with little fold equity and limited implied odds (effective stack is essentially Flash’s ~56BB). A 4‑bet bluff or shove here is too ambitious given table reads and stack dynamics. Preserve the stack and wait for a cleaner spot — fold.

89s is a premium suited connector with excellent playability, equity realization potential, and flush/straight outs. Already invested 300 preflop with great initial odds. Facing Flash's 4x 3bet to 1200 from BB after folding Minni out (tightens range somewhat to JJ+, AKo/s, maybe QQ/AQ occasionally). Pot 1800, calling additional 900 gives ~3:1 direct odds (25% equity needed); 89s has ~36-40% equity vs polarized 3bet range, plus massive implied odds given straight/flush potential on suited boards and Flash's aggressive history (frequent postflop betting from past hands). Effective stack ~44BB (Flash's remaining), but postflop dynamics favor calling OOP: can flop strong draws (open-enders, flush draws ~12-15% chance), pairs + backdoors. 4bet risks (min 2100 or shove vs shorter stack, lower EV realization); folding too tight for pot odds/equity. Call to build pot OOP with monster speculative equity, control pot size postflop if check-fold spots arise, exploit Flash's tendencies to overbet bluffs/value. Stack depths support (121BB effective but SPR ~2.5 post-call).

89s flops second pair (9s) with open-ended straight draw (8 outs to Broadway, ~16% turn/river), plus backdoor spade flush draw on a coordinated Qc9hTh board with two hearts. Strong made hand + powerful draw (~30-35% equity vs Flash's polarized BB 3bet range: QQ+, AK, suited Ax/connectors/bluffs). Acting first OOP postflop in 2700 pot vs shorter-stacked Flash (5625 remaining, ~2.1 SPR). Checking is optimal for pot control, equity realization, and inducing action from Flash's wide range (missed overpairs like KK/AA check back sometimes, draws/bluffs/A-high c-bet frequently per hand history). Betting risks: folds out worse (pure draws, Ax) while facing raises from superior hands (QT, Q9, T9, sets); check allows flexible response (raise bluffs/overpairs, call better value, check-fold if bricked weird turn). Deep effective stacks but low SPR favors seeing turn cheaply or building pot OOP profitably. History shows Flash aggressive postflop c-betting flops often. Goal: maximize EV by exploiting dynamics without overcommitting OOP prematurely.

On a flop of Qc-9h-Th, the board is extremely coordinated and connects very well with Elon's range for calling a pre-flop squeeze from the Small Blind (which often includes medium pairs, suited connectors, and broadway hands). My Ace-Jack gives me an open-ended straight draw (8 outs) and one overcard. While a continuation bet is standard for the pre-flop aggressor, betting into this specific board texture is risky because Elon is unlikely to fold any Queen, Ten, Nine, or significant draw. By checking back, I control the size of the pot, realize my equity for free, and avoid being forced to fold my draw to a large check-raise. If a King or Eight falls on the turn, I will have the nuts or a very strong hand to value bet.

Turn 4s bricks most of Flash's overpairs/value range (QQ+ often still best) but completes some wheel straight draws (56s) and adds spade flush draw, improving my 8s9s from second pair + backdoors to second pair + nut flush draw (8 outs, ~18% equity boost) + gutshot elements runner-runner. Combined ~45-50% equity vs his BB 3bet/check-back flop range (JJ+/AKs/AQs, missed draws like AhKh/JTs, medium pairs Ax suited that checked back). Pot 2700, his stack 5625 (~2.1 SPR), my larger stack allows flexibility. Acting first OOP: checking risks him checking back strong showdown (overpairs/sets) or betting draws/small pairs we beat but deny us value; induces bluffs but low SPR limits fold equity. Betting extracts value from his worse pairs (QJ/QT/T9/J9, Ax suited top pair), weaker draws (heart flush draws, Jxs spade draws), traps overpairs for calls/shoves. Size 1900 (~70% pot) builds to ~4600 pot, gets 1:1.4 if called, commits him near all-in (~3700 back, SPR<1 post), maximizes EV by charging draws/overpairs while protecting vs villainous floats. History: Flash calls stations big bets postflop (e.g., Hand 103 river call, Hand 84 etc.), rarely folds medium strength. No fold equity wasted; pure value/exploit dynamics to grow stack tournament-long.

I have an open-ended straight draw with Ace-high (Js Ah) on a board of Qc-9h-Th-4s. After the flop went check-check, Elon led into me on the turn for 1900 into a pot of 2700. To call, I need approximately 29% equity (1900/6500). With 8 clean outs to a straight (Kings and Eights), my probability of hitting on the river is roughly 17.4%. This is significantly below the required pot odds. While there are implied odds if I hit, the board is highly coordinated, making it likely that Elon will play cautiously on any straight-completing river card. Additionally, my Ace-high has very little showdown value against Elon's range, which likely includes strong Queens, Tens, or two-pair combinations that connect with this texture. Folding allows me to preserve my remaining 56 BBs for more favorable situations later in the tournament.

On the button with 9d7d suited connectors, a speculative hand that plays well postflop in position, especially multiway or against calls. Deep effective stacks, pot 150 with BB 100 to call. Standard play is to raise for value/thin value and fold equity against blinds. Flash in SB is shorter (55BB), Minni deep in BB. Raising to 3x (300) is standard sizing here to steal blinds or build pot. Folding or limping too passive on button.

I am holding 9s 8d in the Small Blind. Elon has opened from the Button for 3x. While 98o is a decent connector, playing it out of position against a Button open and a Big Blind who has been very active is suboptimal, especially as the shortest stack at the table. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity to commit my chips.

Elon opened to 300 from the button; I (BB) hold 7♣6♥. Calling 200 more (300 total) gives ~3.25:1 pot odds (~23.5% equity required) which is reasonable versus a standard button opening range. 7-6o has decent straight/overcard equity and with deep stacks I have good implied odds to realize that equity. I’m out of position so I don’t want to 3-bet light with a weak offsuit connector (poor blockers, difficult postflop spots). Folding would be too tight given the pot odds and my healthy stack. Plan: call 300 to defend the blind; on the flop I’ll proceed cautiously — fold to large aggression on dry boards, and continue or apply pressure when I hit pair/strong draw or favorable runouts.

Check. I’m out of position with 7♣6♥ on As 5s Jd — a very dry Ace‑high flop that heavily favors the opener’s range (lots of Ax, Js, spades). I have no pair, no spade and essentially only weak backdoor outs, so my immediate equity is minimal. With deep stacks the preflop call was fine for implied odds, but postflop I want pot control and to avoid getting bluffed off more chips. Checking keeps the pot small, lets Elon fire a c‑bet if he has one, and allows me to fold to meaningful pressure (or only call a very small c‑bet) — I won’t bluff‑raise here without blockers or a real draw.

Flop: As5sJd rainbow-ish but spade heavy draw. Holding 97dd, complete air: no pair, no strong draw (backdoor diamond flush and gutshot-ish playability). As preflop raiser on button vs BB caller's check, have strong range advantage. Minni's BB calling range is wide (suited connectors, Ax, Jx, broadways). C-bet for thin value against her weak Ax/Jx floats and pure fold equity vs draws/misses. Board favors our polarized raising range (sets, two-pair, flush draws, Ace/Jack). Deep stacks (140BB+), pot 650; bet 1/3 pot (around 200-250 too small, 350 solid for fold equity without overbetting). If called/faced raise, hand has fold equity postflop with backdoors.

The flop (As 5s Jd) heavily favors the raiser’s range (lots of Ax, Js and overcards). I have 7♣6♥ with no spade and only backdoor/weak pair outs. Pot was 1000; Elon bet 350 making the effective pot 1350 — calling 350 requires ~25.9% equity. I have ~6 immediate outs (≈24.1% to pair by the river), and when I do hit I’m often still behind to Ax/Jx or two‑pair/sets. I called preflop for implied odds but planned to fold to meaningful flop aggression on a dry Ace‑high board. This is not a spot to bluff-raise or peel with marginal backdoor equity — I’ll fold and preserve my deep stack for better spots.

I am on the button with Q2 suited. In a three-handed game, I should be looking for opportunities to open or play reasonably wide ranges to keep pace. However, Q2c is on the bottom edge of playable hands. With a stack of 5575 (about 55 big blinds), I have enough to be selective. Minni (SB) and Elon (BB) have much larger stacks and have been aggressive in previous hands. Folding here is a safe, disciplined play given the weak kicker and the likelihood of getting squeezed or outplayed post-flop by the larger stacks.

I'm in the small blind with K♦3♣ — a weak, offsuit hand that's hard to play out of position. Pot is 150 and it costs only 50 more to see a flop (min raise to 200 if I wanted to raise). I don't want to inflate the pot or face a 3-bet with this hand, and raising here is unnecessary and likely to be called/3-bet by a wide defending BB. Calling is a cheap option to realize equity and see how Elon reacts on the flop. Plan: fold to a meaningful preflop 3-bet; on the flop I'll fold to large C-bets when missed, and only continue if I pair the king/trey, pick up backdoor equity, or get a clear read/cheap showdown. Total commit will be 100 chips (my 50 SB + 50 call).

Facing a limp from the small blind (Minni), with A4h suited in the big blind. This is a speculative hand with flush and straight potential. Checking is optimal to realize equity on the flop cheaply, as we have position post-flop heads-up and deep effective stacks (mine 14,200 vs Minni's 20,025). Raising preflop risks bloating the pot with a marginal hand that plays poorly out of position if called or 3-bet. History shows Minni limps a wide range, often weak, making post-flop play profitable.

I missed the flop with K♢3♣ on T♠6♣8♠: no pair, no real draw (only a backdoor club draw). I’m out of position vs Elon (BB) and the pot is only 200 — there’s no reason to barrel with air and risk a raise or giving a free card to better hands. Check to control the pot size and to allow Elon to bluff or show weakness. Plan: fold to any meaningful bet (I have essentially 0 equity vs a typical betting range here), consider calling only an extremely small probe bet (very small bet that gives >20% pot odds), and if checked to showdown I’ll reevaluate on the turn (bet only if I improve or get a clear read).

Heads-up on flop after SB limp and check. Board Ts6c8s is somewhat draw-heavy with possible straights (79, J7, 97) and spade flush draw. Ah4h is ace-high with backdoor heart flush draw and negligible straight potential. Limp-call range from SB is wide and weak (low pairs, suited connectors, Ax offsuit/weak suited). Checking back controls the pot with marginal showdown value, avoids bloating pot OOP for her if she improves, and realizes equity cheaply in position (acting last). Betting thin value risks folding out worse hands (KQo, 87, etc.) or getting called/raised by better (sets, two-pair, draws). Preflop check was to play profitably post-flop multiway or heads-up, and checking flop aligns with that. Pot 200, deep stacks (14k eff), no need to force action now.

I hold K♦3♣ and have no pair or real draws on T♠6♣8♠A♦. Pot is only 200 and I’m out of position vs Elon, who’s been passive (checked preflop and on the flop). This turn is exactly the type of card I don’t want to bluff wildly — betting is a thin bluff that’s likely to be called or raised by a better hand. I’ll check to control the pot and preserve my stack. Plan: fold to any meaningful bet (only consider calling a very tiny probe that gives good pot odds); if Elon checks back I’ll re-evaluate on the river.

Turn pairs our Ace, giving top pair top kicker (TPTK) against Minni's wide SB limp-check-check range, which includes worse Ax (A7-A9, suited), mid pairs (77-99, QQ-JJ possible but unlikely to check twice), busted draws, and weak broadway. Board remains draw-heavy (spade flush draw, OESD with 79/J7/97), so betting denies equity to draws and charges them. Double-check from villain suggests capped range without strong hands like sets (unlikely from limp) or two-pair. Position allows us to bet for thin value from worse and protection, building pot OOP for her on river if checked back. Deep effective stacks (~14k), low SPR favors realizing equity profitably. Bet ~3/4 pot for value/protection, sizing 150 fits aggression patterns seen in history.

I’m out of position with K♦3♣ on T♠6♣8♠ / A♦. Elon checked the flop and then leads the turn for 150 into a 350 pot. To call 150 I need roughly 30% equity (150/(350+150)=30%). I have essentially no made hand and only 6 clean outs (3 kings + 3 treys) to improve on the river → ~6/46 ≈ 13% chance. Even hitting a king often won’t be good enough versus a bet/raise-heavy range that includes A‑x, tens, or other hands that he’d value-bet this turn. Because I’m out of position, have almost no fold equity, and this is a meaningful bet relative to the pot, calling is -EV and a bluff-raise would be too thin. I’ll fold and preserve my deep stack for better, higher-expected-value spots.

Button vs blinds, 8♠5♣ is a weak offsuit holding but in 3-handed play the button should open a very wide stealing range. Stacks are deep (200+ BB) so I can apply pressure without committing myself. A standard raise to 300 (3xBB) is the right play: it gives good fold equity against the blinds, is consistent with table sizing, and keeps the pot controllable with a marginal hand. Plan: fold to a substantial 3-bet, otherwise play the flop in position and use c-bets / position to pick up the pot or realize equity.

J4 suited is a speculative hand with flush and straight potential. Minni's button raise is likely wide given position. Pot odds are favorable: calling 250 more into 450 (pot will be 700 post-call) offers about 2.8:1 direct odds, plus strong implied odds if we hit. SB defend range includes low suited connectors and gappers like this vs button opens. Flash's BB stack is 55BB and may call or raise, potentially creating a multiway pot where suited hands realize equity well despite OOP position. Folding wastes equity; 3-betting too bluff-heavy with this hand. Plan to float or play post-flop aggressively if we improve.

I am in the Big Blind holding K8o. Minni (Button) raised to 300, and Elon (Small Blind) called. The pot is currently 700, and it costs me 200 more to call. This gives me pot odds of 3.5:1. While K8o is a mediocre hand, getting such a good price to see a flop in a three-way pot while closing the action is a standard call in a tournament setting, especially with 55 big blinds.

Flop board As6h2c is dry and ace-high, favoring strong ace ranges from opponents, especially Minni's button open which can include Ax, pairs, and bluffs. My Jd4d has jack-high with only a weak backdoor flush draw and no straight or pair potential, giving minimal equity multiway OOP. Pre-flop call was speculative for implied odds, but we missed completely. Betting air OOP multiway risks getting raised by better hands or floats. Checking is standard: pot control, realize fold equity if checked through, or fold to bets without wasting chips. No value or semi-bluff here; stacks are deep enough that folding is cheap long-term.

On this Ace-high board, I have missed completely with King-high. I am out of position against the pre-flop raiser (Minni) and have the Small Blind (Elon) still to act behind me in the sense of the betting order for the street. Leading into two opponents on a board that hits many calling and raising ranges is unwise. Checking is the standard play here to see what the initial aggressor does.

I opened wide from the button preflop and both callers checked the Ace-6-2 flop — a clear opportunity to apply pressure. My hand (8♠5♣) has no pair and no realistic draw (a five-card flush is impossible and straights are runner-runner), so this is purely a continuation-bet bluff. A small bet (~1/3 pot) maximizes fold equity versus their checking ranges while keeping the pot controllable versus deeper stacks (Elon 14,100; Flash 5,275). Plan: bet 300 to try to take it down now; fold to a significant raise, and if called proceed cautiously on the turn (check or pot-control depending on the card).

Minni's flop bet of 300 into 1200 after two checks is a standard continuation with a wide range from button, including value hands like Ax, pairs, sets, and some bluffs or probes. My Jd4d has complete air: jack-high no pair, no realistic draw (weak backdoor diamond flush), and poor playability OOP multiway. Calling 300 gets ~4:1 direct odds but builds pot OOP with minimal equity (~10-20% vs typical ranges), facing further aggression from Minni or Flash, and no implied odds since I have nothing to realize. Raising is a pure bluff multiway with short stack in BB behind and deep opponents likely to call/raise light. Folding is optimal: pot control achieved by checking, now realize equity by folding trash cheaply to preserve 141BB stack for better spots in high-roller deepstack play over remaining 31 hands.

On this Ace-high board, I have missed completely. Minni, the pre-flop aggressor, has followed up with a 1/3 pot continuation bet after Elon checked and folded. While the pot odds are 4:1, King-high has very little showdown value here and almost no equity against a range that includes many Aces and pocket pairs. Being out of position with no draw or pair makes this a clear fold to preserve my stack.

On the button with Kc7c, a suited king with good playability and position on both blinds. Button range is wide preflop, especially against blinds. K7s has decent equity multiway and blocks top of range. Raise to standard 3x BB size (300 total) to attempt a steal or get value thin/see flops in position. SB is relatively short (52BB), BB deep; raising applies pressure. Calling risks multiway pot OOP for blinds, folding too tight given position.

I have pocket nines in the Small Blind, which is a very strong hand in three-handed play. Elon opened from the Button, where his range is likely wide. Calling allows the Big Blind to enter the pot with great odds and leaves me playing a medium-strength hand out of position. By three-betting to 1000, I take the initiative, put pressure on the Button, and aim to win the pot immediately or isolate Elon while holding a range advantage.

I have Q♦7♠ (offsuit) in the BB facing a BTN open to 300 and an SB 3-bet to 1000. Calling 900 (to make my total 1000) would put ~2300 in the pot, so I need ~39% equity to break even. Against a 3-bet range from the SB and a BTN opener, Q7o is routinely dominated (AQ/KQ/Qx, pocket pairs, broadways) and my multiway equity will be well below that. A min-4-bet to 1700 is a poor bluff here — it won’t credibly fold out either opponent and risks a shove or a call where I’m a big dog. Flash still has ~3.3k behind and can jam or call, and Elon can easily fold/continue. With two aggressive players and a weak, offsuit queen, the +EV play is to conserve chips and fold.

Preflop on the button with Kc7c after my open-raise to 300. Flash 3-bets to 1000 from SB after BB folds, pot 1400, facing 700 more (total 1000 from me). K7s suited is strong enough to continue vs SB 3-bet range: blocks KK/AA, has straight/flush potential, high card value, and excellent playability in position heads-up. Flash has been aggressive historically (frequent raises, 3-bets, postflop bets), suggesting a polarized range with bluffs/value. Folding is too tight from button after opening; raising risks bloating pot OOP for opponent with shorter stack (4275 total), better to realize equity cheaply postflop where position + hand dynamics shine (can fold to c-bet if miss, stack off with hits). Pot odds favorable (~3:1 effective), deep implied odds vs shorter stack. Call to see flop in position.

I am the pre-flop aggressor after 3-betting from the Small Blind with pocket nines. The flop comes Queen-high with two low cards. While there is one overcard to my pair, my hand remains relatively strong in a three-handed game. I will lead with a continuation bet of 750 (approximately 35% of the pot) to maintain initiative, represent a strong range (like AQ, KK, or AA), and protect against potential overcards or draws. If Elon calls, I will proceed with caution on future streets.

Flop Qh5d2h is somewhat coordinated with two hearts to the queen, but relatively dry for straights. Facing a 750 bet into ~2100 pot (~36% pot size) from Flash's SB 3-bet range after my BTN open-call. K7c suited clubs missed completely: king-high no pair, no straight draw (gap too big), backdoor club flush draw. However, position on aggressive SB opponent (history of frequent aggression, continuation betting), favorable pot odds (~2850:750 offers ~21% equity break-even, and K7s likely realizes 25-35% vs polarized 3-bet range including bluffs like suited aces, broadways, connectors). Flash's effective stack ~3525 (35BB), committed ~1750 already, so turn decisions straightforward: fold to overbet if blank/bad, take stab if checks/he checks back, stack off only if improves. Raising as bluff risks commitment from shorter opponent and bloating pot OOP for him; check-fold too passive given dynamics. Call to realize equity cheaply in position, control pot, exploit potential overvaluing by opponent.

The Ace on the turn is a significant scare card. Although I represented a strong range by 3-betting pre-flop and continuation betting on the flop, the Ace now makes it much harder for pocket nines to be the best hand if Elon has any piece of the board or a drawing hand like AX. Given that Elon called both the 3-bet and the flop bet, his range likely contains Queens or Aces. Checking here is the best option to control the size of the pot and potentially see a showdown if he checks back, or to fold if he makes a significant bet, as my hand is now strictly a bluff-catcher against a range that likely improved or was already ahead.

On the turn with Kc7c (king-high, backdoor club draw now with Ac on board, but no made hand or strong draw), facing Flash's check after his flop c-bet into a coordinated board (Qh5d2h flop with heart flush draw and low straight potential). Preflop: Flash 3-bet my button open from SB (polarized range: QQ+/AK suited broadways, some suited connectors/bluffs). Flop bet ~36% pot suggests value (Qx, sets, combo draws) or bluff. My calls pre/flop realized equity in position vs aggressive SB range (K7s blocks KK/AA, plays well postflop). Turn Ac changes little for me (rainbow board now, blocks AA/AQ), improves some straight draws (e.g., 43), Flash checks (possibly weak value like Qx without heart, missed draw, or trap with strong hand). Pot 3600, Flash eff 3525 (~1:1 SPR), my deep stack 12350. Betting as bluff risks short-stack shove/call from better (high fold equity but poor if called, as king-high rarely best); thin value bet unnecessary with no showdown edge over pairs/draws. Checking back controls pot, denies equity to pure floats/bluffs, sees river free in position (cheap if villain bets weak, fold if shove/overbet; can value bet/br if improves to 2-pair/straight/flush). Exploits Flash's history of aggression (frequent cbets but checks/folds postflop sometimes), preserves stack late tournament (hand 120/150, ICM pressure with 3 left, me chip leader). Optimal: check to realize equity/showdown cheaply.

On the river, the nine gives me a set of nines, which is extremely strong. Given the pre-flop 3-bet and the flop continuation bet, my range remains strong, but checking the turn on an Ace-high card likely signaled weakness or pot control to Elon. Elon's check-back on the turn suggests he has a medium-strength hand like a Queen or a weak Ace that he is willing to take to showdown but didn't want to bet into my perceived range. To maximize value, I will lead the river with a substantial bet of 2200 into the 3600 pot. This sizing is designed to look like a polarized attempt to push him off a one-pair hand or a 'thin' value bet, inducing a call from his Queens or Aces.

Facing a 2200 bet into ~3500 pot on river (pot now 5700, calling 2200 for ~22% equity needed) with Kc7c king-high no pair/draw on Qh5d2hAc9c board. Flash's line (SB 3bet pre vs BTN open, flop c-bet on coordinated board, turn check after Ac, river large bet 63% pot leaving 1325 ~38% pot behind) is strong and polarized to value (Qx like AQ/KQ/Q9s, Ax pair A, sets 55/22/QQ, possible club flush with clubs from pre suited, thin two-pair) or thin bluffs (missed heart/SD like 43/Axs weak check-call). However, turn check suggests not monster/draw heavy (traps or weak), but river large bet with short stack screams value/overvalue rather than bluff (short stack bluffs usually shove all-in earlier or smaller). King-high crushed by value range (loses to any Q/A/pair 88+), live vs bluffs but bluff range thinned by sizing/line/history of aggression but value-heavy late. Pot odds good but <22% equity vs range (king-high ~15-20% at best vs polarized bet, blocks AK/KQ help slightly). As second stack (12350, Minni 20k+, Flash short 3525 pre-bet), 30 hands left 3-handed, ICM/fold equity preservation > speculative call risking ~18% stack for low EV spot. Consistent with prior plan: check to realize equity cheaply, fold to bet. Fold preserves stack for better spots.

I am on the button with Qd 8h. In a three-handed game, Q8o is a strong enough hand to open for a raise to steal the blinds or play a pot in position. I have a healthy stack of over 70 big blinds, and a standard 3x open has been my consistent sizing throughout the match. This pressure often forces the blinds to fold marginal hands, and I can evaluate further based on their reaction.

3-handed vs a BTN open to 300 and I’m in the SB with K♠9♠. This is a defendable hand — suited K with decent playability and blocking some strong Kx combos. I’m deep (≈204BB) and Flash has ≈68BB. 3-betting is an option, but I’ll be out of position postflop and a tiny reraise to 500 won’t accomplish much while a larger 3-bet bloats the pot OOP. I’ll complete to 300 (call) to see a flop, keep Flash’s range wide, and play postflop: c‑bet when I connect or have good equity, otherwise proceed cautiously vs aggression.

J♥7♠ is a weak offsuit hand with poor playability post-flop, especially out of position in the big blind facing a button raise and small blind call. The pot odds are about 3.5:1 (200 to call into 700), but the hand has low equity against likely calling ranges and cannot realize it well multi-way or heads-up OOP. Folding preserves stack for better spots in this deep-stack tournament, avoiding speculative defense with trash.

Flop: 5♠ A♠ 2♥. I hold K♠9♠ — the nut flush draw (9 spade outs) plus a respectable K overcard (3 king outs). By the river I’m ~35% to make the flush and roughly ~47% to at least pair or make a flush, so I have strong drawing equity. I’m first to act; this is a great spot to semi-bluff and get folds from non‑spade broadways and small pairs while charging other draws. I’ll bet about half the pot to balance fold equity and pot control against Flash’s ~68BB stack. Plan: take the pot if he folds; if he calls I’ll continue with good equity and re-evaluate on the turn; if he raises large I’ll give credit to a made Ace/set and fold (or fold to very large aggression), but will call reasonable-sized raises to realize equity.

I opened pre-flop with Qd 8h and was called by Minni in the Small Blind. The flop comes As 5s 2h, which is quite favorable for the big blind's range or the caller's range if they have an Ace. Minni leads out (donk bets) for 350 into a 700 pot. With only Queen high and no significant draws on this Ace-high board, I have very little equity. Continuing would require a pure bluff, and since Minni is the chip leader and has shown aggression, it is more prudent to fold and wait for a better opportunity than to float or raise with nothing.

On the button with J♦4♣ — a weak hand but excellent position and deep stacks (≈209 BB). Facing only the blinds I’ll attempt a standard button steal: raise to the minimum allowed (200) to pick up the blinds or take the initiative post‑flop. This keeps the pot small while giving me fold equity; I won’t bloat the pot with a marginal hand. Plan: fold to a sizable 3‑bet, call small resistance and play aggressively in position on favourable, dry flops (or c‑bet to seize the pot), and exercise pot control / fold to strong action on wet or connected boards.

Preflop in SB with 3s4h offsuit, a very weak hand with poor playability and low equity against Minni's button raise range, which is wide but still favors folding. Calling 150 OOP into a 350 pot gives poor implied odds and sets up difficult postflop spots. Folding preserves stack for better opportunities, aligning with long-term maximization in late tournament stages.

I am in the Big Blind holding 9s 2h, which is one of the weakest hands in Texas Hold'em. Minni, the chip leader, has raised from the button. While the pot odds are attractive (100 to call into a 350 pot, roughly 4.5:1), the playability of 92o out of position is extremely poor. As the shortest stack at the table, I should focus on preserving my chips for more favorable spots rather than calling with marginal-to-worthless hands in a situation where I will likely be forced to fold on the flop if I don't hit it perfectly. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

3-handed on the button with Ks8c. Excellent position postflop. K8o is a strong open from BTN in short-handed play: king-high with decent equity, plays well multiway or heads-up, and blocks opponent king hands. Standard open-raise sizing of 3x BB (300 total) to build pot, take initiative, and respect pot odds. Opponents have shown wide ranges and aggression from blinds in history, but position advantage justifies raising. Avoid limping to prevent multiway pots without initiative. Folding too tight 3-handed.

I have Jc 4d in the small blind. The button (Elon) has raised to 3x the big blind. This hand is significantly below the average strength required to call or 3-bet from the small blind, especially out of position against a stack like Elon's and with a chip leader like Minni in the big blind. Folding is the standard and correct play here to preserve my stack.

I have Q♥T♥ in the big blind facing a standard BTN open to 300. QTs is a strong, playable heads-up hand — good blockers, straight/flush potential and strong postflop playability. The pot is 450 and calling 200 more (300 total) gives me ~30.8% break-even equity; against Elon's opening range QTs typically has >= that and plenty of implied odds with both stacks deep (my 20.9k vs his 11.9k). A 3-bet is an option, but a tiny 3-bet to 500 is weak and a larger 3-bet would commit too much with a speculative hand. I’ll call to see a flop and play position/head-up postflop: continue with top pair/strong draws, use backdoor equity and blocker effects to apply pressure when appropriate, and fold to concentrated aggression on highly unfavorable textures.

Check. I have Q♥T♥ on 7♥ 8♠ 8♦: no pair, two overcards and only weak backdoor equity (runner‑runner heart or runner‑runner straight). I’m out of position vs the BTN opener and the pot is already 650 with deep effective stacks (Elon ~11.9k → SPR ≈ 18), so I don’t want to inflate the pot with a marginal hand. Checking controls the pot, lets me realize my equity, and keeps a lot of Elon’s bluffing range in.
