Plan: if Elon cbets a small size (roughly ≤1/3 pot ≈ ≤215) I’ll call to see a turn (I have ~24% to pair by river plus backdoor outs and some implied odds). If he bets big or raises I’ll fold unless I improve. If he checks back I’ll reevaluate on the turn and potentially barreL or take a free card on many runout textures (bet/raise only if I pair or pick up strong draw).

Flopped top trips (888 with K kicker) on a paired 7h-8s-8d board after raising preflop from BTN and getting called by BB. Minni checks, which is standard but could represent weakness or trapping with a monster (unlikely given preflop call range). Board has some straight draw potential (56, 9T) and backdoor flushes, but no strong draws completed. My hand is way ahead of villain's calling range: overpairs, weaker pairs, Ax8, 87, etc. Value bet 2/3 pot (450 into 650) to get called by worse (pairs, two-pair like 87/78), build pot, charge draws, and maintain initiative in position. Avoid checking to induce bluffs or keep pot small; betting extracts max value from one pair types that Minni check-calls frequently per history. Oversizing risks folding out callers; undersizing misses value. If raised, can re-eval shove stacks.

I’m out of position with Q♥T♥ on 7♥ 8♠ 8♦ and Elon (BTN) has bet 450. The pot shows 1100 and my call would be 450, so break‑even equity ≈ 450/(1100+450) ≈ 29%. My realistic equity to improve to at least a pair by the river is ~24% (6 outs to pair) and the runner‑runner heart flush adds only ~4% more — combined ~28% at best, which is slightly below the break‑even threshold. Additionally Elon’s large sizing on a paired board polarizes his range toward strong value (sets, 7x, overpairs) or a committed polar bluff; either way I’ll be playing a big pot out of position with a marginal, often dominated hand. Raising as a bluff is too thin here given his line and stack sizes. I’ll fold and preserve my deeper stack for better spots.

I am on the button with 8s 6c. It is a weak hand, but I am in position. The blinds are 50/100, and it's a 3-handed game. Usually, a button raise is standard to put pressure on the blinds. However, given my stack size relative to the others and the marginal nature of the hand, I have the option to fold or raise. Minni and Elon have been fairly active. Raising 8-high offsuit is a bit loose even for a button open in a 3-handed game when facing larger stacks. I will fold to preserve chips for better spots as we approach the final hands of the tournament.

I'm in the small blind with 3♦T♥ — a marginal, unsuited hand. The pot is 150 and it only costs 50 more to call (bringing my total committed to 100), so the price to see a flop is attractive. I don't want to min-raise with such a weak holding and risk a 3-bet from Elon with deep stacks, so I'll take a cheap flop and evaluate. Plan: call, play cautiously out of position — fold to heavy pressure if I miss the flop, continue only with a pair, two pair, good backdoor equity, or a clear read.

Preflop in BB vs SB limp (Minni called 50, Flash folded on button), pot ~150-200. Held QhTc offsuit, decent queen-high hand with straight potential. SB limp range is extremely wide (any two cards, suited connectors, Ax, etc.) in short-handed play with 3 players left. Raising isolates the weak limper, takes down dead money often, and QTo has good equity domination over much of their range. OOP postflop is a concern but preflop steal value + equity makes raise profitable. Standard sizing ~3x BB into limped pot. Deep stacks (mine 12k+, theirs 20k+), but near end of 150 hands, build stack by exploiting limps. Min raise 200 ok, but pot-build to 300.

I completed the small blind with T♥3♦ to see a flop, but Elon (BB) has now raised to 300. The pot is 400 and it costs me 200 more to call (min-raise to 500). T3o is a very weak, easily dominated hand out of position with poor equity vs a raiser’s likely range (broadways/pairs). Pot odds (~3:1) don’t make up for the fact my realized equity is well below the ~25% needed and I’ll face significant post‑flop pressure. With a deep stack and only three players, I can wait for a much better spot. Fold and preserve my stack.

I'm on the button in a 3-handed pot with K♦9♠. This is a standard spot to open-raise — I have positional advantage and can steal the blinds or play post-flop with initiative. I choose a 3x BB sizing (300 total) to apply pressure without bloating the pot: it folds out weak holdings from the blinds, gets value from worse kings and medium pairs, and keeps post-flop decisions comfortable in position. Plan: if called I'll continuation-bet favorable K-high or dry boards and control the pot on coordinated boards; if I face a sizable 3-bet I'll fold K9o and preserve my stack.

Jh7d is a weak offsuit hand with poor playability and dominated by my opponent's likely button opening range, which includes all better jacks, tens, suited connectors, and broadway cards. Facing a 3x raise from the button in the small blind, I'm out of position postflop, where this hand performs poorly even if I hit. Pot odds to call 250 into 450 are marginal (~1.8:1), but the implied odds are insufficient given the multiway potential with BB behind and my weak equity. Folding preserves my healthy 126x BB stack for better spots in the late stages of the tournament.

I am in the Big Blind with Qs Jd, which is a strong broadway hand in a 3-handed game. Minni, on the button, has raised to 300. With a stack of over 60 big blinds, I have plenty of room to play post-flop. Calling the additional 200 to see a flop is a standard defense against a button open, especially since QJo is well ahead of a typical button opening range. While 3-betting is an option, calling allows me to keep the pot manageable while out of position.

I have flopped top pair with a strong kicker (Queen) on a Jack-Ten-Seven rainbow board. In a heads-up pot against the button raiser, checking to the aggressor is the standard and most balanced play. This allows me to see if Minni continues her aggression or takes a free card, while also protecting my checking range. My hand is strong enough to check-call a standard continuation bet or even consider a check-raise depending on sizing and board texture development.

I opened preflop from the button with K♦9♠ and got one caller from Flash in the big blind. The flop T♦ J♠ 7♣ is relatively dry and not hugely draw-heavy; as the preflop aggressor I have initiative and should c-bet a high frequency. K9 has one overcard (K) and a diamond blocker but is weak for showdown, so I want to use fold equity to take the pot or force weaker hands to pay to see turns. I'll make a standard ~50% pot continuation bet (325 into 650) — big enough to apply pressure but not to overcommit — and plan to fold to a sizable check‑raise or shove. If called, I'll proceed cautiously on the turn and reevaluate based on the card and opponent action.

I have flopped top pair with a Queen kicker (TPGK) on a somewhat coordinated board. In a three-handed game, this is a very strong hand. After checking to Minni, she made a continuation bet of 325 into a pot of 650. This is a standard half-pot bet. While I could raise for protection against straight draws (like 98 or KQ), check-calling is generally more effective here to keep her bluffs in and control the size of the pot while out of position. My stack of approximately 64 big blinds allows for post-flop maneuvering, and QJ is too strong to fold to a single bet. I will call and re-evaluate on the turn.

On the turn, the board pairs with the Seven of Hearts. I have top pair (Jacks) with a Queen kicker. In this situation, acting first, I will check to Minni who has been the aggressor throughout the hand. This allows me to control the size of the pot and evaluate her reaction to the board pairing. My hand is strong, but not strong enough to start leading out into the pre-flop and flop raiser on a coordinated board.

I check. After c-betting the flop I was called and the turn pairs the board (7♥) — that meaningfully increases the number of hands in Flash’s calling range that now beat me (7x, trips, and still-made straights like 9‑8). I have K♦9♠ with no pair and very little showdown equity, and Flash’s check on the turn looks like pot control or a slowplay rather than a clear fold to a second barrel. My fold equity is low vs a caller on the flop, so barreling the turn risks bloating the pot with an inferior hand or inducing a check‑raise I don’t want to face. Checking controls the pot (current pot 1,300), keeps my stack intact, and lets me re-evaluate on the river — I can fold to a meaningful turn check‑raise or a strong river bet, call small river bluffs if sizing and line match, or value-bet only if I pair my king on the river. Overall checking is the prudent, +EV choice here to avoid unnecessary risk with K9o in position.

I have top pair (Jacks) with a Queen kicker on a board of Td-Js-7c-7h-8c. The turn check from Minni after her flop continuation bet suggests she might be pot-controlling with a medium-strength hand or has a draw. However, the 8 on the river is a dangerous card because any 9 now makes a straight (7-8-9-T-J). Given the straight possibilities and the fact that I am out of position, checking is the most prudent play. This allows me to see how Minni reacts; if she checks back, I likely have the best hand against her Tens or missed broadway draws. If she bets, I can decide whether to call based on the sizing, but leading out myself risks running into a straight or trips.

The river 8c gives me a concealed straight: with K♦9♠ I now make 7-8-9-10-J. Opponent (Flash) called my flop c-bet then checked the turn and checked the river — a line consistent with medium-strength hands (T, J, 8, single-pair holdings) or missed draws/pot-control. Very few hands beat me here (quads/full houses from very specific combos, or Q9-type combos), and many of those beating combos would have often acted earlier. Betting for thin value is the right play: a small-to-medium bet will get called by worse pairs and J/T/8 hands while keeping the price reasonable if I face resistance. I'll bet roughly half the pot (650 into 1300) to extract value without overcommitting; if Flash check‑raises big I’ll strongly consider that he has a full house/quads and will proceed cautiously (fold to shove, evaluate small raises).

I have top pair with a Queen kicker (Qs Jd) on a board of Td-Js-7c-7h-8c. In this 3-handed match, this is a relatively strong holding. After I called Minni's pre-flop raise and her flop continuation bet, she checked back on the turn when the board paired (7h). Her check on the turn often suggests she has a marginal made hand (like a Ten or a weaker Jack) or a draw that she's pot-controlling. The 8 on the river is a coordinated card that completes a straight for any 9, but it's not a card that should automatically force me to fold top pair, especially given the pot odds. Minni is betting 650 into a 1300 pot (a 50% pot bet), giving me 3:1 odds on a call. Given my position and the strength of a Jack in a short-handed game, I only need to be right 25% of the time. While she could have a 7 or a 9, she could also be value betting a weaker Jack or attempting a thin bluff with missed broadway cards like KQ or AK. I will call to see the showdown.

7s2c is one of the worst hands in poker, with no high card strength, poor connectivity, and offsuit. Preflop on the button facing a small bet to call 100 into 150 (pot odds ~2:1), but the hand has negligible equity against any calling range and terrible postflop playability. Opponents have large stacks, and with only 25 hands left, folding trash preserves my 12.6k stack for premium spots in this 3-handed tournament.

With 6c 3s in the Small Blind and the Button folding, I am facing a 50-chip completion to see a flop against Minni, who has a significantly larger stack. 6-3 offsuit is one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em and generally does not play well out of position, even in a blind-vs-blind scenario. Given Minni's active and aggressive history in previous hands, I expect to be put under pressure post-flop or potentially raised immediately if I call. I will fold and wait for a better spot to preserve my stack.

I am on the button with 8s2d, which is an extremely weak hand. The stack sizes of my opponents, Minni and Elon, are significantly larger than mine, and there is no strategic reason to enter a pot with such a weak hand when I can wait for better opportunities to improve my position in the remaining hands of the tournament.

Heads-up from the small blind (button postflop) with Q♦2♠ — a marginal hand but worth opening because I have position after the flop and stacks are deep. Pot is 150 and it only costs 50 to complete, but limp/call surrenders initiative and lets the BB see a cheap flop. I’ll raise to 300 total to apply pressure and try to pick up the pot; this sizing is standard and gives decent fold equity. Plan: fold to a large 3-bet (Q2o is not a 3-bet caller), and if called play postflop aggressively on dry/high-card boards (c-bet with queen-showdown value) and control the pot or release to heavy resistance on coordinated/drawing boards.

Facing a standard SB raise to 300 after BTN folds. Ac7s is a strong suited ace suited hand for defending BB vs wide SB range. Excellent postflop playability: blocks AA/AK/AA, dominates weaker Ax, nut flush/straight potential. Pot odds: calling 200 more into 400 (pot becomes 600), laying ~1:2 getting 3:1 direct + implied. Deep stacks (125BB eff), realize equity OOP heads-up. Folding too tight against wide SB steal attempt (Minni raises frequently from early pos per history). 3betting marginal, better to call and play pot. Goal: maximize stack long-term by playing profitable spots.

I raised preflop with Q2o to seize initiative, but this flop (9h 3h 4h) is hostile to my holding. It’s a monotone heart board that heavily favors the BB’s calling range (Ax hearts, Kx hearts, suited connectors and many two‑card hearts) and I have no hearts, no pair and almost no backdoor equity. A standard c‑bet here would be a low‑equity bluff that will get called too often. With position and a weak hand, I prefer to control the pot and gather information — check and let Elon act. Plan: if Elon checks behind I take a free turn and reassess (can barrel a safe turn card like a queen); if he bets small (≈1/3 pot ≲200) I may call to see the turn, but I’ll fold to medium/large bets (>~1/2 pot ≳300) or to any raise, since I have no showdown value or blockers.

Flop board 9h3h4h is extremely wet: three to the flush, open-ended straight draws (52, 25, etc.), wheel draws. Ac7s has zero equity here - no pair, no draw, ace-high is crushed by any pair or draw. Minni's SB raise pre-flop is wide, but her check suggests weakness or trapping with a draw/monster. OOP with pure air, betting is disastrous: gets called by all draws/pairs and OOP we realize very little. Checking preserves stack, takes free card or induces bluffs on turn/river. Pot control critical; realize we defended wide pre-flop but fold equity post-flop by checking. Long-term EV positive to check-fold air here vs aggressive SB.

Board: 9h 3h 4h 6d. I hold Q♦2♠ — no hearts, no pair, virtually no showdown equity. I was the preflop aggressor but I checked the flop because this is a three‑heart monotone board that heavily favors the caller’s range (Ax/ Kx hearts, suited connectors, any two hearts, small pairs). The turn (6♦) is a blank for my hand and doesn’t remove many of Elon’s continuing holdings. Betting here would be a pure bluff with very little fold equity (I don’t block hearts and he can profitably call with any heart or a weak pair), so I don’t want to bloat the pot out of position or face a raise. I’ll check and control the pot, see the river, and only attempt a bluff on a credible card (e.g. a Q or another overcard that fits my perceived range) or fold to aggression. Preserves stack and keeps better spots for extraction later.

Turn board 9h3h4h6d is highly coordinated: nut flush draw still live, multiple straight draws completed (e.g., 2-5, 5-2), wheel draw possibilities, plus pairs improved. Ac7s remains pure ace-high with no pair, no draw, no blocker to strong hands beyond Ac. Minni's double check (flop and turn) from SB after wide preflop raise suggests caution with marginal draws/weak pairs, avoiding betting into OOP player on scary board, or trapping monster. OOP with air: betting induces calls from better and realizes no equity; raising pointless. Checking realizes hand value (likely free river or showdown if checked through), controls pot, preserves stack for better spots. Long-term EV: disciplined check-fold equity realization in tough BB defenses maximizes stack vs aggressive SB.

I have Q♦2♠ on a 9♥-3♥-4♥-6♦-8♣ board. I checked flop and turn because this is a three‑heart monotone board that heavily favors the caller’s range and I had no hearts, pair, or meaningful equity. The river 8♣ is a blank for my holding — it doesn’t credibly add a bluffing story I planned to use (I wanted a Q or another overcard to represent). Betting here would be a pure bluff with very little fold equity (Elon can profitably call with any heart, small pair, or many high cards), so the +EV play is to conserve chips and check for showdown. If Elon now bets, I’ll fold to significant pressure given I have no showdown value.

River board 9h3h4h6d8c is draw-heavy with completed flushes (hearts), straights (e.g., 5x7x for 3-4-5-6-7, 7-T incomplete but possible two-pair+), and pair+ hands. Ac7s is pure ace-high, no pair/draw, blocks nuts minimally (Ac). Minni's preflop SB minraise (wide range: Ax suited, broadway, suited connectors), triple check on scary board indicates weakness (missed draws like KhQh/JhTh/5h2h, weak pairs 77-88/JJ unimproved fearing better, air). Monsters (fh/sf/set) unlikely to check through IP; she'd bet for value/protection. OOP with bluff-catcher, betting thin value futile (called by better pairs/flush), bluff bad (range capped to calling stations on check). Checking realizes full EV: showdowns vs worse ace-high/pairs/draws that check behind, wins pot uncontested often, pot control preserves deep stack (123BB) for 23 hands left. Consistent with prior checks, disciplined play maximizes long-term stack vs aggressive SB.

I’m on the button with K9o in a 3-handed pot — a clear open-raise candidate. Both opponents have shown frequent 3x openings (300) in this game, and the big blind is relatively short (Flash 5,250) while the SB (Elon) is deeper. A standard 2.5–3x BB open (to 300 total) gives good fold equity, avoids giving the short BB too-good odds to see a cheap flop, and preserves position postflop where K9o plays much better. Plan: open to 300 to isolate or pick up the blinds; if Elon 3-bets hard I’ll respect that and fold (K9o is too weak OOP vs a large 3-bet). If Flash (short) shoves, I’ll call or evaluate based on exact sizing because his shove will often be wide and the pot odds will be reasonable. If called, I’ll use position to c-bet favorable flops and fold to heavy resistance on ace-heavy or highly connected boards.

J5o is a very weak offsuit hand with poor playability and low equity against a button raise range, which is wide but still favors folding from SB OOP. Pot odds are about 2:1 (calling 250 to win 700), but postflop dynamics are tough with position disadvantage on Flash and Minni's aggression shown in history (frequent raises and continuation bets). Better to fold and wait for stronger hands late in the tournament to maximize stack.

I am in the Big Blind with 92o, which is a very weak hand. Minni, the chip leader, has raised from the Button. While the price to call is relatively small (200 more into a 450 pot), 92o performs poorly post-flop and I would be playing out of position against a deep-stacked, aggressive opponent. Folding is the most prudent move to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

3-handed on the button pre-flop with Jd4h offsuit, a very weak hand with poor playability, low equity, and no suited or connected value. Pot is 150 after blinds, facing a 100 call. While button range is wide, J4o is below standard calling range against competent opponents. Flash and Minni have shown aggression and calling tendencies in prior hands, risking unnecessary chips late in the tournament (hand 129/150) when better spots exist with my 129BB stack. Folding preserves stack for premium hands to maximize long-term EV.

With Elon folding on the button, the action is on me in the small blind. Holding Ace-Trey offsuit (A3o) in a blind-vs-blind situation is a standard opening range hand. Although Minni is the chip leader and aggressive, I want to take the initiative rather than just completing the blind and playing out of position without the lead. I will raise to 300 to try and take the pot pre-flop or define the hand range.

I have 9♥4♣ in the big blind facing a 300 open from the small blind. This is a very weak hand and not worth a 3-bet bluff, but the raise size is small and calling is cheap. Calling (200 more, 300 total including my posted BB) gives me good pot odds to see a flop heads-up while preserving my 21.8k stack. Postflop I’ll proceed very cautiously out of position: fold to large C-bets on dry boards, only continue with a pair, good draw, or a clear read on a weak continuation. Folding would be too nitty given heads-up dynamics; raising is unnecessary risk with garbage. Call 300 total.

On a dry flop of 7s-5c-Qd, I will follow up my pre-flop raise with a standard continuation bet. This board favors my perceived range as the pre-flop aggressor, as I can easily have a Queen or high pocket pairs. A bet of 300 (half pot) is sufficient to test Minni's hand strength and potentially take down the pot immediately.

I’m OOP with 9♥4♣ on a dry Q♦ 7♠ 5♣ board. Flash (the preflop raiser) fires a small c-bet (300 into 900) — I have no pair and no real draw (only runner‑runner club possibilities). Calling 300 to see runouts is speculative and will often leave me behind even if I pair; raising as a bluff is too risky out of position against a short stack who can easily shove. Preserve my large stack and fold this garbage.

I am holding Ace-Five offsuit on the Button in a 3-handed game. This is a standard opening hand to exert pressure on the blinds. Although I am the shortest stack at the table, I still have 55.5 big blinds, which gives me plenty of room to play post-flop. A standard 3x raise (300) is appropriate here to take initiative and potentially win the blinds or play a pot in position.

Facing a standard button open to 300 (3x BB). I have 9♥5♠ in the small blind — a weak, offsuit holding that is dominated by much of the raiser’s range and plays very poorly out of position. Calling 250 to see a flop OOP (with the big blind still to act) invites difficult postflop decisions and poor implied odds. A 3-bet would be a thin bluff with little justification here. With a healthy stack and many hands left, preserving chips is the right long-term play. Fold.

Preflop in the big blind facing a button raise to 300 (200 to call into 450 pot). Hand is 3c4s, a weak suited connector with low card strength. While it has some potential for straights/flushes, it plays poorly out of position postflop against a wide button opening range. Equity realization is low OOP, and with only 20 hands left, preserve stack for better spots rather than speculative calling. Pot odds ~2.25:1 but not worth it given playability issues and Flash's position. Standard fold.

I'm on the button with 4♠5♦, a speculative but weak hand. The price is cheap (100 to call into a 150 pot) so I want to see a flop in position — calling preserves my positional advantage and lets me realize equity without bloating the pot out of position. 3-betting or raising with this hand is -EV vs the blinds' likely ranges, and I can fold to strong aggression post‑flop. Plan: call, play cautiously on dry or hostile boards, and look to C-bet or bluff-seize when the flop and opponent tendencies allow.

82h is a weak suited hand with poor playability from the small blind facing a button limp. The pot offers 5:1 odds for the 50 call (pot 250), but likely multiway with BB behind who may check or raise. Hand has low equity against calling ranges and poor postflop potential. Folding preserves stack for better opportunities in late tournament stages with unbalanced stacks (Flash short). Standard tight play maximizes long-term EV.

I am in the Big Blind with Jack-Seven suited. Minni has limped from the button and Elon has folded the Small Blind. Given Minni's large stack and active playstyle, I see no reason to raise and build a pot out of position with a marginal hand. I will check and take a free flop to see if I can connect with my diamonds or a pair.

I have middle pair (Sevens) on a King-high board in a limped pot. There is no reason to lead out and build a pot out of position with a marginal made hand. Checking allows me to see how Minni acts and potentially get to a cheap showdown or catch bluffs. I will check and see if she decides to bet.

Flop is K♠-7♣-3♠; I have 4♠5♦ — no pair, only a backdoor spade flush and runner-runner straight possibilities. The board is very dry and I’m in position; Flash checked so I have fold equity. A small c-bet lets me take down the pot most of the time while keeping the pot manageable if I’m called. Pot is 250; a ~40% pot bet (100) represents strength and prices out bluffs/weak hands. If I’m raised I can fold this weak hand; if called I’ll proceed cautiously on the turn (check back most blank turns, continue with better equity or improved hands). Given stacks (I’m deep, Flash is shorter) this is the right in-position bluff.

I have middle pair (sevens) on a relatively dry board. In a limped pot, Minni's small bet of 100 into 250 (40% pot) could represent a wide range of hands, including total air, weak draws, or a king. Folding middle pair to such a small bet would be overly tight, and raising doesn't offer much value as it mostly forces out hands I already beat. I will call to see the turn and re-evaluate based on the next card and Minni's subsequent action.

I have middle pair (sevens) on a board that remains relatively static. In a limped pot, my goal is to reach a cheap showdown or induce bluffs from Minni's wide range. Leading out doesn't provide much value, as better hands (Kings) won't fold and worse hands might fold or raise as a bluff. Checking allows me to control the pot size and re-evaluate if Minni decides to bet again.

Turn: K♠-7♣-3♠-6♥ with 4♠5♦ — I’ve made the straight (3-4-5-6-7). I c‑bet the flop for 100 and was called; Flash’s call then turn check looks like Kx, small pair, a float or a marginal draw rather than a strong trap. Very few hands beat my current straight (only someone holding 4+5 ties); the main river worry is a spade completing a flush if Flash is two‑spaded, but that’s unlikely to dominate his call‑range. Pot is 450 — this is a clear value‑bet spot. I want a size that gets calls from Kx, 7x and medium pairs without folding them all, so I’ll bet ~2/3 pot. If Flash jams I will call most shoves (my straight is ahead of the bulk of his range); a small raise will be evaluated, but I’ll generally prefer calling for value.

I have middle pair (sevens) on a board that remains relatively static, although the six adds some straight possibilities. Minni's bet of 300 into a 450 pot (66% pot) is a step up in aggression from her flop bet. However, in a limped pot, her range is extremely wide and contains many hands that I currently beat, such as straight draws, spade draws, or lower pairs. While I am out of position, folding second pair here would be too exploitable against an aggressive chip leader. I will call to see the river and re-evaluate her action there.

On this river, I have third pair (sevens) on a board that now has two overcards (King and Nine). Minni has shown aggression on both the flop and the turn in a limped pot, suggesting she either has a King, a Nine, or is potentially bluffing with a missed draw like spades or a straight draw. Leading out into the chip leader with a marginal hand doesn't accomplish much, as I'm unlikely to fold out better hands and don't want to get raised. Checking allows me to reach showdown cheaply if she checks back, or evaluate the size of her bet if she decides to barrel a third time. Given the Nine is an overcard and I've already called two streets, my hand strength is clearly defined as a bluff-catcher.

I have 4♠5♦ and the river K♠-7♣-3♠-6♥-9♥ gives me a 3-4-5-6-7 straight. The 9 on the river changes little — there’s no possible flush on the board and virtually no two-card combo that beats my straight except someone holding exactly 4+5 (a tie), which is extremely unlikely. Flash’s line (call small flop, call 300 turn, then check river) looks like Kx, 7x, a medium pair, or a missed draw — hands that will often call a value bet. The pot is 1,050; I want a size that extracts calls from Kx/7x/9x and medium pockets without pricing them out, so I’ll bet ~2/3 pot. If Flash jams I’ll call most shoves because my straight is ahead of the bulk of his range. Action: bet 700 to maximize value.

I am in the Big Blind with Jack-Seven suited. On this river, my second pair has been demoted to third pair by the Nine of Hearts. Minni has followed an escalating betting pattern (100, 300, 700), which in a limped pot often indicates a hand that is improving or a strong top pair (King) looking for value against a stubborn medium pair. While Minni is an aggressive player and could be barreling with missed spade draws or straight draws (like 4-5), the 700-chip bet into a 1050-chip pot (66%) is a significant investment. My hand is now a pure bluff-catcher, and given that the Nine is another overcard to my Seven, many of her semi-bluffs (like Jack-Nine or Queen-Nine) have now overtaken me. I will fold to preserve my stack and wait for a better spot.

Preflop on the button with 2h4c, a complete trash hand with no high card value, poor connectivity, and offsuit. Facing the big blind of 100 into a 150 pot, the pot odds are poor for limping or calling (needing to realize significant equity postflop which this hand lacks against likely calling ranges). Raising would be spew with such a weak holding, even in position against shorter stack Flash in SB and deep Minni in BB, who have shown aggression historically. Tournament late stage (hand 132/150), preserve stack for better spots; folding maximizes EV long-term.

I am in the Small Blind with 5h 5d, a small pocket pair. Elon has folded from the button. The pot is currently 150 (50 from my small blind and 100 from Minni's big blind), and it costs me 50 more to call. Given my stack size of 5150 and the strength of a pocket pair in a three-handed game, I should definitely play this hand. Raising is a strong option to take control of the hand and potentially win the blinds, but a call is also acceptable. However, to exert pressure and define the hand early, I will raise to 300.

I have A♣T♣ in the big blind facing a standard SB open to 300. This is too strong to fold but I don’t need to bloat the pot out of position against a reasonably deep (≈49BB) opener. Calling keeps the pot controlled, realizes my equity and preserves stack flexibility. I’ll call the 200 more (total committed 300) and evaluate the flop — c-bet or raise only on favorable A/club/ten boards or if I pick up strong fold equity.

I am the pre-flop aggressor with pocket fives. The flop comes Qd-7c-6s, which is relatively dry. Although I have an underpair, I will put in a continuation bet of 300 (half pot) to maintain the initiative and try to take down the pot. If Minni calls or raises, I will likely be forced to evaluate my hand strength more cautiously on the turn.

Call 300. A♣T♣ has good practical equity on Q♦7♣6♠: an A overcard (blocks some A‑x), a backdoor nut‑club redraw and reasonable showdown value vs a wide SB c‑bet. Flash’s bet is small (300 into a 900 pot) — calling gives ~25% pot odds, which is attractive against a wide continuation range from the preflop opener. I’m out of position, so I don’t want to bloat the pot or turn a marginal Ace‑high into a tough spot by raising OOP; a raise would commit chips vs hands that have me crushed (Qx, 77/66) or that can shove (given Flash’s stack), and it’s unnecessary to realize equity with a hand that plays well by calling. Plan: call and evaluate the turn — if I pair an A or T or pick up a club I can start building the pot or apply pressure; if the turn is a blank and Flash barrels, I’ll fold to significant aggression unless I improve.

The turn card is a 2 of clubs, which is a complete brick for the current board (Qd-7c-6s). While I still have an underpair with pocket fives, Minni's call on the flop suggests she either has a piece of the board (a Queen, a seven, or a six) or a straight draw (like 89 or 54). Given Minni's tendency in previous hands to be a sticky caller, a second barrel (betting again) is unlikely to force her off a pair, and I am currently behind any Queen, Seven, or Six. Checking here allows me to keep the pot small, evaluate her action, and potentially reach a cheap showdown if the river is favorable or if she checks back.

Turn: Q♦ 7♣ 6♠ 2♣. I hold A♣ T♣ — I didn’t pair but I picked up a big piece: Ace-high plus a 4‑card club draw. Flash was the preflop raiser and c‑bet the flop small, then checked the turn, which shows some weakness or pot‑control. This is a good spot to apply pressure and build when I have clear equity: my hand has both fold equity and real equity to improve. I’ll bet roughly half the pot to charge his floating hands and draws, deny a free river, and extract value if he’s on weaker Ax/Kx or missed broadway cards. Size: 600 into 1200 (≈½ pot) — large enough to get value/folds but not so large that a shove commits my whole stack unnecessarily. If he jams I’ll call reasonably wide given pot odds and my draw+overcard/blockers; if he calls I’ll re‑evaluate river and extract value when I hit or control the pot if I miss.

On the turn, the board is Qd-7c-6s-2c. I have an underpair with pocket fives. After I checked, Minni bet 600 into a 1200 pot. While the pot odds are 3:1 (requiring 25% equity), my hand is extremely vulnerable. Minni's range for calling a pre-flop raise and a flop continuation bet includes many Queens, Sevens, and Sixes, all of which have me soundly beaten. Additionally, since I am out of position, even if I call the turn, I will likely face another significant bet on the river. Given my position as the short stack in the tournament, preserving my remaining 4000 chips for a stronger starting hand or a better board texture is a more prudent strategy than calling down with a weak underpair on a board with three overcards.

I am on the button with 8-7 offsuit. While this is often an opening hand in a three-handed game, I am currently the shortest stack (46 BB) compared to Minni (226 BB) and Elon (126 BB). Both opponents have shown a willingness to defend their blinds and play aggressively. Given the tournament is nearing its end (Hand 133 of 150), I prefer to wait for a stronger starting hand or a better situation to commit chips, rather than opening a marginal hand and potentially facing a 3-bet or playing a difficult post-flop pot out of position against deep stacks.

Raise to 200 total. In a heads-up spot from the button/small blind I can open very wide — 3♣5♦ is marginal but fine as a steal attempt. I have position postflop and a big-chip advantage (22,650 vs 12,600), so applying pressure to pick up the 150-chip pot is profitable. Raising to the minimum (200 total) keeps the risk small while giving fold equity. Plan: if Elon folds I scoop the pot; if he calls I’ll play cautiously postflop, looking to continuation-bet small on low/connected boards that favor my range or check-fold to significant resistance on dry boards. If Elon 3-bets to a meaningful size I’ll concede and fold this weak holding.

A3o is a marginal hand at best, offsuit with a weak kicker. Facing a raise from the SB after BTN folds, I'm out of position postflop. Pot odds are about 3:1 (calling 100 into 300), but equity is poor against Minni's likely range, which includes better aces, pairs, and broadways given her aggressive history. Calling risks difficult spots OOP with dominated ace-high or draws that miss. Raising is overplay; 3-bet jamming or min-raising doesn't justify the risk with 12600 stack late in tournament. Folding preserves stack for better spots in 3-handed play.

8♣2♦ offsuit is one of the worst starting hands. Although I’m on the button, there are two players left to act (SB and BB) and the pot is already multiway — calling 100 to see a flop in a 3‑way pot with 8‑2o will put me in many difficult, dominated spots. Raising as a bluff with this hand is unnecessary and likely to be called or re-raised by deeper stacks. Preserve the deep stack and wait for a better spot — fold.

Qh Th is a strong suited broadway connector in the small blind facing only the big blind after button fold. There is dead money in the pot (150), and completing or raising has excellent implied odds with my stack depth against Flash's 45BB. Raising small takes the initiative OOP, denies equity to Flash's wider calling range, and sets up good postflop playability if called. Min raise is 200, but sizing to 300 (3x BB) is standard for value/thin value and fold equity. Equity holds well vs shove range if Flash jams short. Late tournament, maximizing EV by playing strong hands aggressively.

I am in the Big Blind with Jack-Trey offsuit. Elon has raised from the Small Blind to 300. While the price to call is only 200 more into a 400 pot, J3o is an extremely weak hand to play out of position, especially with a stack of 45 BBs. Elon has been active with raises, and I would likely be forced to fold on most flops or find myself dominated if I hit a Jack. Given we are at hand 134 of 150, I should preserve my stack for better opportunities rather than defending with marginal trash.

3-handed on the button first to act pre-flop with 82 suited. While suited and position is good, 82s is a speculative hand with poor playability and low equity against calling ranges. Blinds are tight-aggressive based on history (frequent raises and folds), and with Flash short-stacked at 4450 and Minni deep, limping invites multiway pots where this hand struggles. Raising is not justified with this trash. Standard fold to preserve stack near tournament end (hand 135/150), avoiding speculative plays that could bust us.

I am in the Small Blind with K d 3 d, a suited King which is a relatively strong hand in three-handed play. Elon has folded from the button. I need to put in 50 more to call the 100 big blind. Given my stack size of 4450 (44.5 big blinds) and the goal to maximize my stack, I should look to take the initiative against Minni or at least see a flop with a hand that has good playability. Raising is a better option than calling here to put pressure on the big blind and potentially win the pot immediately. A standard 3x raise to 300 total is appropriate.

Call to 300. Jd7d is a marginal but suited hand worth defending the big blind against a frequent SB opener. The pot is 400 and I only need to invest 200 more (about 3:1 pot odds), so calling to see a flop is a low-cost way to realize equity. Flash opens a wide range and a small raise to 500 would buy little fold equity while creating an awkward pot and possibly inducing a shove from his 4200 stack. Plan: see the flop and continue only with a diamond draw, a jack, two pair/strong hands or favorable board texture; fold to large aggression on dry, dominating boards.

I am the pre-flop aggressor in a three-handed game. The flop 6d-9s-7h is quite coordinated and likely connects with the big blind's calling range (which often includes middle connectors and small pairs). However, King-high with a backdoor flush draw (Kd) still has some equity. I will place a standard continuation bet of 300 (50% pot) to represent a strong overpair or a hand like 10-10/J-J and try to take down the pot immediately. If Minni calls or raises, I will likely have to proceed with caution or fold on the turn unless my hand improves.

I have J♦7♦ on 6♦-9♠-7♥: a pair of sevens with a strong jack-kicker and a backdoor diamond possibility. Flash opened from the SB and is betting small (300 into a ~900 pot) — likely a wide continuation range that includes many overcards/bluffs that my pair currently beats. Calling 300 gives ~25% break-even equity, keeps the pot manageable out of position versus a shorter-stacked opponent, and lets me realize equity or improve on the turn. I’ll fold to large, committed turn aggression on a dry card, but if I pick up two pair/trips/a jack or a favorable diamond-runner I’ll shift to value/pressure. For now I defend the big blind and call to see the turn.

The turn Ten of Diamonds provides me with a flush draw and a gutshot straight draw (requiring an 8). The board (6-7-9-10) is extremely coordinated and likely connects well with the Big Blind's calling range, which could include many middle connectors or pairs. Given that I have strong drawing equity but likely do not hold the best hand currently, I will check. This allows me to potentially see a free river card or call a reasonable bet to realize my equity, rather than betting into a board where the opponent is unlikely to fold.

Board: 6♦-9♠-7♥-T♦. I hold J♦7♦ — second pair with a very strong 4-card diamond draw and a good Jack kicker. Flash was the preflop raiser and c-bet the flop, then checked the turn, which looks like weakness or a probing check. Pot is 1,200 and it’s checked to me: this is a good spot to take the initiative with a protection/value bet. I have ~9 diamond outs to a flush plus additional outs to trips/Jacks (~30% to improve), and many hands in his wide opening range (overcards, single-pair hands) will call a modest bet. I’ll bet about half the pot (600) to extract value from worse pairs/overcards, charge his draws, deny a free river, and fold out pure air. If he shows heavy action (big check-raise or shove) I’ll re-evaluate — he’s often representing T/9x or a set there and I won’t automatically commit without the correct odds/reads.

I have a King-high flush draw (9 outs) and a gutshot straight draw (4 outs, though some overlap with diamonds). The board is 6-7-9-10, making it very dangerous, but with 12 clean outs to likely the best hand, my equity is approximately 26%. The pot is 1800 and it costs 600 to call, giving me pot odds of 3:1 (25%). Since my equity is slightly higher than the pot odds and I have implied odds if I hit the flush on the river, calling is the most profitable decision. Folding would be too tight given the draw, and raising into such a coordinated board against a Big Blind who has shown interest is too risky.

On the river, I have improved to bottom pair with my Trey, but the board is extremely coordinated (6-7-9-10) and any Eight completes a straight. Minni showed strength by betting the turn after calling the flop, suggesting she either has a piece of the board or a straight. My pair of Treys has very little showdown value in this spot, and betting would essentially be a bluff that is unlikely to succeed against her demonstrated range. I will check and hope to get to a cheap showdown or fold if faced with a significant bet.

I hold J♦7♦ on 6♦-9♠-7♥-T♦-3♣: second pair with a good J‑kicker but my diamond draw missed. Flash (SB) is a wide opener who c-bet the flop, checked the turn and then called my half‑pot turn bet — that line smells like marginal pairs, missed draws or weak showdown hands rather than a slow‑played monster. The river 3♣ is a blank and he checks, giving me an opportunity to extract value from worse pairs (small pockets, 6x), Kx/Qx type hands and missed draws that will call a modest price. I don’t want to overcommit versus straights/sets/T9, so I’ll bet a medium size to get calls from worse while keeping the pot controllable. Bet half the pot (≈1200). If he raises/shoves I will fold to large aggression.

On the river, I have improved to bottom pair with my Trey, but the board is extremely coordinated (6-7-9-10) and any Eight completes a straight. Minni showed strength by betting the turn after calling the flop and now betting half the pot on the river. This line strongly suggests she has at least a middle pair or better, and likely a hand that connects well with this board like two pair or a straight. My pair of Treys has almost no showdown value here, and I am only beating a pure bluff with missed high cards. Given the pot odds and the likelihood that she has a made hand, a fold is the most prudent play to preserve my remaining stack for the final hands of the tournament.

I have 7s8s on the button with a stack of 3300 chips (33 big blinds). Both opponents have significantly larger stacks. 7s8s is a strong suited connector that plays well in multi-way pots or in position. Given the stack depths and the fact that I am on the button, I will raise to 300. This puts pressure on the blinds and allows me to take control of the hand with a hand that has good post-flop potential if called.

Facing a button open to 300 from the cutoff/button while I'm in the small blind with 7♥3♥, this is an easy fold. Calling 250 to see a flop would require roughly 35–36% equity just to break even (250 to win ~700) and 7-3s has very poor equity and playability out of position against a raiser’s range — especially with the big blind (Elon) still to act and likely to either defend or squeeze. A 3-bet bluff or shove isn’t attractive either: Flash’s stack is short (≈3000) so I’d need a specific read to exploit it, and I don’t want to bloat the pot out of position with such a weak hand. Preserve my 23,950 stack and wait for a better spot.

Preflop as BB facing a button raise to 300 (3BB) from short-stacked Flash (30BB) after SB fold. Qc8h offsuit is a marginal hand but has ~38-42% equity against a typical wide button opening range (pairs, broadway, suited aces, connectors). Pot odds excellent: calling 200 more into 450 pot requires ~23% equity. Defending wide OOP vs button is standard GTO, especially vs shorter stack who may overplay postflop. Can check-fold weak boards or realize equity on good ones. 3-bet unprofitable with this hand; fold too tight given odds and range. Call to realize equity.

Flop board K-K-9 rainbow (diamonds and hearts) is very dry and paired, heavily favoring Flash's button raising range which includes many Kx, 98s, and strong Ax. My Qc8h is queen-high with no pair or draw, ~15-25% equity vs his probable betting range (low vs value, higher vs pure bluffs but he'll check those behind OOP). OOP first to act, pot 650, standard check-fold to any bet as calling requires too much equity (e.g., half-pot bet needs ~25%) and I have trash. No value bet, bluffing polarizes poorly here with low fold equity on strong board vs aggressive shortstack (30BB). Defended pre wide per GTO but realize this spot correctly by folding postflop.

The flop of K-K-9 is very favorable for my range as the pre-flop aggressor on the button. I represent more Kings (AK, KQ, KJ, KT) than Elon should have in his big blind calling range. After Elon checks, a continuation bet is standard here to put pressure on his 'air' hands and small pocket pairs. I will bet 250 into the 650 pot, a sizing that is efficient for a dry, paired board, allowing me to win the pot immediately if he missed.

Facing a 250 bet into 900 pot (~28% pot) on a Kd Kh 9d flop with Qc8h (queen-high, no pair, no draw). Equity is poor (~10-20%) vs Flash's button range which hits this paired kings board hard (Kx, 99, 98s, AQo+). Pot odds require ~22% equity to call, which I lack. Short-stacked opponent (27BB) unlikely to bluff heavily OOP on strong board; value-heavy betting range. Check-fold is GTO standard here after wide preflop defend. Raising has no fold equity and polarizes poorly with trash. Folding preserves stack for better spots late in tournament.

I’m on the button in a 3-handed game so open-raising very wide is standard. 6♠3♥ is a weak hand, but I have position on both blinds and a big stack (23,950), so a standard 3bb open is the correct play to apply pressure and pick up the pot preflop. Pot is 150 and calling 100 is a passive line that loses initiative; raising to 300 gives me fold equity against Flash (BB ~35bb) and Elon (SB ~123bb) while risking very little relative to my stack. Plan: fold to a large 3-bet, but if called use positional advantage to c-bet favorable flops and avoid bloating the pot with this poor holding.

Preflop in SB with Ad4d vs button raise to 300 by Minni (deep stack) with Flash short in BB. A4s is a strong suited ace with good playability, ace blocker, and flush potential. Pot odds are excellent (250 to call into 450, ~36% pot odds require ~28% equity). Button opening range is wide, so we have decent equity OOP. Calling keeps the pot small, realizes equity well multiway if Flash calls, and allows us to play post-flop flexibly with position disadvantage minimized by hand strength. 3-betting risks bloating pot OOP without domination, folding too tight late tournament with good price. Call to continue profitably.

I am in the big blind with Queen-Trey offsuit. Minni opened to 300 from the button and Elon called in the small blind. The pot is currently 700 and it costs me 200 more to call. While Q3o is a weak hand, the pot odds are 3.5 to 1, which might tempt some players to defend. However, my stack size is only 35.5 big blinds (3550 chips). Playing a weak hand out of position in a three-way pot is extremely difficult and often leads to losing more chips post-flop. Given that I need to preserve my stack to pick better spots in the remaining 14 hands of the tournament, I will choose to fold here and wait for a more advantageous situation.

Flop of 2s8s7c is draw-heavy with flush draws, straight draws (e.g., 56, 65, T9, 45), and pairs possible in Minni's wide button raising range. Ad4d gives ace-high, strong blocker to top pair Ax hands, and backdoor diamond flush/straight potential, but no immediate equity beyond that. OOP first to act with 121bb effective vs Minni's deeper stack, donk betting weak ace-high is suboptimal: risks check-raise from monsters or calls from any draw/draw+blocker, poor post-flop playability if called. Checking realizes equity best - induces bluffs/misses to check behind (I likely best hand vs air), allows controlled pot size if bet into (call small bets with blocker/live ace, fold to large polarization), controls SPR for river decisions. Late tournament (137/150), stack preservation key while seeking spots to accumulate; no need force action here profitably. Pot-committed minimally preflop (called 250 into ~450), good price to see flops like this.

I raised preflop and have position on Elon, who checked the flop (2♠ 8♠ 7♣). My hand (6♠3♥) has no pair and only weak backdoor equity (runner-runner spade/straight), so I don't want to bloat the pot with marginal holdings. However, in 3-handed play a high-frequency c-bet is correct to pick up pots and pressure his wide calling range. I will make a modest, credible half-pot continuation bet (~350 into 700) to fold out missed hands and realize fold equity. Plan: bet here but be ready to fold to a meaningful check-raise — I have too little equity to continue against large aggression. If called, proceed cautiously on the turn and only continue with improved equity or a clear plan to bluff on a later street.

Facing a 350 bet into 1050 pot (~1/3 pot) on a draw-heavy flop 2s8s7c after checking OOP. Ad4d is ace-high with no pair, no flush draw (diamonds), no straight draw, just backdoor potential. Board favors Minni's wide button range: likely contains spades, pairs (77,88,22 sets, overpairs, Ax suited spades, straight draws like 56s/9Ts). Ace-high has showdown value vs pure air but poor equity vs polarized c-bet range (strong hands or draws bluffing weak). Pot odds ~4:1 require ~20% equity, but realized equity OOP is low - calling bloats pot to ~1400 OOP, vulnerable to turn barrels, tough rivers with 121bb effective. Folding preserves stack late (hand 137/150), avoids tough spots vs aggressive button, seeks better leverage spots. Previous plan to check/call small worked to see cheap flop, but this sizing doesn't justify continuing profitably without stronger holdings.

3-handed on the button with T9h suited, a strong speculative hand with great playability, position, and suited connectors that perform well multiway or heads-up. Unraised pot (pot 150 from blinds, to call BB 100), perfect spot to open-raise standard 3x BB to 300 to steal dead money or play post-flop in position. Opponents: Flash short-stacked ~35BB in SB, Minni deep in BB; both have shown aggression but also folding tendencies pre-flop from history. Raising builds pot, applies pressure, and maximizes EV with fold equity + direct equity vs calling ranges. Limping weak, folding too tight late tournament to grow stack.

I am holding King-Jack offsuit in the small blind, which is a very strong hand in a three-handed game. Elon has opened from the button, a position from which he likely has a wide range. With a stack of 3,500 chips (35 big blinds), I am currently the short stack and need to look for opportunities to accumulate. A three-bet is preferred over calling here to take the initiative, potentially win the pot pre-flop, and prevent the big blind from entering with a wide range. Raising to 900 allows me to put pressure on Elon while still leaving room to fold if he 4-bets all-in, though I'll be very committed to most favorable flops.

Big blind with 9♣7♣ facing BTN open to 300 and an SB 3-bet to 900. Pot is 1300 and I only need to put in 800 more (900 total) — break-even equity ~38%. 9♣7♣ is a suited connector that plays well postflop and can realize equity, especially if Elon folds and I end up heads-up vs the short-stacked 2,650 SB. A min 4-bet to 1,500 is too small to reliably isolate and a larger shove would be overleveraging a marginal hand. I prefer to call, see a flop, and play exploitatively: if Elon folds I can apply pressure to the short stack; if Elon calls I’ll proceed cautiously out of position and evaluate based on flop texture.

Facing a 3-bet to 900 from short-stacked Flash (~26BB effective) in SB over my button open, and a flat-call from deep-stacked Minni OOP in BB. Pot is 2100 with only 600 to call (~3.5:1 direct odds, plus implied). T9h is a premium suited connector with huge playability, straight/flush potential, and dominates many 3-bet shoves/calls from short stack's wide SB 3-bet range (e.g., Ax suited, broadway, pairs). Position on both allows controlling pot postflop, realizing equity multiway if we hit, or folding to aggression cheaply if missed. Folding wastes fold equity from initial raise and dead money; 4-betting risks short shove or deep jam/call with better, turning hand face-up preflop. Late tournament (138/150), preserve stack but exploit position/speculative equity vs aggressive opponents' history of wide 3-bets/flats. Call to play postflop optimally.

I am in a three-way 3-bet pot with a stack-to-pot ratio (SPR) of nearly 1. My hand, King-Jack offsuit, has missed the flop of 4s-Qc-8s, although I do hold the Jack of Spades for a backdoor flush draw and two overcards. Both opponents called my 3-bet pre-flop, which suggests they have relatively strong ranges containing pocket pairs, suited connectors, or broadway cards that may have connected with this board. Since I am out of position against two players, jamming my remaining 2650 chips into a 2700 pot is a very high-risk move with limited fold equity, as any Queen or even a strong draw is likely to call. Checking allows me to see how the other players react. If the board checks around, I get a free turn card to potentially improve. If someone bets, I can safely fold and preserve my 26.5 big blinds for a better opportunity, rather than risking my tournament life on King-high.

I called the SB’s 3-bet preflop to see a flop OOP and now face a relatively dry board: 4♠ Q♣ 8♠. I hold 9♣7♣ — no pair, no immediate made draw. The only real equity is a runner-runner club flush (Qc plus my two clubs gives me three clubs total, so I need both turn+river clubs) and very limited straight backdoors. Flash (SB) checked and is short-stacked (~2,650) so a bet could sometimes fold him, but Elon (BTN) cold-called the 3-bet and can have a wide, often strong range (Qx, Ax/Kx, medium pairs, or floats). Betting OOP here would bloat the pot with a marginal holding and commit me to tough decisions if I’m raised. I prefer to control pot size and see how Elon reacts — check and evaluate: if Elon checks behind I get a free turn to improve; if he fires a small bet I can call to realize backdoor equity and use my SPR to bluff later; if he makes a large bet or raises I’ll fold unless I improve. Action: check to keep options and avoid committing chips with a weak, unimproved hand.

Flop checked through by both opponents (Flash as preflop 3-bettor first to act, Minni OOP), signaling potential weakness, missed draws, or traps but unlikely given their lines. Board 4s-Qc-8s is coordinated with spade flush draw, possible straight draws (5-6-7, 9-T-J), but my T9h blocks parts of broadway like QT/KT and has backdoor heart flush/straight potential (7/J turn cleans gutshot). In position on button late tournament (138/150, 3-handed), pot 2700 is large dead money; betting exploits fold equity against short Flash (2650 behind, ~1 stack) who may fold medium strength post-3bet check, and deep Minni who flats OOP but checks indicate caution. Semi-bluff bet ~55% pot denies equity to worse (pairs/draws), builds stack without overcommitting 11200 stack. Check risks giving free card to spades/straights where I lose position EV. Bet sizing 1500 pressures short stack commit range while keeping Minni pot odds poor OOP.

I am in a very difficult spot here. Pre-flop, I 3-bet with King-Jack offsuit and got two callers, leading to a large pot of 2700. The flop (Qs-4s-8s) has missed my hand entirely, though I do have the Jack of Spades for a backdoor flush draw. After I checked, Elon bet 1500 into the pot of 2700. This is a very strong lead into two players, especially into me, the pre-flop aggressor. With only 2650 chips remaining, calling 1500 would leave me with just 1150 chips, essentially committing my stack with only King-high. Minni is also still to act behind me and could easily have a piece of this board. Given that Elon is showing significant strength in a three-way 3-bet pot, I have very little equity here. It is better to preserve my remaining 26.5 big blinds and wait for a more favorable opportunity in the final 12 hands of the tournament.

Elon (button) barrels 1,500 into a 4,200 pot after Flash folded — I only need 1,500 to see the turn, which gives me ~26% pot odds. 9♣7♣ has very little immediate equity (no pair, only runner-runner club/straight outs), but against a wide continuation range in position my equity is borderline and calling is cheap enough to realize it. Raising OOP with nothing would bloat the pot and overcommit; folding denies good odds. Plan: call to see the turn, then fold to heavy turn aggression unless I pick up a pair or a club (or a clear runner for a straight), and become more aggressive if I improve.

Turn is 4♠ Q♣ 8♠ 4♥ and I hold 9♣7♣. I have no pair and the club backdoor is now dead — only two river outs (9 or 7), ~4% equity. Elon was the preflop/flop aggressor and his flop barrel suggests a range of Qx, mid pairs or strong holdings; betting OOP with essentially air would bloat the pot and put me in a poor spot if called or raised. Pot control is the priority: check and see the river for a cheap chance to pair one of my two outs; fold to meaningful river aggression unless I improve or face a very small probe.

Turn board pairs the 4h, completing some straight draws (e.g., 5-6-7 becomes two pair) but keeping spade flush draw live and improving backdoor hearts for me slightly. Minni checks again OOP after calling flop bet on coordinated spade-heavy flop, suggesting caution: possibly medium strength like Qx (QTen blocked by me), 8x, pocket pairs below Q, or missed draw trapping less likely given check-check preflop aggressor. My perceived range as button preflop raiser who called 3bet, bet flop into field, reps polarized strong (QQ/88/sets/two-pair, top spades) or bluffs. T9h has decent blockers (T9 blocks JTs, QTs straights/draws), no showdown but fold equity high vs her range. Late tournament (138/150, 3-handed), pot 5700 dead money with position; betting ~2/3 pot (3800) applies max pressure on short-to-medium stack effective, denies equity to live draws (spades ~9outs, ~18% river), extracts thin value from worse, builds stack without overcommitting (5900 behind). Checking risks free river card improving her or giving pot away cheaply; exploit her history of calling stations but folding to aggression postflop (e.g., hands 50,53,84). Optimal semi-bluff to maximize EV.

Turn decision: Elon bets 3,800 into a 9,500 pot. I hold 9♣7♣ on 4♠ Q♣ 8♠ 4♥ — I have no pair and the turn killed my backdoor (I need two clubs on turn+river to flush, now impossible). I have only two clean outs (9 or 7) → ~4–4.5% equity on the river. Calling 3,800 into the 9,500 pot requires ~28.6% equity to break even (3,800 / (9,500+3,800) ≈ 28.6%), so this is a massive negative EV call. Elon’s line (open, flop barrel, big turn bet) is consistent with strong value (Qx, pairs, trips) and is unlikely to fold to an OOP raise; raising/bluffing here would risk chips with virtually no equity and poor breakout chances. Preserve a big healthy stack and fold.

With a weak hand like Nine of Spades and Deuce of Diamonds (92o) on the Button, there is no strategic reason to enter the pot, especially with a relatively short stack compared to the opponents' massive leads. Folding is the most prudent action.

I’m in the small blind with Q♥J♣ and there’s only one opponent (Elon) left. Heads-up QJ offsuit is a strong hand and performs well against a typical big-blind defend-range, so I want to seize the initiative and apply pressure rather than just completing the small blind. A minimum raise to 200 (total) puts pressure on weaker holdings, isolates Elon, and keeps the pot manageable with deep stacks — if he folds I win the 150 pot right away; if he calls I have good equity and can play postflop. If he 3-bets large I will re-evaluate (likely fold or proceed cautiously given I can be dominated), but the default is an open-raise to take control of the hand.

KsTs is a strong suited Broadway hand with good playability, straight and flush potential. Facing a small raise to 200 from Minni in the SB after button folds, the pot odds are excellent: calling an additional 100 into a 300 pot (4:1). Minni's range from SB is wide, especially after a fold, including many Ax, Kx, suited connectors, and pairs that we dominate or have equity against. With deep stacks (15.3k effective vs her 21.75k), calling to realize equity post-flop is optimal rather than folding a premium speculative hand or 3-betting a marginal spot OOP. Will play aggressively post-flop if hits.

Board K♥ 8♦ A♦. I hold Q♥J♣ — I have missed completely: no pair, no diamond, no realistic draw, and both hole cards are dominated by the Ace and King on board. Elon called preflop from the big blind, so his defending range is wide but contains a lot of A‑x, K‑x, diamond draws and pocket pairs that connect well with this flop. Heads‑up I could fire a continuation bet, but on an A‑high, two‑diamond board my fold equity is low (many hands will call with a pair or a diamond draw) and I don’t want to bloat the pot OOP with air. With deep stacks this is a low‑variance spot: check and control the pot. Plan: if Elon checks back we get a free turn to improve; if he bets a small probe (≈≤1/3 pot ≈ ≤130) I’ll consider calling to realize equity and target turn decisions; if he fires a large bet (≈≥1/2 pot) I’ll fold to preserve stack. Therefore I check.

Flop Kh 8d Ad gives me top pair top kicker with Ks Ts, blocking AA/AK and dominating many Kx/Ax in Minni's SB opening range. Board has diamond flush draw and possible straight draws (e.g., QJ, T9), but it's relatively dry. Minni checks OOP after her small preflop raise, which polarizes her range: either very strong (sets, two-pair unlikely yet) or weak (missed high cards, floats). Betting extracts value from her Ax (worse kicker), weaker Kx, pairs below K, and draws while protecting against them. Pot is 400, a 2/3 pot bet of 275 builds it nicely without overbetting, denies equity to draws, and keeps fold equity vs bluffs. Checking risks giving free cards to draws or allowing her to rep strength. With 15.2k effective stack, this spot maximizes EV post-flop aggression as planned preflop.

I checked the A‑high, two‑diamond flop as planned — Q♥J♣ has missed (no pair, no diamond draw) and is frequently dominated by Elon’s defending range. Elon’s bet of 275 into the ~400‑chip pot (pot 675 after the bet; sizing ≈69% pot) is large and heavily indicates Ax, Kx, sets or strong diamond hands. Calling 275 would require about 29% equity (275/(675+275)=275/950≈29%), which my hand doesn’t have versus that range. With no realistic redraws and deep stacks, I preserve chips and fold rather than inflate the pot out of position with air.

Button with Ks5d — marginal hand but great position and table dynamics favor opening. Pot is 150 and to-call 100; Flash in BB is short (≈25 BB) and Elon in SB is deep/aggressive. I want to take the initiative and apply pressure to the short stack while preserving position vs Elon, so I’ll open to a standard 3x BB. Raise to 300: big enough to earn folds/isolate the short stack and still allow postflop play in position if called by Elon. If Flash shoves, K5o is usually behind a shove range so I’ll fold to preserve my deep stack; if Elon calls we play postflop with positional edge.

Q9o is a marginal offsuit hand from the small blind facing a button raise. Minni likely has a wide range, but my hand lacks suitedness, strong blockers, or premium playability out of position. Calling commits chips OOP with poor postflop prospects, and 3-betting risks playing a bloated pot OOP against a strong range or short-stack shove from Flash. With deep stacks and near end of tournament (hand 140/150), better to fold and wait for stronger spots to maximize stack.

I am currently the short stack with 25.5 big blinds. Minni, the chip leader, has raised from the button, which is a standard play for her. While I am getting 3.25:1 odds to call with J7o, I will be out of position for the rest of the hand against a very aggressive opponent. Given that there are only 10 hands remaining in the tournament, I need to be selective about which pots I enter to avoid being bled out by post-flop pressure. J7o is too weak to defend profitably here; I'll wait for a better spot to commit my chips.

A♦4♣ is a weak offsuit hand with poor playability and low equity multi-way. On the button with 155BB effective, facing a short-stacked SB (25BB) who is likely to shove wide over a raise and a deep BB who defends aggressively based on history, folding preserves my stack for better spots near the end of the tournament (hand 141/150). No steal value justifies risking chips with this holding.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Queen offsuit, which is a very strong hand in a three-handed game. Since the Button has folded, I am heads-up against Minni in the Big Blind. With only 10 hands remaining in the tournament and being the short stack, I need to look for opportunities to increase my chips. Raising to 3x the big blind is a standard opening size that puts pressure on the Big Blind and allows me to take control of the pot early with a premium hand.

Facing a 3x open from the small blind with 4♦7♥ is a weak holding, but the call is cheap and defensible. To call I need 200 more (300 total including my 100 BB). Pot odds are ~2:1 to see a flop and Flash is a medium/short stack (≈22.5bb) so a shove or big re‑raise is possible — I don’t want to reraise and face a shove with garbage. Folding surrenders good equity for a small price. Plan: call to see a flop, fold to strong action if I miss, and avoid bloating the pot out of position with a marginal hand.

I have hit top pair with the best possible kicker (TPTK) on the flop. Given that I am the short stack with only 10 hands remaining in the tournament, I need to maximize value from my strong hands to attempt a double-up. The board is relatively safe, though there are some gutshot straight draws and two spades. A bet of 300 (half pot) is a standard continuation bet that extracts value from weaker queens, nines, and middle pairs while protecting my hand against draws.

I called preflop with 4♦7♥ and missed this dry-ish flop (Q♠ 9♣ 3♠). Flash (SB) has a short stack (~19–20 BB) and has bet a small 300 into a 900 pot — a 1/3-pot probe. Calling 300 gives me 300 to win ~1500 (pot after my call) — ~5:1 pot odds, so I only need ~16.7% equity. I have 6 clean outs to pair (4s and 7s) and roughly ~24% to hit by the river, so a call is marginally +EV to realize equity. I’m out of position and have virtually no showdown value, so I don’t want to bluff-raise and get shoved on by a short-stacked opponent. Plan: call the 300 to see the turn, fold to a shove/large raise unless I improve, and re-evaluate on the turn if he shows weakness.

I have top pair with the best possible kicker (TPTK) on a board of Qs-9c-3s-6h. My stack-to-pot ratio is approximately 1.1 (1350 chips remaining with 1200 in the pot). With only 10 hands left in the tournament and being the short stack, I must look to maximize value from my strong holdings. Minni has called both pre-flop and on the flop, which suggests she likely has a piece of the board (like a weaker Queen or a Nine) or a flush draw. A bet of 600 (half the pot) allows me to extract value from her calling range while keeping enough behind to potentially fold or shove on the river depending on the runout, though with my stack size, I am likely committed to this hand.

Pot is 1800 and I must call 600. After my call the pot would be 2400, so I need ~25% equity to break even (600/2400). With 4♦7♥ on Q♠ 9♣ 3♠ 6♥ I only have 6 clean outs (the three remaining 4s and three 7s) — ~6/46 ≈ 13% to hit the river. Some of those outs can be blocked or still lose to better hands (two pair/trips), and Flash’s line (raise pre, C‑bet flop, turn aggression) is consistent with a made Q/9, a spade draw that picked up a river card, or a short‑stack shove candidate. Raising as a bluff is thin after I called the flop and will likely get jammed or called by hands that beat me. With insufficient pot odds and little showdown value, folding preserves my large stack for better spots.

I am on the button with Ten-Four offsuit. With only 31.5 big blinds and two massive stacks behind me in the blinds who have shown considerable aggression throughout the tournament, opening with such a weak hand is unprofitable. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity to commit my chips as the tournament approaches its final hands.

3♦8♥ is a weak, uncoordinated hand and I'm out of position as the small blind versus only the big blind. The cost to see a flop is just 50 more chips (total 100 committed) which is cheap with deep stacks; raising to 200 would unnecessarily open myself to 3-bets from Elon, who has shown aggression and a big stack. I’ll call to see a flop cheaply, fold to a substantial preflop reraise, and play the flop cautiously — only continue aggressively if I connect or get favorable action.

Preflop in BB facing a limp from SB after BTN fold. A2o is a marginal ace-high hand with little playability offsuit. SB limp suggests a wide range, possibly weak. Raising OOP risks getting called by better and playing postflop awkwardly with a short-stacked Flash behind if he wakes up, though he folded. Checking realizes equity cheaply, allows seeing flop for free, and keeps pot small. Ace blocker helps in position postflop. Late tournament, preserve stack, no need to force action with trash.

I completely missed this flop with 3♦8♥ — no pair, no realistic draw (only a remote two-card straight backdoor). The board (5♥ 4♠ 5♣) is paired and favors hands with a 5 or a 4; Elon is capable of betting a wide range but also shows aggression, so I don’t want to inflate the pot with air. Pot is small relative to my stack, so the +EV play is to check and see his action. I will fold to a standard c-bet but re-evaluate if he checks back or gives a very weak, tiny bet on which I can exploit.

Flop board is 5h-4s-5c, a paired board favoring sets, two pairs, or straight draws like 23, 36, etc. My As2c is ace-high with no pair, no flush draw, and no straight draw (gutshot to 3 is unlikely to be clean). Minni's SB limp-check range is wide and weak, often including suited broadways, small pairs, Ax suited/offsuit, but this flop hits her calling range hard with trips, 45s, 35s, etc. Her check suggests possible weakness but could be trapping or air. Betting OOP risks getting raised by better hands or floated, turning my hand face-up, and committing more chips with marginal equity. Pot control is key with a bluff-catcher that blocks AA/KK but has poor playability. Checking realizes equity for free, induces bluffs on scary turns, and keeps the pot small late in the tournament (hand 142/150) when preserving our healthy 154BB stack to exploit short-stacked Flash or bubble dynamics is priority. If checked back, turn brings potential improvement or fold equity later.

I checked the flop to see Elon’s reaction and he showed weakness by checking back. I’ve completely missed with 3♦8♥ and have no real draw, so my goal is to take down a small pot cheaply rather than giving a free river. A half-pot probe (100 into a 200 pot) applies pressure to hands that checked back (overcards, weak K/Q/J, some turned 6/7) while risking very little of my 21,200 stack. If Elon check-raises I’ll concede (he’s representing real strength: a 5, trips, or a strong 9); if he calls I’ll re-evaluate on the river and fold to significant aggression if I don’t improve. Small bluff with clear fold plan to preserve chips if met with strength.

Facing a small 100 bet into 300 pot on the turn (pot odds ~4:1), with As2c for ace-high on a paired 5h4s5c9h board. My hand has decent showdown value as a bluff-catcher, blocking strong Ax hands like AsXs or AK. Minni's SB limp-check-flop range is wide (suited connectors, small pairs, weak Ax, draws), and her small turn bet after checking flop likely represents thin value (trips like 55/A5/45, middle pairs), bluffs (missed draws like 67, JTs), or weak shows (89, 67). Raising OOP risks isolating vs better or folding out worse, while folding squanders equity late (hand 142/150) with deep stacks (154BB) and short Flash (32BB). Calling cheaply controls pot, realizes equity, induces bluffs on heart/spade rivers, and preserves stack for bubble/ICM pressure on short stacks. Post-river OOP but SPR low (~0.5 after call), ace-high often good. Optimal to take free cards preflop paid off here.

I have nothing (3♦8♥) — no pair, no draw. I led the turn as a small probe (100 into the ~200 pot) and Elon called, which looks like at least some showdown value (a 5, a 9/7, or a medium pair/slow-play). The river (7♦) didn’t improve me and actually helps some of his calling hands. To get enough fold equity here I’d have to risk a large portion of a relatively small pot; with deep stacks that’s unnecessary and thinly bluffing an opponent who just called the turn is low EV. I’ll check and let him show aggression — fold to a meaningful bet, but if he checks back I take down the pot cheaply.

River board 5h4s5c9h7d completes some straights (e.g., 36o, 68o, 89o) and two-pair combos (57s), but As2c blocks strong Ax (AA, A5) and provides ace-high showdown value against Minni's SB limp-check-flop-turn-bet-check range, which caps at trips (55, A5, 45), medium pairs (66-JJ QQ?), or missed draws (JTs, QJs, 68, hearts). Her small turn bet after checking paired flop likely thin value or semi-bluff, checking river weakness or trap. Ace-high beats 40-60% of her checking range (worse Ax, broadways, air) late (hand 142/150) with deep stacks (153BB) and short Flash (31BB); thin value bet ~50-60% pot extracts from J-high/T-high/pairs she checks behind, denies equity to draws, maximizes EV without overcommitting (SPR <1 post-call). Checking risks free showdown but misses value from callers; bet balances bluffing missed nuts (sets full? unlikely check river). History shows Minni calls light OOP (e.g., Hand 50,131). Bet 250 ~60% pot optimal.

I have 3♦8♥ and missed the entire board (5♥ 4♠ 5♣ 9♥ 7♦). I checked the river planning to fold to meaningful aggression — Elon’s 250 into a 650 pot (900 total) requires ~28% equity to call (250/900 ≈ 27.8%). I have no pair, no blocker to his value hands and no realistic river hero-call equity. His line (check flop, call my small turn probe, now bet the river) is consistent with showable value hands — 5x, 9x, occasional 7x or medium pairs — and is unlikely to be bluff-heavy enough to justify calling. With a deep stack and plenty of good spots left, preserving chips is the +EV play. I’ll fold rather than make a thin, marginal call.

Folded to me on the button with Qc8d. Pot is 150 and it’s 100 to call (BB=100); my stack 21,100 (≈211bb), SB Elon 15,700, BB Flash 3,050 (≈30bb). Q8o is marginal but in position with both blinds to act it’s a reasonable steal attempt. A minimum raise to 200 is too small and invites cheap defense; a 3x open to 300 gives more fold equity against both the short-stacked big blind and the SB, and matches common sizing seen at this table. Plan: raise to 300 to try to pick up the blinds or play postflop in position. If Elon 3-bets big I’ll fold Q8o; if Flash shoves I’ll re-evaluate by pot odds and his shove tendencies.

Hand #143 pre-flop, SB with 7s6h offsuit, a marginal connector at best but weak overall. Minni raises to 300 (3x) from Button, standard raise size. Facing a call of 250 into 450 pot for ~8.3:1 odds, but 76o plays poorly OOP vs a button range which is wide but strong enough. Flash short at 3050 (30BB) but doesn't change this; no squeeze value with this hand. Late tournament (143/150), preserve deep stack (157BB) for better spots. Standard fold.

We are near the end of the tournament (Hand 143 of 150) and I am the short stack with 3050 chips. Minni, the chip leader, has opened from the button to 300. I am in the big blind holding 7h 5s. This is a very weak hand to defend, even at this stage. Calling would commit a significant portion of my remaining stack while being out of position against a dominant leader. With only a few hands left, it is better to preserve my stack for a more favorable spot to push or double up. I will fold.

3-handed on the button in an unopened pot (likely SB folded), Q9o is well within the standard opening range for button in short-handed play (around 60-70% of hands). Excellent position to realize equity, steal blinds (150 chips for 100 risk effectively if raised), and build stack late in tournament (hand 144/150). Limp weak, fold too tight. Standard raise size 3x BB to 300 given history of similar sizes. Flash short at 30BB may shove wide but we have blockers and position; Minni deep but we play postflop well.

I am holding Ten of Clubs and Eight of Clubs in the small blind with approximately 30 big blinds. The button (Elon) has opened for a 3x raise.
