T8s is a strong speculative hand that plays well post-flop, and I am getting decent pot odds (250 to call into a pot of 450). While I will be out of position, the hand is worth a call to see a flop, especially as we approach the end of the tournament and I need to look for opportunities to increase my stack without over-committing.

Three-handed, facing a 3bb button open to 300 with the small blind cold-calling. I have A♥5♣ in the big blind. This hand is marginal multiway and often dominated by other A‑x hands; I’m out of position to the original raiser postflop, so I don’t want to bloat the pot OOP with a weak kicker. A small reraise (to the min 500) won’t fold the button reliably and can get me 3‑bet or committed in a spot I don’t like. Calling 200 more (300 total committed) keeps the pot controlled, gives me the Ace blocker and reasonable pot odds (200 into 900 ≈ 22% required equity) to see a flop. Plan: see the flop; proceed aggressively only if I pair the Ace with a safe board or pick up strong equity (straights/flushes). Fold to heavy action on dry boards where I miss.

I have T8 clubs and the flop is 4d Jh 3d. I have completely missed this flop, possessing only ten-high. With two opponents in the pot, including the pre-flop raiser (Elon), and being out of position, checking is the most prudent play. I cannot justify a bet here as a bluff or for value.

Check. I missed this dry J‑high flop with A♥5♣ — I only have ace‑high plus a 4‑out gutshot to a 2 (~16% to river) and no flush/backdoor equity. I’m out of position to the preflop raiser and the small blind has already checked, so a c‑bet here would bloat a pot OOP with a weak kicker and get called or raised by many better hands. Pot control is the priority: check to keep the pot small, realize equity, and only continue if I pick up the Ace/2 or face a small probe bet I can call. Fold to significant aggression.

Preflop raiser on the button with position on both opponents. Flop is 4d Jh 3d: low, uncoordinated but with diamond flush draw and possible wheel straight draws (A2, 25, 52). Q9o misses completely (queen-high, no pair/draw/gutshot), but as PFR, c-bet for thin value (blocks QJ, KQ), fold equity (both checked, Flash short ~27BB may fold light, Minni deep but passive check), and range advantage/denial of equity. Pot 900, bet ~2/3 pot (600) balances vs draws/pairs; induces shoves from worse we can call light due to position/ICM (hand 144/150, preserve stack but exploit). Checking risks giving free cards to draws improving or strong hands trapping.

I am in the small blind with Ten-high (Tc 8c) on a board of 4d Jh 3d. I have no pair and no draws. Elon, the pre-flop aggressor, has followed up with a 600-chip bet into a 900-chip pot (two-thirds pot). With only 2750 chips remaining in my stack, calling here would commit over 20% of my stack with zero current equity. Since I am out of position and Minni is still to act behind me, the most disciplined play is to fold and preserve my remaining chips for the final six hands of the tournament.

Elon (button) bets 600 into a 1500 pot after both checked, and Flash folded — it’s heads-up and I’m out of position. I have A♥5♣: essentially Ace‑high with only a four‑out wheel to a 2 and no diamond backdoor. Calling 600 to win a ~2100 pot requires ≈22% equity; my realistic equity versus a typical button c‑bet/continuation range (Jx, broadway overcards, Ax, medium pairs) is well below that. A raise as a bluff is unappealing OOP with little fold equity and poor equity if called. With a healthy stack and a marginal hand on a dry board, the correct play is to preserve chips — fold.

I am on the button with Jack-Trey offsuit (J3o) and a stack of 2750 chips (27.5 BB). In a three-handed game, the button is a strong position, but J3o is an extremely weak hand. Both opponents have much larger stacks and have shown significant aggression throughout the tournament. Given that there are only 5 hands remaining, I should wait for a better hand to commit my chips rather than attempting to steal with a sub-optimal range, especially against deep stacks who can easily call or re-raise.

I’m in the small blind with T♥6♠ (marginal, speculative hand) and the button folded, so it’s just me vs the big blind (Elon). Pot is 150 and it costs me 50 more to complete to 100. With this stack depth and being out of position, a raise here would be thin and could be met with 3-bets from an opponent who’s shown aggression in prior hands. Folding would concede my posted 50 for free; calling is cheap and allows me to realize equity and see a flop where I can continue with top pair, good draws, or fold to significant action. Plan: call the 50, play the flop cautiously (check/raise or barrel only with reasonable equity).

BB facing a SB limp after button fold. Qs4s is a speculative suited hand with position postflop. Checking realizes equity for cheap, perfect pot odds (already invested 100 into 200 pot). Raising risks bloating pot OOP with marginal hand; postflop playability is better heads-up where we control action. Near tournament end (145/150), preserve stack, no need to bluff-raise wide vs limper.

Out of position vs one opponent (Elon). I hold T♥6♠ on A♦ 8♦ 2♥ — no pair, no diamond, only 6 clean outs (three tens + three sixes). Two-card equity to hit by the river is ~24%; turn-only is ~12.8%. Pot is 200 and it’s checked to me. With no real equity and a board that heavily favors hands containing an Ace or a diamond draw, I don’t want to bloat the pot as the preflop caller out of position. Checking preserves stack and lets me see another card for free; if Elon fires a small probe I can reassess but will fold to large aggression since my hand is easily dominated when we pair. Plan: check and give up to significant bet, consider calling a very small probe only when implied odds justify it.

Flop misses Qs4s completely: no pair, no strong draws (backdoor spade at best), queen-high OOP in heads-up pot. Minni's SB limp-check range is wide and weak, but betting risks raising from better hands or floats while bloating pot OOP. Pot odds excellent (free turn card in 200 pot after posting BB). Deep stacks late tournament; check preserves chips, realizes thin equity/multiway potential if Flash enters later streets, allows turn control. Avoid bluffing marginal spot vs passive limper-checker.

I have T♥6♠ — the turn 6♣ gives me bottom pair. Pot is 200 and Elon checked flop and turn (passive line). This hand is easily dominated by any Ace, any 8/2, or a made two‑pair/set; there was also a two‑card diamond draw on the flop. Out of position with a marginal one‑pair hand, I don’t want to bloat the pot or turn this into a bluff‑catching mess. Checking controls the pot, preserves stack, and lets me see the river for free. Plan: check to the river; call small river probes (roughly ≤1/3 pot) as he may be barreling missed draws, fold to pot‑sized or larger bets unless I improve to two‑pair/trips; if he checks behind and the river is a blank I will often show and win the small pot.

Heads-up pot OOP vs SB limper who has checked flop and turn. Qs4s has complete air: queen-high no pair, no draws (backdoor spade flush at best, no straight potential on 8d-2h-Ad-6c board). Minni's limp-check range is wide/capped (weak Ax, low pairs, draws that missed), unlikely to have monsters after double check. Betting bloats pot with no showdown value or equity realization, risks raise/call OOP late tournament (hand 145/150, stacks 16k+ effective). Checking preserves stack, takes free river card to realize thin equity/implied odds if board pairs/improves backdoors, or showdown if Minni weak. Optimal passive line consistent with prior streets; no value, marginal bluff spot vs passive opponent.

Check. I have a marginal one-pair (6s) on A♦ 8♦ 2♥ 6♣ J♣ and the pot is only 200 while both players have checked every street. Elon’s passive line makes his range either a slow‑played Ace/8/J or missed draws/air. Betting for thin value risks folding out worse hands (Kx/Qx/Tx) and will usually get called by hands that already beat me (Ax/Jx/8x) — so it's high variance and low expected value. Best line is pot control: check to take it to showdown, call any small river probe (~≤1/3 pot ≈ 60–70) but fold to large bets. If he checks back I win the small pot or show down safely.

River heads-up OOP vs SB limper-checker on 8d-2h-Ad-6c-Jc board. Qs4s is queen-high air with no pair/draw completed; showdown value is thin but present against wide limp-check range (missed Ax, low pairs like 55/77, backdoor draws, pure air). Minni's triple check caps her range heavily (unlikely strong hands like sets/Ax pairs check down), but she could have stubborn weak holdings we beat. Betting risks folding out better hands we dominate (e.g., weak Aces, 9x for gutshot) or worse air while facing calls/raises from better (AJ, AT, sets unlikely after checks). Pot 200 into 16k+ stacks late tournament (145/150, effective deep); bluff sizing minimal EV as opponent passive but pot-committed minimally. Checking realizes equity at showdown, preserves stack for final 5 hands where ICM/position matter more with short-stacked Flash. Consistent passive line from prior streets maximizes thin equity without risk.

I’m on the button with K♣4♠ — a weak, easily dominated hand, but excellent position. Pot is 150 and it’s 100 to call. With SB (Elon) deep (~161bb) and the BB (Flash) short (~26bb), I want to use position and fold equity to avoid playing K4o multiway. I’ll open to 300 (≈3x the BB): that’s a standard button steal size that pressures the short blind and gives me the initiative postflop. Plan: take the pot down preflop a good percentage of the time; if Elon (SB) 3-bets big I’ll fold this holding (it performs poorly vs a strong 3-bet range). If Flash (short BB) shoves, it’s a borderline decision — his shove range will be wide, but K4o is still a marginal call; I’ll be inclined to fold to a committed shove unless I have a specific read that he’s jamming extremely wide. Overall raise to 300 to maximize fold equity and keep pots heads-up or versus the short stack where I can make a clean decision.

Preflop in SB with J3s facing a button raise to 300 (to call 250 into 450 pot). J3s is a weak suited hand with poor equity against a wide button raising range. While SB defends wide, this hand plays poorly postflop, especially OOP, and doesn't warrant a 3-bet given the short BB stack behind who might shove. Late in tournament (hand 146/150), preserve stack and wait for better spots. Fold is -EV for call or raise.

I am in the Big Blind with 7s 5s, a suited gapper. The button (Minni) has raised to 300, requiring 200 more from me to call. My stack is very short (2650) compared to Minni's massive stack. While 75s is a playable hand in many contexts, I am at a severe chip disadvantage with only 26.5 big blinds left. Defending the blind here is marginal, and given the tournament's late stage (Hand 146 of 150), I need to preserve my stack for a more premium spot or a high-equity shove rather than bleeding chips in a multi-way or out-of-position pot against a dominant chip leader.

Preflop on the button with 33, a speculative pocket pair but strong enough in position against blinds. Blinds are 50/100, pot 150, to call 100. Folding is too tight; limping is weak from button. Standard play is to raise for value/isolation/steal, especially with Flash short-stacked in SB (2600) who may shove wide but 33 has good equity. Minni deep in BB but I have position. Raise to 3x BB (300 total) is standard sizing to build pot while denying equity. Late in tournament (147/150), maximize stack by playing profitable spots.

I am holding King-Jack offsuit in the Small Blind. In a three-handed game, this is a very strong hand. Elon, on the button, has been opening frequently. I have approximately 26 big blinds, which gives me some room to play, but I need to be careful. I will call the raise to 300 to see a flop. While 3-betting is an option to take control of the hand, calling allows me to keep the pot manageable and see how the board develops, especially since I will be out of position for the rest of the hand.

Facing a button raise to 300 with the small blind calling, I'm in the big blind with J♠3♦ — a weak, easily dominated hand. Calling 200 into a 700 pot requires ~22% equity to break even, and J3o’s multiway equity against BTN opening and SB calling is well below that. A raise here would be a pure bluff with poor blockers and would either isolate the short SB or get called by the BTN, both undesirable. With a healthy 21,100 stack and only three players left, preserve chips for better spots — fold.

I have hit top pair with a strong King kicker on a relatively dry board of Jack-Four-Five. Since Elon was the pre-flop aggressor from the button, it is standard to check to him to see if he follows up with a continuation bet. Given the strength of my hand and my remaining stack size of about 20 big blinds, I am prepared to commit chips here, but checking first allows Elon to bluff or bet with a wider range of hands that I currently beat.

Heads-up on the flop vs Flash in the SB who called my button raise OOP. Board is Jc4s5d, rainbow, with possible wheel draws (A23,36) and gutshots, but my pocket 33 blocks some straight draws (e.g., 36) and provides decent equity (~40-50% vs his range). As preflop raiser, my range is perceived as strong (AJ+, TT+, suited connectors), hitting top pair/overpairs/draws often. Flash's check indicates weakness; his range includes Ax suited, broadway, mid pairs, suited connectors that missed. Continuation bet charges draws, denies equity to floats/improvers, extracts value from worse pairs (22,66,77?). Pot 700, bet ~50% (350) balances value/protection without overbetting vs short stack (23BB). Deep effective stacks favor betting; checking invites free cards or traps bluffs later. Late tournament, exploit position and initiative to build stack.

I have top pair with a King kicker on a relatively dry board. In a 3-handed game, King-Jack is a very strong hand on this texture. Elon has been aggressive as the button raiser, and this continuation bet is expected from his range. With an SPR (Stack-to-Pot Ratio) of approximately 2, I am essentially committed to this hand. However, I will choose to call rather than raise at this stage to keep Elon's bluffs in his range and see how he reacts to the turn. If I raise now, I likely only get action from hands that beat me (like Ace-Jack or sets), while calling allows him to continue betting with air or weaker pairs.

I have top pair with a King kicker on a relatively dry board of Jack-Four-Five-Seven. My current stack is 2000 and the pot is 1400, giving me an SPR of about 1.4. I checked the flop and called Elon's continuation bet. On this turn, I will check again. This strategy serves two purposes: it allows Elon to continue bluffing with hands like overcards or straight draws, and it keeps the pot manageable if I am behind (though unlikely given the action). If Elon bets, I intend to call or potentially go all-in depending on the sizing, as my hand is too strong to fold in a three-handed game.

Heads-up on turn with pocket 33 against Flash who has checked twice OOP after calling preflop raise and flop c-bet. Board Jc4s5d7s has completed gutshot straights (36), added spade flush draw (two spades), and possible pairs/overpairs/draws in villain's range. My underpair blocks some straight combos (3x) and has some showdown value against bluffs/missed draws, but is behind most of villain's continuing range (Jx, 45/47/57, Ax suited, overpairs). Pot 1400, villain effective 2000 (~20BB short), late in tournament (147/150). Betting risks stacking off behind with thin equity (~30% vs polarized range), low value from worse hands. Checking controls pot, denies villain cheap river, induces bluffs from missed draws (spades, 68, A3), realizes equity in position. Preflop c-bet took initiative; now pot control optimal to maximize stack preservation/growth by winning at showdown or bluff-catching.

I have top pair with a King kicker (KJ) on a board of J-4-5-7-T. In a three-handed game, this is a very strong hand. After calling Elon's continuation bet on the flop, the turn went check-check, which often suggests that Elon might be pot-controlling a medium-strength hand or giving up on a bluff. By checking the river, I am providing Elon with the opportunity to bluff with his missed overcards (like AQ or AK) or to value bet a weaker hand that he might have checked on the turn. Since my stack is relatively short and we are nearing the end of the tournament, I want to ensure I reach showdown with this hand, as it is likely the winner, while also giving my opponent a chance to put more chips in the pot with a wider, weaker range.

Heads-up on the river vs Flash (2000 chips effective, ~14BB) after he checks OOP for the third time this hand. Board Jc4s5d7sTs is draw-heavy: spade flush possible (three spades), gutshot straights completed (e.g., 36 straight), pairs/overpairs/Tx/Jx/7x beat my 33 underpair, which has showdown value vs Ax high, missed draws (spades like KsQ s, gutshots like 26/68), and worse pairs (22). Preflop I raised button (perceived strong range AJ+/TT+), he called wide OOP; flop c-bet polarized my range, his call caps him somewhat at Jx/draws/mid pairs; mutual turn check showed caution. His river check suggests weakness (missed draw, Ax/Jx weak kicker, midpair), unlikely monster (would bet for value/protection). Pot 1400, my deep stack (15500) vs his short: betting risks him hero-calling better or folding (low EV), while checking induces bluffs (none pending but realizes equity), wins at showdown frequently (~45-55% vs his capped range per equity calcs vs calling range). Late tournament (147/150), prioritize stack preservation/maximization: pot control optimal, avoid variance with marginal hand, exploit position to showdown profitably. Check-behind maximizes EV.

I am on the button with T9o in a 3-handed game. With only three hands left in the tournament and a stack of 34 big blinds, I need to look for opportunities to accumulate chips without taking unnecessary risks. Opening for a standard raise of 3x the big blind is a common and aggressive move from the button to pick up the blinds or play a pot in position against the blinds.

Facing a button open to 300 from the small blind with 5♦3♦. This is a very weak, low-suited connector with poor playability OOP. Calling 250 to see a flop leaves me out of position against the raiser and the big blind and I’ll be dominated by most of the opener’s calling/raising range — poor equity and bad implied odds. A light 3-bet is possible as a bluff, but Flash has shown frequent aggression and a 3-bet here risks a call or 4-bet out of position with a hand that has little postflop potential. With a healthy stack I’d much prefer to wait for a better spot. Fold and preserve chips for higher EV opportunities.

Ks6d is a weak offsuit king-high hand with a poor kicker. Facing a button raise from Flash, who often raises wide in late position, my hand is not strong enough to call out of position in the big blind. Pot odds are favorable (200 to call into 450), but the hand has poor playability post-flop, low equity against Flash's wide range, and I'm OOP. With only 2 hands left after this and a deep stack (154BB), folding preserves chips for premium spots. No value in raising or shoving with this hand.

On the button with J♠6♠ in a three-handed pot I want to take the initiative and use position. Pot is 150 and 100 to call — J6s is speculative but has good playability postflop, so a standard 3xBB open to 300 gives fold equity and keeps the pot manageable. This sizing pressures Flash (BB, 3.45k) and often isolates heads-up vs the SB; if I face a large 3-bet or shove from Elon I will fold and preserve my big stack for the final hand. If called I'll play aggressively in position, c-betting or floating as the board dictates.

Ac7o is a marginal hand from the small blind facing a button raise from Minni, who has been aggressive from late position throughout the history. The hand has blocking value with the Ac but a weak kicker, making it vulnerable to domination post-flop (e.g., A8+, AK, AQ). Out of position against two opponents, including short-stacked Flash in the BB who might squeeze or call wide. Pot odds are decent (250 to call into 450, ~26% equity needed), but equity realization OOP with this hand is poor due to difficult post-flop play. With deep stacks (153BB effective vs Minni) and only 2 hands left, preserve stack for premium spots or ante-steal opportunities rather than speculating OOP. Folding maximizes EV long-term.

With Q9o in the Big Blind and only two hands remaining in the tournament, I am getting good pot odds (2.25:1) to call Minni's button raise. My stack is relatively short compared to the others, but Q9o is a standard hand to defend against a wide button opening range. I will call to see a flop and look for opportunities to increase my stack before the final hand.

On this King-high flop of 4h-Kd-3h, my Queen-high hand (Qc 9d) has completely missed. I am out of position against Minni, who has a massive chip lead. Leading out into the pre-flop aggressor on a board that favors their range (containing Kings and Broadway cards) is not a winning strategy. I will check to see how Minni responds; if she bets, I will likely fold to preserve my remaining 3250 chips for the final hand of the tournament.

I missed this flop with J♠6♠ — K♦4♥3♥ is a dry, low board and I have very little equity (only 6 clean outs to pair by river, ~24%). Importantly I have no flush draw: with two spades in my hand and zero spades on the flop it’s impossible to make a five-card spade flush by the river (would need three spades on board and only two cards remain). Flash is short-stacked (≈3.25k) and checked; a c-bet would often take the pot down, but it also invites a shove that I don’t want to hero-call with a marginal hand. I have position and a big chip lead, so I’ll control the pot and realize equity — check and re-evaluate the turn. If Flash bets a small amount I’ll consider a call to keep the pot manageable; I’ll fold to large shoves unless I improve.

On the turn card (Ten of Hearts), the board now shows three hearts and a King. I have completely missed this board with Queen-high and no heart in my hand. Minni's check-back on the flop suggests she might have a marginal hand or is giving up, but the Ten of Hearts is a dangerous card that completes potential flushes. With only two hands left in the tournament and a stack that needs protecting to stay competitive, I will check again. I am not looking to bluff into the chip leader on a board that could have easily improved her range, and I have no equity to continue if faced with a bet.

I have J♠6♠ on K♦4♥3♥T♥ — I completely missed: no pair, no heart, only weak backdoor equity. Flash is short (~3.25k) and has checked twice, so a c-bet here would often take it down but also invites a shove I don’t want to face with a marginal hand. Priority is pot control and preserving my large stack heading into the final hand. I check to keep the pot small, induce bluffs, and re-evaluate the river (I’ll call a small bet to keep things manageable but fold to a large shove unless I improve).

On the river with the board showing 4h-Kd-3h-Th-5d, I have only Queen-high. Minni checked back on both the flop and the turn, which suggests she likely doesn't have a King or a flush. However, she could easily have a small pair or Ace-high, both of which beat my Queen-high. With only one hand remaining in the tournament after this, I need to preserve my stack of 3250. Bluffing into the chip leader, who can easily afford to call a small bet with marginal hands, is too risky. I will check and hope for a check-back, fully expecting to lose to most of Minni's range at showdown, but I will fold if faced with any bet.

I missed this river completely (Js6s on 4h Kd 3h Th 5d): no pair and no heart flush. Flash is the short stack (≈3.25k) and checked every street, so his range is likely medium-strength pairs (Kx, Tx, small pairs like 5s/4s/3s), slow-played two-pair is possible but unlikely from a short, passive caller — he also could be air. Pot is only 650 and my stack is large (20.75k), so my priority is pot control and minimizing variance heading into the final hand. Betting to bluff risks a shove or a call of a short stack and forces difficult hero calls with a marginal hand. I check to keep the pot small, induce any thin bluffs, and avoid committing chips to a marginal spot; if Flash now bets a small amount I’ll consider a call, but I’ll fold to a large shove.

3-handed on the button with 68hh, a strong suited connector with excellent playability for straights, flushes, and two-pair potential. Deep stacks relative to pot (my 153BB, Minni deep, Flash ~38BB). Pot 150, to call 100 offers great odds (~1.5:1 direct, massive implied), but raising from the button is standard to take initiative, build pot in position, or steal blinds. Raising small to 300 (3x BB) pressures short-stacked Flash while keeping pot manageable vs Minni. Last hand of tournament, but position and hand strength favor aggression to maximize stack EV.

On the final hand of the tournament, the primary objective is to preserve or maximize the existing stack. Jack-Deuce suited is a very weak hand to defend from the small blind against a standard button raise, even in a three-handed game. Being out of position for the rest of the hand against Elon, who has been active and has a significant stack advantage, makes it unlikely that playing this hand will result in a net gain. Folding is the most disciplined way to ensure I finish with my current stack of 3,800 chips rather than risking them on a marginal high-variance spot.

Heads-up vs Elon’s button open to 300. I hold pocket 5s and both stacks are deep (I have 20,650), so this is a classic set‑mining spot. Calling the extra 200 (300 total into the 450 pot) keeps the pot manageable out of position, preserves disguise, and gives strong implied odds when I hit a set. A small 3‑bet would force clearer decisions out of position and a larger 3‑bet is unnecessary with a marginal hand; folding surrenders too much. I’ll call and see a flop.

I flopped a set (5c5s on Qs 3d 5h) heads-up vs Elon who opened wide from the button. This is a pure value‑building spot — my hand is near the nuts on this runout and I want to extract calls from Qx, weaker pairs, Ax/Kx floats and any curious overcards. The board has almost no dangerous draws, so I don't need a tiny blocker bet; I size to ~2/3 pot to get value while building a pot with deep stacks. If Elon raises big I’ll re‑evaluate (likely call/jam based on size), but against a call I plan to continue extracting on later streets. Betting now also keeps initiative and prices worse hands into mistakes.

Heads-up vs Minni on a Qs-3d-5h flop with 6h8h from the button in position. Board is fairly dry with no flush draw complete and disconnected low cards. My hand has two gutshot straight draws (4 for 3-4-5-6; 7 for 5-6-7-8, 8 outs ~16% if runner-runner clean), backdoor flush draw, and overcards for live potential. Pot 1100, facing 450 bet (~41% pot), offering ~2.4:1 direct odds becoming 3.4:1 after. Minni called my button raise OOP pre-flop then donk-led, which could be a strong hand like sets/pairs with draw blocker, or polarized with air trying to deny equity. However, history shows Minni aggressive post-flop (frequent bets/raises in pots), but I have position to realize equity cheaply. Folding weak, raising risks bloating pot OOP with marginal equity (~25-35% vs her range: beats bluffs, some weak Qx, draws; loses to Qx+, sets). Calling optimal: pot odds good, deep stacks (mine 150BB, hers deeper) for implied on turns that improve (20+ outs factoring runners), control pot size, see cheap turn. Last hand, but EV-maximizes stack by playing position/draws profitably over folding or spew-raising.

I have a disguised set (5♣5♠) on Q♠‑3♦‑5♥ and I led the flop for value (450) and was called. The A♣ on the turn helps parts of Elon’s range (A3, other A‑x), but my set still crushes most hands that would call the flop (Qx, medium pairs, some Ax that float). With both stacks deep and the pot only 1,550, I want to keep building and extract value rather than give a free river. A bet around two‑thirds pot keeps worse A‑hands and Qx/pocket pairs in the calling range while still protecting against the very small number of impossible/rare full‑house/quads lines. If Elon check‑raises big I will re‑evaluate (set plays very strongly — likely call or jam depending on size); if he calls I’ll re‑assess river for further value. Betting ~2/3 pot is the best way to maximize value now.

Heads-up vs Minni on turn (Qs3d5hAc), holding 68hh from button in position. Hand has two gutshot straight draws (4 for 3456, 7 for 5678; 8 outs ~16%), backdoor heart flush draw, and 6/8 overcards somewhat live. Pot 2550 facing 1000 bet (~40% pot), pot odds 2.55:1 direct (need ~28% equity); implied odds strong with deep stacks (my 14600, her 19000) and position into river (last hand). Minni's line: called OOP pre vs BTN raise (wide), flop donk half-pot (polarized: value like Qx/set/TPDK or bluffs/draws), turn donk again after Ac (blocks AA/AK, could be Qx value, weaker pairs/draws betting for pot control/protection, or continued bluff). History shows Minni hyper-aggressive postflop (frequent bets/raises, value-heavy but bluffs into checkers). My flop call was correct for equity/playability; folding now weak (abandoning ~30% equity cheaply). Raising polarizes too thin (marginal hand, Ac may strengthen her); better to call, control pot, realize equity on river (beats bluffs/A-high/weak Qx, draws ~35-45% vs polarized range). Stack commitment tiny (1000/14600 ~7%), EV+ to call maximizing tournament stack.

I have a disguised set of 5s on Q♠–3♦–5♥–A♣–T♦. I led the flop (450) and continued the turn (1000) and Elon called both — his line is wide from a button open and is most likely Qx, AQ/Ax floaters, KQ/Kx and some medium pairs. The river T doesn’t complete any big flushes and only a narrow Broadway straight (KJ) — very few hands legitimately beat my set (pocket Aces or pocket Queens), so this is largely a pure value spot. I want to extract from two‑pair/top‑pair hands and worse pocket pairs while keeping those hands in, so I’ll size for value rather than check to showdown. Betting ≈2/3 pot keeps worse A/Q hands and Qx/pocket pairs comfortable calling but isn’t so small that I give away too much. Bet 2,400 into 3,550. If Elon check‑raises big or jams I’ll strongly re‑evaluate (small raises I’ll mostly call; a polarized huge shove could indicate AA/QQ and will force a decision based on sizing and reads).

River decision heads-up vs Minni OOP holding missed draw 6h8h (pure air: no pair, no straight, no flush on Qs3d5hAcTd board; 8-high loses to any pair/set/TK). Her line ultra-aggressive: OOP call pre vs BTN raise (defends wide), flop donk 450 (~1/3 pot polarized), turn donk 1000 (after Ac bricks my draws, ~40% pot), river overbet 2400 into 3550 (~67% pot, total pot post-call 8350). Pot odds require ~29% equity (2400/8350), but 0% vs her value-heavy range (Qx/Ax/sets/TwoPair like A3/A5/Q5; history shows Minni hyperaggro valuebetting thin, rarely triple-barrel bluff OOP into position). Missed all equity from flop/turn calls (gutshots to 4/7, bdfd); position useless at showdown with no value. Stacks deep (me 13600→11200 post-call ok ICM but EV- as bluff-catch never triggers; her 16600). Last hand tournament but maximize stack: folding preserves 13600 vs risking 2400 (~18% stack) for 0EV call. Standard fold avoids spew.
